```java
        List<T> result = new ArrayList<>();
        for (int i = 0; i < times; i++) result.add(item);
        return result;
    }

    // Multiple type parameters
    public static <K, V> Map<V, K> invertMap(Map<K, V> original) {
        Map<V, K> inverted = new HashMap<>();
        original.forEach((k, v) -> inverted.put(v, k));
        return inverted;
    }

    // Bounded type parameter тАФ T must extend Comparable
    public static <T extends Comparable<T>> T max(T a, T b) {
        return a.compareTo(b) >= 0 ? a : b;
    }

    // Return type depends on input type тАФ identity function
    public static <T> T identity(T value) {
        return value;
    }
}

// Type inference тАФ compiler infers T = String
List<String> strings = Utils.repeat("hello", 3);

// Explicit type witness (rarely needed)
List<String> explicit = Utils.<String>repeat("hello", 3);

// Inverted map тАФ K=String, V=Integer inferred
Map<String, Integer> original = Map.of("a", 1, "b", 2);
Map<Integer, String> inverted = Utils.invertMap(original);
```

**Generic methods with multiple bounds:**
```java
// T must extend both Comparable AND Serializable
public static <T extends Comparable<T> & Serializable> T clampedMax(T a, T b) {
    return a.compareTo(b) >= 0 ? a : b;
}
// Syntax: class bound must come first, interfaces after &
// <T extends ClassName & Interface1 & Interface2>
```

**Generic constructor:**
```java
public class Container<T> {
    private T value;

    // Generic constructor тАФ adds its own type param U
    public <U extends T> Container(U value) {
        this.value = value;
    }
}
```

**Key Details:**
- Type inference works left-to-right from argument types тАФ explicit type witness `<Type>method()` is a last resort
- Generic methods are preferred over generic classes when the type relationship is local to one method
- In Java 8+, lambda inference handles most cases where explicit witnesses were previously needed

**Common Pitfalls:**
```java
// Pitfall: type inference failure with ambiguous targets
// Compiler can't always infer from return type alone
// This may require explicit witness:
Collections.<String>emptyList();  // older code
List<String> list = Collections.emptyList(); // fine тАФ inferred from assignment
```



### 3. Type Erasure

**Definition:** The JVM has **no knowledge of generic type parameters at runtime**. The compiler removes all generic type information, replacing type parameters with their bounds (or `Object` if unbounded) and inserting casts where needed.

**What the compiler does:**
1. Replaces `T` with its bound (e.g., `Object` if unbounded, `Comparable` if `T extends Comparable`)
2. Inserts synthetic bridge methods where needed to maintain polymorphism
3. Inserts casts at call sites where the erased return type is used

```java
// Source code
public class Box<T> {
    private T value;
    public T get() { return value; }
    public void set(T value) { this.value = value; }
}

// After erasure (what the JVM sees)
public class Box {
    private Object value;           // T тЖТ Object (unbounded)
    public Object get() { return value; }
    public void set(Object value) { this.value = value; }
}

// Call site тАФ compiler inserts cast automatically
Box<String> box = new Box<>();
box.set("hello");
String s = box.get();    // Compiled as: String s = (String) box.get();
```

**Erasure with bounds:**
```java
// Source
public class SortedBox<T extends Comparable<T>> {
    private T value;
    public T get() { return value; }
    public int compareTo(T other) { return value.compareTo(other); }
}

// After erasure тАФ T replaced with first bound: Comparable
public class SortedBox {
    private Comparable value;
    public Comparable get() { return value; }
    public int compareTo(Comparable other) { return value.compareTo(other); }
}
```

**Bridge methods** тАФ compiler-generated to preserve polymorphism:
```java
// Source
public class StringBox extends Box<String> {
    @Override
    public String get() { return "hello"; }
}

// After erasure тАФ compiler generates a bridge method
public class StringBox extends Box {
    public String get() { return "hello"; }         // overriding method

    // Bridge method тАФ synthetic, generated by compiler
    public Object get() { return this.get(); }       // calls String version
}
```

**Consequences of erasure:**

```java
// CANNOT do at runtime:
new T();                    // cannot instantiate type parameter
new T[10];                  // cannot create generic array
obj instanceof List<String> // cannot check parameterized type
T.class                     // no .class literal for type parameter

// CAN work around with Class<T> token:
public class TypeSafeContainer<T> {
    private final Class<T> type;
    private final T value;

    public TypeSafeContainer(Class<T> type, T value) {
        this.type = type;
        this.value = value;
    }

    public boolean isInstance(Object obj) {
        return type.isInstance(obj); // runtime check via token
    }

    public T cast(Object obj) {
        return type.cast(obj);
    }
}

TypeSafeContainer<String> c = new TypeSafeContainer<>(String.class, "hello");
```

**Heap pollution** тАФ when a variable of a parameterized type holds a value of a different type:
```java
@SafeVarargs  // suppresses unchecked warning тАФ only use when safe
public static <T> List<T> asList(T... elements) {
    return Arrays.asList(elements);
}

// Varargs + generics = heap pollution risk
public static void dangerous(List<String>... lists) {
    Object[] arr = lists;           // allowed тАФ array covariance
    arr[0] = List.of(42);           // heap pollution тАФ List<Integer> in List<String> slot
    String s = lists[0].get(0);     // ClassCastException at runtime
}
```

**Reifiable types** (full type info available at runtime):
- Primitive types: `int`, `double`, etc.
- Non-generic classes: `String`, `Object`
- Raw types: `List`, `Map`
- Unbounded wildcards: `List<?>`, `Map<?,?>`
- Arrays of reifiable types: `String[]`, `List<?>[]`

**Key Details:**
- This is why you get "unchecked cast" warnings тАФ the compiler cannot guarantee type safety after erasure
- `getClass()` on `List<String>` and `List<Integer>` returns the same `Class` object
- Reflection cannot distinguish `List<String>` from `List<Integer>` тАФ unless using `TypeToken` (Guava) or `ParameterizedType` via reflection

**Common Pitfalls:**
```java
// Unchecked cast тАФ compiler warning, potential ClassCastException
Object raw = getFromCache("key");
List<String> list = (List<String>) raw; // unchecked тАФ can't verify at runtime

// Suppressing correctly тАФ only when you have verified correctness
@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
List<String> safe = (List<String>) raw;
```



### 4. Bounded Types

**Upper bound** (`extends`): restricts T to a type or any of its **subtypes**.

```java
// T must be Number or a subtype (Integer, Double, etc.)
public static <T extends Number> double sum(List<T> list) {
    double total = 0;
    for (T item : list) {
        total += item.doubleValue(); // can call Number methods on T
    }
    return total;
}

sum(List.of(1, 2, 3));         // T = Integer
sum(List.of(1.5, 2.5));        // T = Double
// sum(List.of("a", "b"));     // compile error тАФ String not a Number
```

**Multiple bounds:**
```java
// Class bound must come FIRST, interfaces follow with &
public static <T extends Cloneable & Serializable & Comparable<T>>
    T process(T item) { ... }

// Practical example: bounded type for a generic sorter
public static <T extends Comparable<T>> void sort(T[] arr) {
    // Can call compareTo because T is bounded by Comparable<T>
    for (int i = 0; i < arr.length - 1; i++) {
        for (int j = i + 1; j < arr.length; j++) {
            if (arr[i].compareTo(arr[j]) > 0) {
                T temp = arr[i];
                arr[i] = arr[j];
                arr[j] = temp;
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Recursive type bound** тАФ type that refers to itself:
```java
// T extends Comparable<T> тАФ T can be compared to itself
// This is the most common recursive bound
public static <T extends Comparable<T>> T min(List<T> list) {
    return list.stream().min(Comparator.naturalOrder()).orElseThrow();
}

// Builder pattern with recursive bound (self-referential fluent API)
public abstract class Builder<T, B extends Builder<T, B>> {
    protected String name;

    @SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
    public B withName(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        return (B) this;  // returns concrete subtype
    }

    public abstract T build();
}

public class UserBuilder extends Builder<User, UserBuilder> {
    private int age;

    public UserBuilder withAge(int age) {
        this.age = age;
        return this;
    }

    @Override
    public User build() { return new User(name, age); }
}

// Fluent chaining works correctly
User user = new UserBuilder()
    .withName("Alice")  // returns UserBuilder, not Builder
    .withAge(30)
    .build();
```

**Lower bound** тАФ only available on wildcards, not type parameters:
```java
// ILLEGAL тАФ cannot use lower bound on type parameter
// public static <T super Integer> void method(T t) {} // compile error

// Legal тАФ lower bound only on wildcards
public static void addIntegers(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(1);
    list.add(2);
}
```

**Key Details:**
- `extends` in bounds means "extends or implements" тАФ works for both classes and interfaces
- A type parameter with no bound implicitly has `extends Object`
- When multiple bounds are specified, the erased type is the leftmost bound

**Common Pitfalls:**
```java
// Common confusion: <T extends Comparable<T>> vs <T extends Comparable>
// Raw Comparable loses type safety тАФ always use the parameterized form
public static <T extends Comparable<T>> T max(T a, T b) { ... } // correct
public static <T extends Comparable>    T max(T a, T b) { ... } // raw, unsafe
```



### 5. Wildcards

**Wildcards represent an unknown type.** They appear only in **type usage** (variable declarations, parameters, return types), never in class/method definitions as type parameters.

**Three forms:**

```
?              тАФ unbounded wildcard тАФ any type
? extends T    тАФ upper-bounded wildcard тАФ T or any subtype
? super T      тАФ lower-bounded wildcard тАФ T or any supertype
```

**Unbounded wildcard `<?>`:**
```java
// Accept a list of any type тАФ only use Object methods
public static void printAll(List<?> list) {
    for (Object item : list) {  // elements are Object тАФ no type info
        System.out.println(item);
    }
}

printAll(List.of("a", "b"));  // List<String>
printAll(List.of(1, 2, 3));   // List<Integer>

// Cannot add to List<?> (except null) тАФ type is unknown
public static void badAdd(List<?> list) {
    list.add("hello"); // compile error тАФ cannot add to unknown type
    list.add(null);    // null is always allowed
}

// Useful for instanceof (wildcards are reifiable)
if (obj instanceof List<?>) { ... } // OK
```

**Upper-bounded wildcard `<? extends T>`:**
```java
// Read from a collection of T or any subtype
public static double sumNumbers(List<? extends Number> list) {
    double sum = 0;
    for (Number n : list) {     // element type is at least Number
        sum += n.doubleValue(); // can call Number methods
    }
    return sum;
}

sumNumbers(List.of(1, 2, 3));      // List<Integer> тАФ Integer extends Number
sumNumbers(List.of(1.5, 2.5));     // List<Double>  тАФ Double extends Number

// Producer тАФ you can read T from it, but NOT write T to it
public static void noWrite(List<? extends Number> list) {
    Number n = list.get(0);      // OK тАФ produces Number
    list.add(1);                 // compile error тАФ which Number subtype is it?
    list.add(null);              // null only
}
```

**Lower-bounded wildcard `<? super T>`:**
```java
// Write T (or subtypes) into a collection of T or any supertype
public static void addNumbers(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(1);      // OK тАФ Integer is-a Integer
    list.add(2);      // OK
    // list.add(1.5); // compile error тАФ 1.5 is Double, not Integer or subtype
}

addNumbers(new ArrayList<Integer>());   // Integer super Integer
addNumbers(new ArrayList<Number>());    // Number  super Integer
addNumbers(new ArrayList<Object>());    // Object  super Integer

// Consumer тАФ you can write T to it, but reading gives only Object
public static void noSpecificRead(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(42);        // OK тАФ writing Integer
    Integer i = list.get(0);  // compile error тАФ could be Number or Object
    Object  o = list.get(0);  // OK тАФ worst case it's Object
}
```

**Wildcard capture:**
```java
// Helper method pattern to capture wildcard type
public static void swap(List<?> list, int i, int j) {
    swapHelper(list, i, j); // delegate to capture helper
}

// Captures the unknown type as T
private static <T> void swapHelper(List<T> list, int i, int j) {
    T temp = list.get(i);
    list.set(i, list.get(j));
    list.set(j, temp);
}
```

**Key Details:**
- Wildcards are primarily for **API flexibility** тАФ method parameters that accept a wider range of inputs
- Wildcards in return types force callers to also use wildcards тАФ avoid as return types
- `List<?>` and `List<Object>` are very different: `List<Object>` only accepts `List<Object>`, while `List<?>` accepts any `List<X>`

**Common Pitfalls:**
```java
// Pitfall: confusing List<Object> with List<?>
void process(List<Object> list) {} // only accepts List<Object> тАФ too restrictive
void process(List<?> list)       {} // accepts any List<X> тАФ more flexible

// Pitfall: nesting wildcards incorrectly
List<List<?>> listOfLists;          // each inner list can be a different type
List<? extends List<?>> wildList;   // outer is also wildcard
```



### 6. Covariance and Contravariance

These describe how type relationships between parameterized types relate to type relationships between their arguments.

**Invariance** (Java default for generics):
```
Dog extends Animal
BUT: List<Dog> is NOT a subtype of List<Animal>
```

Why? Because it would break type safety:
```java
List<Dog> dogs = new ArrayList<>();
List<Animal> animals = dogs;       // if this were allowed...
animals.add(new Cat());            // Cat is an Animal тАФ valid add
Dog d = dogs.get(0);               // ClassCastException тАФ it's a Cat!

// Java prevents this at compile time тАФ List<Dog> is NOT a List<Animal>
```

**Covariance** тАФ `List<? extends Animal>` is a supertype of `List<Dog>`:
- You can **read** Animals from it (produces)
- You **cannot write** to it (consumer side broken тАФ which subtype is it?)

```java
List<Dog> dogs = new ArrayList<>(List.of(new Dog()));
List<? extends Animal> covariant = dogs;  // OK тАФ covariant assignment

Animal a = covariant.get(0);  // OK тАФ produces Animal
covariant.add(new Dog());     // compile error тАФ cannot write
covariant.add(new Animal());  // compile error тАФ cannot write
covariant.add(null);          // null only

// Covariant read example
public static void makeNoise(List<? extends Animal> animals) {
    for (Animal a : animals) {  // each element is at least an Animal
        a.makeSound();
    }
}
makeNoise(new ArrayList<Dog>());  // List<Dog> accepted
makeNoise(new ArrayList<Cat>());  // List<Cat> accepted
```

**Contravariance** тАФ `List<? super Dog>` is a supertype of `List<Animal>`:
- You can **write** Dogs to it (consumes)
- Reading gives only `Object` (producer side broken тАФ could be anything above Dog)

```java
List<Animal> animals = new ArrayList<>();
List<? super Dog> contravariant = animals;  // OK тАФ contravariant assignment

contravariant.add(new Dog());      // OK тАФ Dog is a Dog
contravariant.add(new Labrador()); // OK тАФ Labrador is a Dog (subtype)
// contravariant.add(new Cat());   // compile error тАФ Cat is not a Dog

Object o = contravariant.get(0);   // OK тАФ worst case it's Object
Animal a = contravariant.get(0);   // compile error тАФ might not be Animal
```

**Arrays are covariant (and unsound тАФ a design flaw):**
```java
Dog[] dogs = new Dog[3];
Animal[] animals = dogs;       // ALLOWED тАФ arrays are covariant
animals[0] = new Cat();        // compiles тАФ Cat is an Animal
// Runtime: ArrayStoreException тАФ JVM checks at runtime

// This is why arrays + generics mix poorly
// List<Dog>[] is illegal for this reason
```

**Visual summary:**
```
Invariant:     List<Dog>              is NOT a  List<Animal>
Covariant:     List<? extends Dog>    IS    a   List<? extends Animal>
Contravariant: List<? super Animal>   IS    a   List<? super Dog>

Array (covariant, unsound):
               Dog[]                  IS    an  Animal[]   (but ArrayStoreException risk)
```



### 7. PECS Principle

**PECS = Producer Extends, Consumer Super**

Coined by Joshua Bloch in *Effective Java*. It is the definitive rule for choosing between `? extends T` and `? super T`.

```
If a parameterized type T is a PRODUCER of T  тЖТ use <? extends T>
If a parameterized type T is a CONSUMER of T  тЖТ use <? super T>
If it's BOTH                                  тЖТ use <T> (invariant)
If it's NEITHER                               тЖТ use <?>
```

**Mnemonic:** Think from the **method's perspective** тАФ does it **produce** (read) elements, or **consume** (write/accept) elements?

**PECS in action тАФ `Collections.copy()`:**
```java
// Source PRODUCES T тАФ use extends
// Destination CONSUMES T тАФ use super
public static <T> void copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src) {
    for (int i = 0; i < src.size(); i++) {
        T element = src.get(i);   // read from producer (extends)
        dest.set(i, element);     // write to consumer (super)
    }
}

List<Integer> src  = List.of(1, 2, 3);
List<Number>  dest = new ArrayList<>(List.of(0.0, 0.0, 0.0));
Collections.copy(dest, src);  // works тАФ Number super Integer, Integer extends Integer
```

**PECS in action тАФ custom example:**
```java
public class Stack<T> {

    private List<T> elements = new ArrayList<>();

    // CONSUMER тАФ pushAll accepts items to WRITE onto stack
    // Use ? super T тАФ accept T or any supertype
    public void pushAll(Iterable<? extends T> src) {  // src PRODUCES T
        for (T item : src) {
            elements.add(item);
        }
    }

    // PRODUCER тАФ popAll reads items FROM stack into dest
    // Use ? super T тАФ dest CONSUMES T
    public void popAll(Collection<? super T> dest) {   // dest CONSUMES T
        while (!elements.isEmpty()) {
            dest.add(elements.remove(elements.size() - 1));
        }
    }
}

Stack<Number> stack = new Stack<>();

// pushAll: List<Integer> passed тАФ Integer extends Number тЬУ
stack.pushAll(List.of(1, 2, 3));

// popAll: List<Object> passed тАФ Object super Number тЬУ
List<Object> result = new ArrayList<>();
stack.popAll(result);
```

**Without PECS тАФ overly restrictive:**
```java
// Without PECS тАФ too restrictive
public void pushAll(Iterable<T> src) {}        // only accepts Iterable<Number>
public void popAll(Collection<T> dest) {}      // only accepts Collection<Number>

Stack<Number> stack = new Stack<>();
stack.pushAll(List.of(1, 2, 3));  // compile error! List<Integer> тЙа List<Number>

// With PECS тАФ flexible
stack.pushAll(List.of(1, 2, 3));  // List<Integer> тАФ Integer extends Number тЬУ
stack.pushAll(List.of(1.5, 2.5)); // List<Double>  тАФ Double extends Number  тЬУ

List<Object> objects = new ArrayList<>();
stack.popAll(objects);             // Object super Number тЬУ
```

**PECS and `Comparator`/`Comparable`:**
```java
// Comparator CONSUMES T (to compare) тАФ use ? super T
public static <T extends Comparable<? super T>> T max(List<? extends T> list) {
    //                              ^^^^^^^^^^
    //              T or supertypes of T can define compareTo
    //              e.g., if Dog extends Animal, and Animal implements Comparable<Animal>
    //              then Dog can use Animal's compareTo
    return list.stream().max(Comparator.naturalOrder()).orElseThrow();
}

// This is why Collections.sort signature is:
public static <T extends Comparable<? super T>> void sort(List<T> list) {}
//             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
//             T can be compared by itself OR a supertype's compareTo
```

**PECS cheat sheet:**
```
METHOD READS  elements of type T from parameter тЖТ parameter is Producer тЖТ ? extends T
METHOD WRITES elements of type T to  parameter тЖТ parameter is Consumer тЖТ ? super T
METHOD does both                               тЖТ use T (no wildcard)
METHOD only uses Object methods                тЖТ use ?

Return types: AVOID wildcards in return types
              тАФ forces callers to use wildcards too
              тАФ use T or concrete type instead
```



## Interview Questions

**Q1: What is type erasure and what are its practical consequences?**

Type erasure is the process by which the Java compiler removes all generic type information, replacing type parameters with their bounds (or `Object` if unbounded) and inserting casts at call sites. At runtime, `List<String>` and `List<Integer>` are identical тАФ both are just `List`. Practical consequences: (1) Cannot use `instanceof` with parameterized types тАФ `obj instanceof List<String>` is a compile error. (2) Cannot create generic arrays тАФ `new T[10]` is illegal. (3) Cannot instantiate type parameters тАФ `new T()` is illegal. (4) Unchecked cast warnings when casting to generic types. (5) Overloading based solely on type parameter is impossible тАФ `void method(List<String>)` and `void method(List<Integer>)` have the same erasure. Work arounds include passing `Class<T>` tokens, using reflection with `ParameterizedType`, or library solutions like Guava's `TypeToken`.



**Q2: Explain why `List<Dog>` is not a `List<Animal>` even though `Dog extends Animal`. How do wildcards solve this?**

Generics are **invariant** by design to ensure type safety. If `List<Dog>` were assignable to `List<Animal>`, you could add a `Cat` to what is actually a `List<Dog>`, causing a `ClassCastException` when reading. This is unlike arrays, which are covariant but compensate with a runtime `ArrayStoreException` тАФ a weaker guarantee. Wildcards solve this by restricting operations: `List<? extends Animal>` (covariant) allows reading Animals but not writing; `List<? super Dog>` (contravariant) allows writing Dogs but reading gives only `Object`. The type system encodes the constraint: you lose the unsafe operation in exchange for the flexible subtype relationship.



**Q3: Explain PECS with a real example.**

PECS (Producer Extends, Consumer Super) tells you which wildcard to use based on the role of the parameter. A **producer** provides elements you read out тАФ use `? extends T`. A **consumer** accepts elements you write in тАФ use `? super T`. Classic example: `Collections.copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src)`. The `src` list *produces* T values (you read from it) so `? extends T`. The `dest` list *consumes* T values (you write to it) so `? super T`. This signature means you can copy `List<Integer>` into `List<Number>` тАФ something impossible without PECS.



**Q4: What is the difference between `<T extends Comparable<T>>` and `<T extends Comparable<? super T>>`?**

`<T extends Comparable<T>>` requires that T directly implements `Comparable<T>`. `<T extends Comparable<? super T>>` is more flexible: T (or any of its supertypes) can implement `Comparable`. The practical difference: if `Animal implements Comparable<Animal>` and `Dog extends Animal` without implementing `Comparable`, then `Dog` satisfies `<T extends Comparable<? super T>>` (because `Animal` is a supertype that implements `Comparable<Animal>`) but does NOT satisfy `<T extends Comparable<T>>`. This is why `Collections.sort` uses the `? super T` form тАФ it handles inherited `Comparable` implementations correctly.



**Q5: When would you use an unbounded wildcard `?` vs a bounded wildcard vs a type parameter?**

Use **`<?>`** when you only need `Object`-level operations and don't need to relate types across parameters тАФ e.g., `printList(List<?> list)`. Use **`? extends T`** or **`? super T`** (wildcards) when you want flexibility in a *single* position with no type relationship to other positions тАФ typically method parameters. Use a **type parameter `<T>`** when: (1) The type appears in multiple positions that must be consistent тАФ return type + parameter type. (2) You need to name the type to use it тАФ `T get()` vs `? get()` (illegal). (3) You need to enforce a relationship between two parameters тАФ `<T> void copy(List<T> src, List<T> dest)`. Rule of thumb: use wildcards in parameters for flexibility, use type parameters when you need to refer to the type in multiple places.



**Q6: Why can't you create a generic array like `new T[10]` or `new List<String>[10]`?**

Because of type erasure combined with array covariance. Arrays are covariant (`Dog[]` is an `Animal[]`) and store their element type at runtime for `ArrayStoreException` checks. Generic arrays can't work safely: (1) `new T[10]` тАФ after erasure, this becomes `new Object[10]`, but the runtime type is wrong тАФ the array won't enforce that only `T` is stored. (2) `new List<String>[10]` тАФ after erasure it becomes `new List[10]`, and through array covariance it could be assigned to `Object[]`, allowing a `List<Integer>` to be stored, causing a later `ClassCastException`. Alternatives: use `List<T>` instead of `T[]`, use `(T[]) new Object[n]` with `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")`, or pass a `Class<T>` and use `Array.newInstance(type, n)`.



**Q7: What is a bounded wildcard and when would you use it over a type parameter?**

A bounded wildcard (`? extends T` or `? super T`) expresses a constraint on an *unknown* type. Use wildcards in method parameters when you don't need to refer to the type elsewhere. Use bounded type parameters `<T extends SomeClass>` when you need to use the type by name (return it, use it in multiple parameters, create instances). Example: `<T extends Number> T sum(List<T> list)` returns `T` тАФ must be a type parameter. But `double sum(List<? extends Number> list)` where the return is always `double` тАФ wildcard is fine and more flexible (accepts `List<Integer>`, `List<Double>`, etc.).



**Q8: What is heap pollution and how does it relate to generics?**

Heap pollution occurs when a variable of a parameterized type holds a value of a different type тАФ violating the invariant that the JVM's type system is supposed to ensure. This happens when you mix raw types with generic types, or with generic varargs. The `@SafeVarargs` annotation suppresses the warning when you can guarantee no heap pollution occurs. The classic trap: `void dangerous(List<String>... lists)` тАФ inside the method, `lists` is a `List[]` (erased), and you can store a `List<Integer>` into it via array covariance, corrupting the type assumptions of callers.



**Q9: How do you retain generic type information at runtime (workarounds for erasure)?**

Three main approaches: (1) **Class token** тАФ pass `Class<T>` as a constructor/method argument: `new TypedContainer<>(String.class)`. Allows `isInstance()` and `cast()` checks. (2) **Reflection with `ParameterizedType`** тАФ inspect generic superclass type at runtime: `((ParameterizedType) getClass().getGenericSuperclass()).getActualTypeArguments()[0]`. Used by frameworks like Jackson for deserialization. (3) **Anonymous subclass trick / TypeToken** (Guava) тАФ create an anonymous subclass to "capture" the type: `new TypeToken<List<String>>(){}` preserves `List<String>` in the bytecode as the generic supertype of the anonymous class, making it inspectable via reflection. This is the basis of Jackson's `TypeReference` and Guava's `TypeToken`.



**Q10: Explain the difference between raw types and unbounded wildcards.**

A raw type (`List`) turns off all generic type checking тАФ you can add anything, and you get `Object` back with unchecked cast warnings. It exists solely for backward compatibility with pre-Java 5 code. An unbounded wildcard (`List<?>`) retains type safety тАФ you cannot add non-null elements because the element type is unknown. Reading gives `Object`. The compiler still tracks that this is a parameterized list and enforces that you can't add typed elements. Prefer `List<?>` over raw `List` тАФ raw types lose all compile-time checking. The only valid use of raw types is for class literals (`List.class`) and `instanceof` checks (`obj instanceof List`).



**Q11: Can you overload methods that differ only in their type parameters? Why or why not?**

No. Due to erasure, `void method(List<String> list)` and `void method(List<Integer> list)` erase to the same signature `void method(List list)` тАФ a compile error. This is why you see `process(List<String>)` and `processInts(List<Integer>)` in older APIs. Similarly, you cannot overload based on a generic return type. The workaround is to use different method names or to pass a `Class<T>` discriminator parameter. Note: you CAN overload with different numbers of type parameters or when the erasure produces different signatures, e.g., `void method(T t)` and `void method(List<T> list)` erase to `void method(Object)` vs `void method(List)` тАФ those are different.



**Q12: What does `<T extends Comparable<T>>` mean and why is it a recursive bound?**

It means "T is a type that can be compared to other instances of itself." The recursion is that T appears both as the type being bounded and as the argument to `Comparable`. This is necessary to guarantee the `compareTo` method takes a `T` argument тАФ not just any `Object`. Without the recursive bound, calling `a.compareTo(b)` where `b` is `T` would be unsafe. The pattern appears everywhere natural ordering is needed: `TreeMap`, `Collections.sort`, custom generic sorting utilities. The extended form `<T extends Comparable<? super T>>` is even more flexible тАФ T or any of its supertypes can define the `compareTo` method.



**Q13: How does the generic type system interact with inheritance and method overriding?**

When a subclass overrides a generic method from a superclass with a different type parameter, the compiler generates a **bridge method** to maintain polymorphism after erasure. For example, if `class StringBox extends Box<String>` overrides `set(String value)`, after erasure `Box` has `set(Object value)` тАФ the compiler generates a bridge `set(Object v) { set((String) v); }` in `StringBox` so that the overriding works correctly through polymorphic dispatch. Bridge methods are synthetic and invisible in source code but visible in bytecode via `javap`. They can cause confusion with reflection when you see unexpected methods. They're also why you may see multiple `compareTo` methods in decompiled output.



**Q14: What is wildcard capture and when do you need it?**

Wildcard capture is when you need to perform an operation on `List<?>` that requires knowing the type тАФ but the type is unknown. You solve it by delegating to a private generic helper method that "captures" the wildcard into a named type parameter. Example: swapping elements in a `List<?>` requires a temp variable of the list's element type. You can't declare `? temp` but you can write `private <T> void swapHelper(List<T> list, int i, int j)` and call it from the public `swap(List<?> list, int i, int j)` method. The compiler performs the capture when passing `List<?>` to `List<T>` тАФ it infers a specific (unknown) type for `T`.



**Q15: How do frameworks like Jackson handle generic type deserialization given erasure?**

Jackson (and similar frameworks) use the **anonymous subclass trick**. The `TypeReference<T>` class is designed to be subclassed: `new TypeReference<List<String>>(){}`. The anonymous subclass's generic supertype `TypeReference<List<String>>` is preserved in bytecode as a `ParameterizedType`. Jackson retrieves it via `getClass().getGenericSuperclass()`, then calls `getActualTypeArguments()` to get the `List<String>` type, which it uses to drive deserialization. Guava's `TypeToken` formalizes this pattern. This is one of the few places where runtime generic type info survives тАФ because the type is embedded in the class hierarchy, not in a local variable.



## Advanced / Senior-Level Considerations

### Generic Design Patterns

**Type-safe heterogeneous container (Bloch's pattern):**
```java
// A map that can hold values of different types, each type-safe
public class TypesafeMap {
    private Map<Class<?>, Object> map = new HashMap<>();

    public <T> void put(Class<T> type, T value) {
        map.put(type, type.cast(value)); // cast verifies type at put time
    }

    public <T> T get(Class<T> type) {
        return type.cast(map.get(type)); // safe cast
    }
}

TypesafeMap tm = new TypesafeMap();
tm.put(String.class, "hello");
tm.put(Integer.class, 42);
String s = tm.get(String.class);   // no cast needed, type-safe
Integer i = tm.get(Integer.class);
```

**Generic singleton factory:**
```java
// Single instance works for all types because of erasure
// but appears typed to callers
private static final UnaryOperator<Object> IDENTITY = x -> x;

@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
public static <T> UnaryOperator<T> identityFunction() {
    return (UnaryOperator<T>) IDENTITY;
}

UnaryOperator<String> strId = identityFunction();
UnaryOperator<Integer> intId = identityFunction(); // same object, different type view
```

### Generics and Performance
- Autoboxing cost: `List<Integer>` requires boxing/unboxing on every access тАФ for performance-critical numeric collections, consider `int[]`, or Eclipse Collections' `IntList`, or `trove4j`
- Type erasure means no code bloat (unlike C++ templates which generate code per type) тАФ one `.class` file serves all parameterized uses
- Virtual dispatch through generic interfaces adds no overhead beyond normal polymorphism

### Generics and Reflection
```java
// Inspect generic type at runtime via reflection
Field field = MyClass.class.getDeclaredField("myList");
ParameterizedType pt = (ParameterizedType) field.getGenericType();
Type elementType = pt.getActualTypeArguments()[0]; // String for List<String>

// Works for fields, method return types, method parameter types
// Does NOT work for local variables тАФ erased entirely
```

### Integration with Modern Java
- **Streams API**: Heavily generic тАФ `Stream<T>`, `Collector<T,A,R>`, `Function<T,R>`, etc. тАФ PECS appears throughout
- **Optional<T>**: Monadic container тАФ use `map()`, `flatMap()` to chain generic transformations
- **CompletableFuture<T>**: Generic async pipelines тАФ `thenApply(Function<T,R>)` uses bounded wildcards internally
- **Records** (Java 16+): Can be generic тАФ `record Pair<A,B>(A first, B second) {}`
- **Sealed classes** (Java 17+): Combine with generics for algebraic data types тАФ `sealed interface Result<T> permits Success<T>, Failure<T>`



## Summary / Cheat Sheet

```
DECLARATION SYNTAX:
тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
class Box<T>                      generic class
class Pair<A, B>                  multiple type params
<T> T method(T arg)               generic method
<T extends Comparable<T>> T max() bounded type param
<T extends C & I1 & I2>           multiple bounds (class first)

WILDCARD RULES (PECS):
тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
? extends T    Producer  тЖТ READ from it  (covariant)
? super T      Consumer  тЖТ WRITE to it  (contravariant)
?              Unknown   тЖТ Object ops only
No wildcard    Both      тЖТ Read AND write, type must match

WHAT YOU CAN'T DO (erasure):
тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
new T()                 no generic instantiation
new T[n]                no generic array
instanceof List<String> no parameterized instanceof
T.class                 no class literal
overload on T only      same erasure = same signature

TYPE RELATIONSHIPS:
тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
List<Dog>              NOT a  List<Animal>   (invariant)
List<? extends Dog>    IS  a  List<? extends Animal>
List<? super Animal>   IS  a  List<? super Dog>
Dog[]                  IS  an Animal[]  (covariant, unsound)

WHEN TO USE WHAT:
тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
Return a typed value            тЖТ <T> T method()
Accept readable collection      тЖТ List<? extends T>
Accept writable collection      тЖТ List<? super T>
Accept any typed collection     тЖТ List<?>
Relate two params by type       тЖТ <T> void method(List<T> a, List<T> b)
Runtime type operations         тЖТ Class<T> token
Preserve full generic type      тЖТ TypeToken / TypeReference

KEY RULES FROM EFFECTIVE JAVA:
тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
Item 26: Don't use raw types
Item 28: Prefer lists to arrays (arrays covariant + reified = unsafe mix)
Item 29: Favor generic types
Item 30: Favor generic methods
Item 31: Use bounded wildcards to increase API flexibility (PECS)
Item 32: Combine generics and varargs judiciously (@SafeVarargs)
Item 33: Consider typesafe heterogeneous containers
```
# Java Exception Handling тАФ Senior Developer Study Notes



## Topic Overview

Exception handling is Java's mechanism for dealing with **abnormal conditions** that disrupt normal program flow. It separates error-handling logic from business logic, enables structured error propagation up the call stack, and provides a contract between method callers and implementors about what can go wrong.

**Why it matters:**
- Distinguishes **recoverable conditions** (checked) from **programming errors** (unchecked)
- Enforces error handling at compile time for critical failure paths
- Enables clean resource management via `finally` and `try-with-resources`
- Defines the **failure contract** of an API тАФ as important as the success contract
- Misuse is one of the most common sources of bugs, resource leaks, and silent failures in production systems

**Real-world applications:**
- Database connection failures тЖТ checked `SQLException` тЖТ retry logic or fallback
- Invalid user input тЖТ custom `ValidationException` тЖТ HTTP 400 response
- Null pointer in business logic тЖТ `NullPointerException` тЖТ signals a programming bug
- File not found тЖТ `FileNotFoundException` тЖТ user-facing error message
- Service timeout тЖТ custom `ServiceUnavailableException` тЖТ circuit breaker triggers
- Transaction rollback on any `RuntimeException` in Spring `@Transactional`



## Core Concepts

- **Throwable** is the root of the entire exception hierarchy тАФ only `Throwable` instances can be thrown
- **Checked exceptions** are part of a method's contract тАФ compiler enforces handling or declaration
- **Unchecked exceptions** (`RuntimeException` + `Error`) тАФ compiler does not require handling
- **Stack unwinding** тАФ when an exception is thrown, the JVM walks up the call stack looking for a matching `catch`
- **Exception propagation** тАФ uncaught exceptions bubble up until caught or they reach the JVM and terminate the thread
- **Finally** always executes (with one narrow exception) тАФ guarantees resource cleanup
- **Exception chaining** тАФ wrapping a lower-level cause in a higher-level abstraction preserves root cause



## Exception Hierarchy

```
java.lang.Throwable
тФЬтФАтФА java.lang.Error                        (unchecked тАФ JVM-level, unrecoverable)
тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА OutOfMemoryError
тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА StackOverflowError
тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА AssertionError
тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА VirtualMachineError
тФВ     тФФтФАтФА LinkageError
тФВ           тФФтФАтФА NoClassDefFoundError
тФВ
тФФтФАтФА java.lang.Exception                    (root of all exceptions)
      тФЬтФАтФА IOException                      (checked)
      тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА FileNotFoundException
      тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА SocketException
      тФВ     тФФтФАтФА EOFException
      тФЬтФАтФА SQLException                     (checked)
      тФЬтФАтФА CloneNotSupportedException       (checked)
      тФЬтФАтФА InterruptedException             (checked)
      тФЬтФАтФА ReflectiveOperationException     (checked)
      тФВ
      тФФтФАтФА RuntimeException                (unchecked тАФ programming errors)
            тФЬтФАтФА NullPointerException
            тФЬтФАтФА IllegalArgumentException
            тФВ     тФФтФАтФА NumberFormatException
            тФЬтФАтФА IllegalStateException
            тФЬтФАтФА IndexOutOfBoundsException
            тФВ     тФЬтФАтФА ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            тФВ     тФФтФАтФА StringIndexOutOfBoundsException
            тФЬтФАтФА ClassCastException
            тФЬтФАтФА ArithmeticException
            тФЬтФАтФА UnsupportedOperationException
            тФЬтФАтФА ConcurrentModificationException
            тФФтФАтФА NoSuchElementException
```

**Key structural rules:**
- `Error` тАФ JVM-level, catastrophic, should never be caught (except in frameworks/JVM tools)
- `Exception` тАФ base for all application exceptions
- `RuntimeException` тАФ unchecked; signals programmer mistakes
- Everything except `RuntimeException` and `Error` subtrees тЖТ checked



## Subtopics Breakdown



### 1. Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions

**Checked exceptions:**
- Subclass of `Exception` but NOT `RuntimeException`
- Compiler enforces: must either `catch` or declare with `throws`
- Represent **anticipated, recoverable** conditions outside the program's control
- Examples: `IOException`, `SQLException`, `InterruptedException`

**Unchecked exceptions:**
- Subclass of `RuntimeException` or `Error`
- No compiler enforcement тАФ can propagate freely
- Represent **programming errors** or **unrecoverable** JVM conditions
- Examples: `NullPointerException`, `IllegalArgumentException`, `StackOverflowError`

```java
// Checked тАФ compiler forces you to handle or declare
public String readFile(String path) throws IOException {       // must declare
    return Files.readString(Path.of(path));                    // throws checked IOException
}

public void caller() {
    try {
        String content = readFile("data.txt");
    } catch (IOException e) {                                  // must catch or re-throw
        System.err.println("File read failed: " + e.getMessage());
    }
}

// Unchecked тАФ no compiler enforcement
public int divide(int a, int b) {                              // no throws needed
    if (b == 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Divisor cannot be zero");
    return a / b;
}

public void uncheckedCaller() {
    int result = divide(10, 0);    // compiles тАФ no forced handling
    // ArithmeticException would propagate if b=0 naturally
}
```

**The checked vs unchecked debate тАФ senior perspective:**

Joshua Bloch's rule from *Effective Java*:
- Use **checked** when the caller can reasonably be expected to recover
- Use **unchecked** when the exception indicates a programming error, or recovery is not realistic

Modern trend (Spring, most frameworks) leans toward **unchecked**:
- Checked exceptions break functional interfaces (`Function<T,R>` cannot throw checked exceptions)
- They create verbose boilerplate that often just gets swallowed
- They couple API layers тАФ a low-level `SQLException` leaks into high-level business code

```java
// Checked exceptions break lambdas
List<String> paths = List.of("a.txt", "b.txt");

// DOES NOT COMPILE тАФ Files.readString throws checked IOException
paths.stream()
     .map(Files::readString)     // compile error
     .collect(Collectors.toList());

// Workaround тАФ wrap in unchecked
paths.stream()
     .map(path -> {
         try { return Files.readString(Path.of(path)); }
         catch (IOException e) { throw new UncheckedIOException(e); } // wrap
     })
     .collect(Collectors.toList());

// Or use a utility
@FunctionalInterface
interface CheckedFunction<T, R> {
    R apply(T t) throws Exception;

    static <T, R> Function<T, R> wrap(CheckedFunction<T, R> fn) {
        return t -> {
            try { return fn.apply(t); }
            catch (Exception e) { throw new RuntimeException(e); }
        };
    }
}

paths.stream()
     .map(CheckedFunction.wrap(p -> Files.readString(Path.of(p))))
     .collect(Collectors.toList());
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Catching `Exception` or `Throwable` to silence checked exceptions тАФ hides real problems
- Catching `Error` subtypes тАФ almost always wrong
- Declaring `throws Exception` on everything тАФ defeats the purpose of checked exceptions



### 2. try-catch-finally

**Basic structure:**
```java
try {
    // guarded code тАФ may throw
} catch (SpecificException e) {
    // handle specific exception
} catch (AnotherException e) {
    // handle another type
} finally {
    // ALWAYS executes тАФ with or without exception, with or without return
}
```

**Multi-catch** (Java 7+) тАФ handle multiple unrelated exceptions the same way:
```java
try {
    riskyOperation();
} catch (IOException | SQLException e) {    // e is implicitly final here
    log.error("Operation failed", e);
    throw new ServiceException("Data access failed", e);
}
```

**Exception hierarchy in catch blocks тАФ order matters:**
```java
// WRONG тАФ unreachable catch block
try {
    Files.readString(Path.of("file.txt"));
} catch (Exception e) {          // catches everything
    // ...
} catch (IOException e) {        // compile error тАФ unreachable, Exception is broader
    // ...
}

// CORRECT тАФ specific to general
try {
    Files.readString(Path.of("file.txt"));
} catch (FileNotFoundException e) {    // most specific first
    // file doesn't exist
} catch (IOException e) {             // broader
    // other I/O error
}
```

**Finally behavior тАФ the guarantee:**
```java
public int finallyDemo() {
    try {
        System.out.println("try");
        return 1;                  // finally still runs before method returns
    } catch (Exception e) {
        System.out.println("catch");
        return 2;
    } finally {
        System.out.println("finally"); // ALWAYS runs
        // return 3;  // BAD тАФ overrides try/catch return, swallows exceptions
    }
}
// Output: "try" then "finally", returns 1

// Finally does NOT run in two cases:
// 1. System.exit() is called
// 2. JVM crashes / power failure (OS-level)
// 3. Infinite loop or deadlock in try block тАФ never exits
```

**Return in finally тАФ dangerous antipattern:**
```java
public int dangerous() {
    try {
        throw new RuntimeException("real problem");
    } finally {
        return 42;    // SWALLOWS the exception тАФ exception is lost entirely
    }
}

// Returns 42 silently тАФ the RuntimeException is gone forever
```

**try-with-resources** (Java 7+) тАФ automatic resource management:
```java
// Old way тАФ error-prone
Connection conn = null;
try {
    conn = dataSource.getConnection();
    // use conn
} catch (SQLException e) {
    // handle
} finally {
    if (conn != null) {
        try { conn.close(); }           // close can ALSO throw!
        catch (SQLException ignored) {} // easy to suppress incorrectly
    }
}

// try-with-resources тАФ correct and clean
try (Connection conn = dataSource.getConnection();
     PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(SQL)) {   // multiple resources
    ps.executeUpdate();
} catch (SQLException e) {
    throw new DataAccessException("Query failed", e);
}
// conn and ps are BOTH closed in reverse order, even if exception occurs
// close() exceptions are suppressed (accessible via e.getSuppressed())

// Custom AutoCloseable
public class ManagedResource implements AutoCloseable {
    public ManagedResource() { System.out.println("opened"); }

    public void use() { System.out.println("using"); }

    @Override
    public void close() {
        System.out.println("closed");   // called automatically
    }
}

try (ManagedResource r = new ManagedResource()) {
    r.use();
} // prints: opened тЖТ using тЖТ closed
```

**Suppressed exceptions тАФ accessing close() failures:**
```java
try (ManagedResource r = new ManagedResource()) {
    throw new RuntimeException("primary");
    // r.close() throws тАФ that exception is suppressed, not lost
} catch (RuntimeException e) {
    System.out.println("Primary: " + e.getMessage());
    for (Throwable suppressed : e.getSuppressed()) {
        System.out.println("Suppressed: " + suppressed.getMessage());
    }
}
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Empty catch blocks тАФ silently swallows exceptions, extremely dangerous in production
- Catching `Throwable` тАФ catches `Error` subtypes like `OutOfMemoryError`
- `return` in `finally` тАФ swallows exceptions
- Forgetting to close resources without try-with-resources тАФ resource leaks
- Closing resources in wrong order manually (should be reverse open order)



### 3. throw vs throws

**`throw`** тАФ the statement that actually throws an exception instance:
```java
// throw an exception immediately
throw new IllegalArgumentException("Value must be positive: " + value);

// Always creates a new instance (or re-throws existing)
throw new RuntimeException("wrapped", originalException);
```

**`throws`** тАФ the declaration on a method signature that announces possible checked exceptions:
```java
// Method declares it may throw тАФ callers must handle or propagate
public void processFile(String path) throws IOException, ParseException {
    // implementation
}

// Interface can declare throws тАФ implementations must honor it
public interface DataLoader {
    Data load(String source) throws IOException;
}
```

**Re-throwing exceptions:**
```java
try {
    riskyOperation();
} catch (IOException e) {
    log.error("Failed", e);
    throw e;                              // re-throw same exception тАФ stack trace preserved
}

// Wrapping in higher-level exception (exception translation)
try {
    jdbcTemplate.query(SQL, mapper);
} catch (DataAccessException e) {
    throw new OrderRepositoryException("Failed to load orders", e);  // chain cause
}

// Java 7+ тАФ re-throw with more specific type
// Compiler is smart enough to know only IOException can reach this catch
public void smart() throws IOException {
    try {
        throw new FileNotFoundException("file");
    } catch (Exception e) {
        throw e;                          // compiler allows тАФ knows actual type is IOException
    }
}
```

**throws on overriding methods тАФ the covariance rule:**
```java
public class Parent {
    public void method() throws IOException {}
}

public class Child extends Parent {
    // Can throw FEWER or MORE SPECIFIC checked exceptions
    @Override
    public void method() throws FileNotFoundException {}  // OK тАФ more specific

    // @Override
    // public void method() throws Exception {}            // compile error тАФ broader
    // Cannot declare broader checked exceptions than parent
}
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
```java
// Declaring unchecked in throws тАФ legal but pointless/misleading
public void method() throws NullPointerException {}   // adds nothing

// throws clause is not enforced for RuntimeException тАФ misleading to declare
```



### 4. Custom Exceptions

**Design principles:**
- Extend `RuntimeException` for unchecked (preferred in modern Java)
- Extend `Exception` for checked (when recovery is genuinely expected)
- Always provide constructors for: message, cause, both, and neither
- Add domain-specific fields for structured error information
- Never just wrap an exception with no added value

```java
// Minimal correct custom exception
public class InsufficientFundsException extends RuntimeException {

    private final BigDecimal balance;
    private final BigDecimal amount;

    // Constructor: message only
    public InsufficientFundsException(String message) {
        super(message);
        this.balance = null;
        this.amount = null;
    }

    // Constructor: domain fields тАФ most useful for callers
    public InsufficientFundsException(BigDecimal balance, BigDecimal amount) {
        super(String.format(
            "Insufficient funds: balance=%.2f, required=%.2f", balance, amount
        ));
        this.balance = balance;
        this.amount = amount;
    }

    // Constructor: message + cause тАФ for wrapping
    public InsufficientFundsException(String message, Throwable cause) {
        super(message, cause);
        this.balance = null;
        this.amount = null;
    }

    // Constructor: all four тАФ standard boilerplate
    protected InsufficientFundsException(String message, Throwable cause,
                                          boolean enableSuppression,
                                          boolean writableStackTrace) {
        super(message, cause, enableSuppression, writableStackTrace);
        this.balance = null;
        this.amount = null;
    }

    public BigDecimal getBalance() { return balance; }
    public BigDecimal getAmount()  { return amount; }
}

// Usage
if (account.getBalance().compareTo(amount) < 0) {
    throw new InsufficientFundsException(account.getBalance(), amount);
}

// Caller can extract structured data
catch (InsufficientFundsException e) {
    log.warn("Payment declined: balance={}, required={}", e.getBalance(), e.getAmount());
    return PaymentResult.declined("Insufficient funds");
}
```

**Exception hierarchy for a domain:**
```java
// Root domain exception тАФ catch-all for the domain
public class OrderException extends RuntimeException {
    public OrderException(String message)                  { super(message); }
    public OrderException(String message, Throwable cause) { super(message, cause); }
}

// Specific subtypes
public class OrderNotFoundException extends OrderException {
    private final String orderId;
    public OrderNotFoundException(String orderId) {
        super("Order not found: " + orderId);
        this.orderId = orderId;
    }
    public String getOrderId() { return orderId; }
}

public class OrderAlreadyShippedException extends OrderException {
    public OrderAlreadyShippedException(String orderId) {
        super("Cannot modify shipped order: " + orderId);
    }
}

public class InvalidOrderStateException extends OrderException {
    private final OrderStatus current;
    private final OrderStatus required;

    public InvalidOrderStateException(OrderStatus current, OrderStatus required) {
        super(String.format("Invalid state: expected %s but was %s", required, current));
        this.current  = current;
        this.required = required;
    }
    // getters...
}

// Layered catching in a controller
try {
    orderService.ship(orderId);
} catch (OrderNotFoundException e) {
    return ResponseEntity.notFound().build();
} catch (OrderAlreadyShippedException e) {
    return ResponseEntity.status(409).body(e.getMessage());
} catch (OrderException e) {
    return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(e.getMessage()); // catch-all for domain
}
```

**When NOT to create a custom exception:**
- If `IllegalArgumentException`, `IllegalStateException`, or `UnsupportedOperationException` already expresses the intent
- If the exception will only be thrown in one place and caught generically
- If you're just adding a different name with no new information

**Common Pitfalls:**
```java
// Anti-pattern: exception with no added value
public class MyException extends RuntimeException {
    public MyException(String msg) { super(msg); }
}
// Just use IllegalStateException directly

// Anti-pattern: losing the cause
try {
    somethingRisky();
} catch (IOException e) {
    throw new ServiceException("Failed"); // original cause LOST тАФ never do this
}
// Correct:
throw new ServiceException("Failed", e); // chain the cause
```



### 5. Exception Propagation

**Call stack unwinding:**
```
main()
  тФФтФАтФА orderService.placeOrder()
        тФФтФАтФА paymentService.charge()
              тФФтФАтФА bankClient.transfer()
                    тФФтФАтФА throws IOException  тЖР thrown here
```

If `bankClient.transfer()` throws and no method in the chain catches it, the exception unwinds through each frame until caught or the thread terminates.

```java
// Propagation through layers
public class BankClient {
    public void transfer(BigDecimal amount) throws IOException {
        // network call throws
        throw new IOException("Network timeout");
    }
}

public class PaymentService {
    public void charge(Order order) throws IOException {
        bankClient.transfer(order.getTotal());  // propagates up
    }
}

public class OrderService {
    public void placeOrder(Order order) {
        try {
            paymentService.charge(order);
        } catch (IOException e) {
            // Exception translation тАФ convert technical to domain exception
            throw new PaymentFailedException("Payment processing failed", e);
        }
    }
}
```

**Exception translation тАФ the critical pattern:**
```java
// WRONG тАФ leaks implementation detail (SQLException) into domain layer
public Order findOrder(String id) throws SQLException {
    return jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(SQL, orderMapper, id);
}

// CORRECT тАФ translate to domain exception, preserve cause
public Order findOrder(String id) {
    try {
        return jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(SQL, orderMapper, id);
    } catch (EmptyResultDataAccessException e) {
        throw new OrderNotFoundException(id);              // domain exception
    } catch (DataAccessException e) {
        throw new OrderRepositoryException("DB error", e); // cause preserved
    }
}
```

**Exception tunneling тАФ bypassing checked exception restriction:**
```java
// Sometimes you need to throw a checked exception through an interface
// that doesn't declare it (e.g., Runnable, Callable, streams)

// Option 1: Wrap in RuntimeException (most common)
public void run() {
    try {
        doCheckedWork();
    } catch (IOException e) {
        throw new UncheckedIOException(e);   // JDK built-in wrapper for IOException
        // OR: throw new RuntimeException(e);
    }
}

// Option 2: Sneaky throw (advanced тАФ use with extreme caution)
// Exploits type erasure to throw checked as unchecked тАФ bypasses compiler
@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")
public static <T extends Throwable> void sneakyThrow(Throwable t) throws T {
    throw (T) t;
}

// Option 3: Lombok @SneakyThrows (does the above under the hood)
@SneakyThrows(IOException.class)
public void method() {
    Files.readString(Path.of("file.txt")); // IOException thrown without declaration
}
```

**Thread boundaries тАФ exceptions don't cross threads:**
```java
// Exception thrown in a thread does NOT propagate to the spawning thread
Thread t = new Thread(() -> {
    throw new RuntimeException("thread exception");
});
t.setUncaughtExceptionHandler((thread, ex) -> {
    log.error("Thread {} failed: {}", thread.getName(), ex.getMessage());
});
t.start();

// With CompletableFuture тАФ exceptions are captured and re-thrown on get()
CompletableFuture<String> future = CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
    throw new RuntimeException("async failure");
});

try {
    future.get();
} catch (ExecutionException e) {
    Throwable cause = e.getCause();     // unwrap to get original exception
    log.error("Async task failed", cause);
}

// exceptionally тАФ handle inline
future.exceptionally(ex -> {
    log.error("Failed", ex);
    return "fallback";
});
```



### 6. Best Practices

**1. Be specific тАФ catch the most specific exception possible:**
```java
// BAD тАФ too broad, hides bugs
try {
    processOrder(order);
} catch (Exception e) {
    log.error("Error", e);
}

// GOOD тАФ specific, intentional
try {
    processOrder(order);
} catch (OrderNotFoundException e) {
    return notFound(e.getOrderId());
} catch (InsufficientFundsException e) {
    return paymentDeclined(e);
}
```

**2. Never swallow exceptions silently:**
```java
// WORST POSSIBLE CODE тАФ exception disappears without trace
try {
    criticalOperation();
} catch (Exception e) {
    // empty тАФ exception swallowed, never do this
}

// Minimum: log it
} catch (Exception e) {
    log.error("criticalOperation failed", e);  // always pass exception object to logger
}
```

**3. Always preserve the cause chain:**
```java
// BAD тАФ cause lost forever
throw new ServiceException("Failed");

// BAD тАФ cause lost even though caught
catch (IOException e) {
    throw new ServiceException("Failed: " + e.getMessage()); // string only, not cause
}

// GOOD тАФ full chain preserved
throw new ServiceException("Failed", e);
```

**4. Include context in the message:**
```java
// BAD тАФ no context
throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid input");

// GOOD тАФ actionable message with context
throw new IllegalArgumentException(
    String.format("Age must be between 0 and 150, got: %d", age)
);

throw new OrderNotFoundException(
    "Order not found for id=" + orderId + ", userId=" + userId
);
```

**5. Use try-with-resources for everything AutoCloseable:**
```java
// Always тАФ files, connections, streams, HTTP clients
try (var conn = dataSource.getConnection();
     var ps   = conn.prepareStatement(sql)) {
    return ps.executeQuery();
}
```

**6. Don't use exceptions for flow control:**
```java
// BAD тАФ exceptions as control flow (extremely expensive тАФ stack trace construction)
public boolean isValidUser(String id) {
    try {
        userRepository.findById(id);
        return true;
    } catch (UserNotFoundException e) {
        return false;
    }
}

// GOOD тАФ use Optional or explicit check
public boolean isValidUser(String id) {
    return userRepository.existsById(id);
}

// GOOD тАФ Optional
public Optional<User> findUser(String id) {
    return userRepository.findById(id); // returns Optional.empty() if not found
}
```

**7. Exception logging тАФ log once, at the right level:**
```java
// BAD тАФ logged at multiple layers, creates noise
catch (IOException e) {
    log.error("Error in service", e);  // logs here
    throw new ServiceException("Failed", e); // and re-throws, logged again up the chain
}

// GOOD тАФ log at the boundary where it's caught for the last time
// Lower layers: wrap and rethrow without logging
catch (IOException e) {
    throw new ServiceException("Failed", e); // no logging тАФ let it propagate

// At the top (controller/handler): log once
catch (ServiceException e) {
    log.error("Request processing failed for user={}", userId, e); // log once, at boundary
    return serverError(e);
}
```

**8. Null safety тАФ prefer NPE prevention over catching:**
```java
// BAD тАФ NullPointerException is a programming error, not a condition to handle
try {
    return user.getAddress().getCity();
} catch (NullPointerException e) {
    return "Unknown";
}

// GOOD тАФ explicit null checks or Optional
return Optional.ofNullable(user)
               .map(User::getAddress)
               .map(Address::getCity)
               .orElse("Unknown");
```

**9. Document exceptions in Javadoc:**
```java
/**
 * Transfers funds between accounts.
 *
 * @param from source account ID
 * @param to   destination account ID
 * @param amount amount to transfer, must be positive
 * @throws IllegalArgumentException if amount is not positive
 * @throws AccountNotFoundException if either account does not exist
 * @throws InsufficientFundsException if source account has insufficient balance
 */
public void transfer(String from, String to, BigDecimal amount) {
    // ...
}
```



## Interview Questions

**Q1: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions? When do you use each?**

Checked exceptions extend `Exception` (but not `RuntimeException`) and require the compiler to enforce handling тАФ the caller must either catch or declare them with `throws`. Unchecked exceptions extend `RuntimeException` or `Error` and have no compiler enforcement. Use checked when the caller can realistically recover from the condition and it's outside the program's control тАФ file not found, network timeout, SQL failure. Use unchecked for programming errors тАФ invalid arguments, violated contracts, null where not expected. Modern best practice (reflected in Spring and most frameworks) leans toward unchecked because checked exceptions break functional interfaces, create layered leakage, and often get swallowed anyway. The guiding question: "Can the caller reasonably recover from this?" If yes, consider checked. If it's a programming mistake, always unchecked.



**Q2: Explain `finally` тАФ when does it NOT execute?**

`finally` executes after `try` completes (normally or via exception) and after any matching `catch` block. It executes even when there's a `return` in the `try` or `catch` тАФ the JVM saves the return value, runs `finally`, then returns. The three cases where `finally` does NOT execute: (1) `System.exit()` is called тАФ JVM terminates immediately. (2) The JVM itself crashes or is killed at the OS level. (3) The thread is stuck in an infinite loop or deadlock inside `try` тАФ it never exits. Important: a `return` statement in `finally` overrides any return value or pending exception from `try`/`catch` тАФ this is a dangerous antipattern that silently swallows exceptions.



**Q3: What is try-with-resources and how does it handle exceptions from `close()`?**

`try-with-resources` (Java 7+) automatically closes any `AutoCloseable` resources declared in the `try` header when the block exits, whether normally or exceptionally. Resources are closed in reverse declaration order. If the `try` body throws and `close()` also throws, the `close()` exception is **suppressed** тАФ added to the primary exception via `addSuppressed()` and accessible via `e.getSuppressed()`. The primary exception is NOT lost. This is superior to the old manual `finally` approach, where a `close()` exception in `finally` would completely replace and lose the original exception. Multiple resources can be declared, and all are guaranteed to close.



**Q4: What is exception chaining and why is it important?**

Exception chaining (also called exception wrapping or translation) preserves the original cause when converting a lower-level exception to a higher-level one. Every `Throwable` constructor accepts a `cause` parameter. Critically important because: (1) it preserves the full stack trace from root cause to surface, (2) it enables exception translation without losing debugging information, and (3) it maintains layer separation while still allowing post-mortem diagnosis. The anti-pattern is `throw new HighLevelException("Failed")` тАФ the original `IOException` or `SQLException` cause is gone forever. Always: `throw new HighLevelException("Failed", originalException)`. The full chain is printed by `printStackTrace()` and logged by SLF4J when you pass the exception as the last logger argument.



**Q5: What happens when an exception is thrown in a `finally` block?**

If an exception is thrown in `finally`, it replaces any exception that was in flight from the `try` or `catch` block тАФ the original exception is **completely lost**. This is one of Java's dangerous edge cases. Example: if `try` throws `IOException` and `finally` throws `RuntimeException`, the caller sees only the `RuntimeException`. The `IOException` disappears. This is why you should never throw from `finally`, and why try-with-resources is safer тАФ it suppresses `close()` exceptions rather than replacing the primary one.



**Q6: What is the difference between `throw e` and `throw new Exception(e)` when re-throwing?**

`throw e` re-throws the exact same exception instance тАФ the original stack trace is preserved entirely. The caller sees exactly where the exception originally occurred. `throw new Exception(e)` creates a new exception wrapping the original as a cause тАФ this adds a new stack frame at the wrapping point, which is correct when doing exception translation (changing exception type). However, it also loses the original exception type. When you want to re-throw the same type with the same trace, use `throw e`. When translating exceptions across abstraction layers, use `throw new DomainException("message", e)`. Java 7+ also allows smarter re-throws: if you catch `Exception` but only `IOException` can be thrown in the `try`, you can re-throw as `IOException` without wrapping.



**Q7: Can you catch `Error`? Should you?**

Technically yes тАФ `Error` extends `Throwable` so `catch (Error e)` compiles. You should almost never catch `Error`. `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`, and `VirtualMachineError` indicate the JVM is in an unrecoverable state тАФ attempting to handle them is likely to make things worse. Legitimate exceptions: frameworks and application servers sometimes catch `OutOfMemoryError` to log diagnostics and attempt graceful shutdown. Thread pool executors catch `Throwable` to prevent thread death. JUnit catches `AssertionError` (which extends `Error`) to report test failures. If you do catch `Error`, your only reasonable responses are logging and (at most) attempting graceful shutdown тАФ never try to continue normal operation.



**Q8: Why is catching `Exception` or `Throwable` considered a bad practice?**

Catching `Exception` catches all checked and unchecked exceptions тАФ including programming errors like `NullPointerException` and `ClassCastException` that indicate bugs. This hides bugs that should surface and be fixed. Catching `Throwable` also catches `Error` subclasses including `OutOfMemoryError`. The problems: (1) bugs are silently swallowed instead of surfacing as crashes that prompt fixing, (2) impossible to take appropriate action тАФ different exceptions require different responses, (3) code becomes impossible to reason about тАФ any method call could throw anything and it's all handled the same way. Acceptable uses: top-level framework handlers, `UncaughtExceptionHandler`, and monitoring/logging infrastructure тАФ always log the full exception and only for the purpose of last-resort handling or diagnostics.



**Q9: How do exceptions work across thread boundaries?**

Exceptions thrown in one thread do NOT propagate to other threads тАФ each thread has its own call stack. If an exception goes uncaught in a thread, it terminates that thread and invokes the thread's `UncaughtExceptionHandler` (or the default one if not set). With `Future`/`ExecutorService`: exceptions are captured and wrapped in `ExecutionException`, re-thrown when `future.get()` is called on the submitting thread. With `CompletableFuture`: exceptions are captured and can be handled with `exceptionally()`, `handle()`, or `whenComplete()`. With virtual threads (Java 21+): same semantics тАФ exceptions are contained within the virtual thread unless explicitly propagated via `Future` or shared state.



**Q10: What is exception translation and when should you apply it?**

Exception translation is converting a lower-level exception into a higher-level one appropriate to the current abstraction layer. Apply it at every layer boundary: when a `SQLException` from JDBC would leak into the service layer, translate it to a domain exception like `OrderRepositoryException`. Principles: (1) always preserve the cause тАФ `throw new DomainException("msg", cause)`, (2) translate at the boundary, not deep inside тАФ the repository layer translates JDBC exceptions, not the service layer, (3) choose the right level of specificity тАФ not everything needs a unique exception type. Never translate by wrapping with the same exception type and no added information тАФ that adds noise without value.



**Q11: How would you design exception handling for a REST API?**

Use a global exception handler (`@ControllerAdvice` in Spring) to centralize HTTP response mapping. Define a domain exception hierarchy with a base exception. Map exceptions to HTTP status codes in one place. Include structured error responses with consistent fields (code, message, details). Log with appropriate severity тАФ 4xx errors at WARN (client mistakes), 5xx at ERROR (server failures). Never expose stack traces or internal details in responses. Example structure:
```java
@RestControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {
    @ExceptionHandler(EntityNotFoundException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleNotFound(EntityNotFoundException e) {
        return ResponseEntity.status(404).body(new ErrorResponse("NOT_FOUND", e.getMessage()));
    }
    @ExceptionHandler(ValidationException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleValidation(ValidationException e) {
        return ResponseEntity.status(400).body(new ErrorResponse("VALIDATION_ERROR", e.getMessage()));
    }
    @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleGeneral(Exception e) {
        log.error("Unhandled exception", e);
        return ResponseEntity.status(500).body(new ErrorResponse("INTERNAL_ERROR", "An error occurred"));
    }
}
```



**Q12: What is the performance cost of exceptions?**

Creating an exception is expensive primarily because of **stack trace capture** тАФ `fillInStackTrace()` walks the entire call stack to record it, costing tens of microseconds. Throwing and catching itself is relatively cheap. This is why using exceptions for flow control (e.g., catching `NumberFormatException` to detect non-numeric strings) is a performance antipattern in hot paths. For high-frequency paths, prefer explicit checks (`if (str.matches("\\d+"))`) or `Optional`-returning methods. You can override `fillInStackTrace()` and return `this` to create zero-cost exception instances for cases where the stack trace has no value тАФ sometimes done for sentinel exceptions in internal APIs. `new RuntimeException("msg") { @Override public Throwable fillInStackTrace() { return this; } }`.



**Q13: What is the problem with `throws Exception` or `throws Throwable` in method signatures?**

It defeats the entire purpose of checked exceptions. Callers are forced to handle `Exception` тАФ but they can't know what specific exceptions to expect, so they either catch `Exception` broadly (hiding bugs) or declare `throws Exception` themselves, propagating the problem. It conveys zero information about what can actually go wrong. It prevents callers from writing specific recovery logic. It poisons the call chain тАФ every caller must declare `throws Exception`. Better alternatives: declare specific checked exceptions, use unchecked exceptions for things callers can't recover from, or use a domain base exception class so callers can at least catch by domain boundary.



**Q14: Explain the `InterruptedException` handling contract.**

`InterruptedException` is thrown when a thread is interrupted while blocked (in `sleep`, `wait`, `join`, etc.). The critical rule: **never swallow it silently**. When you catch `InterruptedException`, you must either: (1) re-throw it тАФ the cleanest option if your method signature allows, (2) restore the interrupt flag тАФ `Thread.currentThread().interrupt()` тАФ if you can't propagate it (e.g., inside `Runnable.run()`). Swallowing it (`catch (InterruptedException e) {}`) suppresses the interrupt signal, potentially preventing the thread from shutting down properly. This is especially important in thread pool tasks, where the pool uses interrupts for task cancellation and shutdown.



**Q15: What are suppressed exceptions and when do they occur?**

Suppressed exceptions occur in try-with-resources when both the `try` body and the `close()` method throw exceptions. The `close()` exception is added to the primary exception (from the `try` body) via `addSuppressed()` rather than replacing it. The primary exception propagates normally, and suppressed exceptions are accessible via `primaryException.getSuppressed()`. This was introduced in Java 7 specifically to solve the problem with the old manual `finally` pattern, where a `close()` exception in `finally` would completely replace and lose the original exception. You can also manually add suppressed exceptions: `primaryException.addSuppressed(secondaryException)` тАФ useful in custom resource management code that orchestrates multiple closeable resources.



## Advanced / Senior-Level Considerations

### Exception Handling in Reactive / Async Code
```java
// Project Reactor тАФ exceptions in reactive pipelines
Mono<Order> order = orderService.findById(id)
    .onErrorMap(DataAccessException.class,
                e -> new OrderRepositoryException("DB error", e))
    .onErrorReturn(OrderNotFoundException.class, Order.empty())
    .doOnError(e -> log.error("Order fetch failed for id={}", id, e));

// CompletableFuture тАФ structured exception handling
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> orderService.findById(id))
    .thenApply(order -> enrichOrder(order))
    .exceptionally(ex -> {
        Throwable cause = ex instanceof CompletionException ? ex.getCause() : ex;
        if (cause instanceof OrderNotFoundException) return Order.empty();
        throw new CompletionException(cause);
    })
    .handle((result, ex) -> ex == null ? result : fallbackOrder());
```

### Structured Logging of Exceptions
```java
// Pass exception as last argument тАФ SLF4J logs full stack trace
log.error("Order processing failed for orderId={}, userId={}", orderId, userId, e);

// NOT this тАФ loses stack trace
log.error("Order processing failed: " + e.getMessage());

// MDC for correlation
MDC.put("correlationId", request.getCorrelationId());
try {
    processRequest(request);
} catch (Exception e) {
    log.error("Request failed", e);  // correlationId in MDC adds context
    throw e;
} finally {
    MDC.clear();
}
```

### Design Patterns for Exception Handling

**Result type pattern** тАФ avoid exceptions for expected failures:
```java
public sealed interface Result<T> permits Result.Success, Result.Failure {
    record Success<T>(T value) implements Result<T> {}
    record Failure<T>(String code, String message, Throwable cause) implements Result<T> {}

    static <T> Result<T> success(T value)          { return new Success<>(value); }
    static <T> Result<T> failure(String code, String msg) { return new Failure<>(code, msg, null); }

    default boolean isSuccess()    { return this instanceof Success; }
    default T getValue()           { return ((Success<T>) this).value(); }
}

// Usage тАФ no exceptions in normal flow
Result<Order> result = orderService.placeOrder(request);
if (result.isSuccess()) {
    return ResponseEntity.ok(result.getValue());
} else {
    Result.Failure<Order> failure = (Result.Failure<Order>) result;
    return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(failure.message());
}
```

### Integration Considerations
- **Spring `@Transactional`**: rolls back on `RuntimeException` by default; checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback unless `rollbackFor` is specified тАФ a common source of data integrity bugs
- **Spring `@Retryable`**: catches specified exceptions and retries тАФ works best with unchecked
- **Hibernate**: wraps all exceptions in `HibernateException` (unchecked) тАФ important for transaction management
- **Kafka consumers**: uncaught exceptions in `@KafkaListener` affect offset commit behavior
- **gRPC**: exceptions map to `Status` codes тАФ use `StatusRuntimeException`



## Summary / Cheat Sheet

```
HIERARCHY:
```
