```java
                    packet.getData(), 0, packet.getLength(), StandardCharsets.UTF_8
                );
                System.out.println("Got: " + message + " from " + packet.getSocketAddress());
                
                // Echo back to sender
                byte[] response = ("ECHO: " + message).getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
                DatagramPacket reply = new DatagramPacket(
                    response, response.length,
                    packet.getAddress(), packet.getPort()
                );
                socket.send(reply);
            }
        }
    }
}

// UDP Client
public class UdpClient {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        try (DatagramSocket socket = new DatagramSocket()) {
            socket.setSoTimeout(5_000); // 5s wait for response
            
            InetAddress serverAddr = InetAddress.getByName("localhost");
            byte[] data = "Hello UDP!".getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
            
            // Send packet
            DatagramPacket packet = new DatagramPacket(data, data.length, serverAddr, 9090);
            socket.send(packet);
            
            // Receive response
            byte[] buffer = new byte[1024];
            DatagramPacket response = new DatagramPacket(buffer, buffer.length);
            socket.receive(response); // throws SocketTimeoutException if no reply
            
            System.out.println("Response: " + new String(
                response.getData(), 0, response.getLength(), StandardCharsets.UTF_8
            ));
        }
    }
}
```

**UDP Multicast:**
```java
// Send to a group тАФ all members receive (used for service discovery, mDNS)
InetAddress group = InetAddress.getByName("239.0.0.1"); // multicast range: 224.x - 239.x

// Sender
try (MulticastSocket sender = new MulticastSocket()) {
    byte[] data = "Service announcement".getBytes();
    DatagramPacket packet = new DatagramPacket(data, data.length, group, 5555);
    sender.send(packet);
}

// Receiver
try (MulticastSocket receiver = new MulticastSocket(5555)) {
    NetworkInterface ni = NetworkInterface.getByName("eth0");
    receiver.joinGroup(new InetSocketAddress(group, 5555), ni);
    
    byte[] buffer = new byte[1024];
    DatagramPacket packet = new DatagramPacket(buffer, buffer.length);
    receiver.receive(packet); // blocks until multicast arrives
    
    receiver.leaveGroup(new InetSocketAddress(group, 5555), ni);
}
```

**Building reliability on top of UDP (conceptual):**
```java
// Simple stop-and-wait ARQ over UDP
public class ReliableUdpChannel {
    private final DatagramSocket socket;
    private int sequenceNumber = 0;
    
    public void sendReliably(byte[] data, InetSocketAddress dest) throws IOException {
        int retries = 0;
        int maxRetries = 5;
        long timeoutMs = 1000;
        
        // Prepend sequence number
        byte[] packet = prependSeqNum(sequenceNumber, data);
        
        while (retries < maxRetries) {
            socket.send(new DatagramPacket(packet, packet.length, dest));
            
            // Wait for ACK
            try {
                socket.setSoTimeout((int) timeoutMs);
                byte[] ackBuf = new byte[4];
                DatagramPacket ack = new DatagramPacket(ackBuf, ackBuf.length);
                socket.receive(ack);
                
                int ackedSeq = ByteBuffer.wrap(ackBuf).getInt();
                if (ackedSeq == sequenceNumber) {
                    sequenceNumber++; // success
                    return;
                }
            } catch (SocketTimeoutException e) {
                retries++;
                timeoutMs *= 2; // exponential backoff
                System.out.println("Timeout, retry " + retries);
            }
        }
        throw new IOException("Delivery failed after " + maxRetries + " retries");
    }
}
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Assuming UDP packets arrive in order (they don't тАФ implement sequence numbers if needed)
- UDP payload limited to ~65507 bytes (65535 - headers); fragment at the application layer for larger messages
- Not handling lost UDP packets for critical data
- Using UDP for large data transfers without implementing flow control тАФ receiver overwhelm



### 3. HTTP Basics

**HTTP Request/Response Structure:**
```
Request:
  METHOD /path?query HTTP/1.1\r\n
  Host: example.com\r\n
  Content-Type: application/json\r\n
  Content-Length: 42\r\n
  \r\n
  {"body": "data"}

Response:
  HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
  Content-Type: application/json\r\n
  Content-Length: 28\r\n
  Connection: keep-alive\r\n
  \r\n
  {"status": "ok", "id": 123}
```

**HTTP Versions:**
```
HTTP/1.0  тАФ new TCP connection per request (no keep-alive by default)
HTTP/1.1  тАФ persistent connections (keep-alive), pipelining (rarely used), chunked transfer
HTTP/2    тАФ multiplexing (multiple requests over one TCP connection), header compression (HPACK),
            server push, binary framing layer
HTTP/3    тАФ built on QUIC (UDP-based), eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking, faster handshake
```

**Status Codes тАФ what a senior must know deeply:**
```
1xx Informational   100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols
2xx Success         200 OK, 201 Created, 202 Accepted, 204 No Content, 206 Partial Content
3xx Redirection     301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found, 304 Not Modified, 307/308 Temporary/Permanent
4xx Client Error    400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized (unauthenticated!), 403 Forbidden,
                    404 Not Found, 405 Method Not Allowed, 409 Conflict, 410 Gone,
                    422 Unprocessable Entity, 429 Too Many Requests
5xx Server Error    500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable,
                    504 Gateway Timeout
```

**Keep-Alive and Connection Reuse:**
```
HTTP/1.1 default: Connection: keep-alive
Server declares how long to keep idle connection open
Client must not send request on connection after server sends Connection: close
Failure to respect this тЖТ "connection reset" errors under load
```

**Raw HTTP over Socket (illustrative):**
```java
// Shows what HTTP client libraries do under the hood
public class RawHttpClient {
    public static String get(String host, int port, String path) throws IOException {
        try (Socket socket = new Socket()) {
            socket.connect(new InetSocketAddress(host, port), 5_000);
            socket.setSoTimeout(10_000);
            
            PrintWriter out = new PrintWriter(
                new BufferedWriter(new OutputStreamWriter(socket.getOutputStream())),
                false
            );
            BufferedReader in = new BufferedReader(
                new InputStreamReader(socket.getInputStream())
            );
            
            // Write HTTP/1.1 request
            out.print("GET " + path + " HTTP/1.1\r\n");
            out.print("Host: " + host + "\r\n");
            out.print("Connection: close\r\n"); // close after response
            out.print("Accept: application/json\r\n");
            out.print("\r\n"); // blank line = end of headers
            out.flush(); // CRITICAL
            
            // Read status line
            String statusLine = in.readLine();
            System.out.println("Status: " + statusLine); // HTTP/1.1 200 OK
            
            // Read headers
            Map<String, String> headers = new LinkedHashMap<>();
            String headerLine;
            while (!(headerLine = in.readLine()).isEmpty()) {
                int colon = headerLine.indexOf(':');
                headers.put(
                    headerLine.substring(0, colon).trim().toLowerCase(),
                    headerLine.substring(colon + 1).trim()
                );
            }
            
            // Read body
            StringBuilder body = new StringBuilder();
            String line;
            while ((line = in.readLine()) != null) {
                body.append(line).append('\n');
            }
            
            return body.toString();
        }
    }
}
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Conflating 401 (not authenticated) with 403 (authenticated but not authorized)
- Not following redirects тАФ manual implementation must cap redirect depth (loop prevention)
- Ignoring `Content-Length` тАФ reading past end of response or not reading enough
- Not handling chunked transfer encoding when `Content-Length` is absent



### 4. URL Connections

**`java.net.URL` and `HttpURLConnection` тАФ legacy but widely encountered:**

```java
// HttpURLConnection тАФ Java's built-in HTTP client pre-Java 11
public class HttpUrlConnectionClient {
    
    public static String get(String urlString) throws IOException {
        URL url = new URL(urlString);
        HttpURLConnection conn = (HttpURLConnection) url.openConnection();
        
        try {
            conn.setRequestMethod("GET");
            conn.setConnectTimeout(5_000);    // connection timeout
            conn.setReadTimeout(10_000);      // read timeout
            conn.setRequestProperty("Accept", "application/json");
            conn.setRequestProperty("User-Agent", "MyApp/1.0");
            conn.setInstanceFollowRedirects(true); // follow 3xx automatically
            
            int responseCode = conn.getResponseCode(); // triggers connection + request
            
            if (responseCode >= 200 && responseCode < 300) {
                return readStream(conn.getInputStream());
            } else {
                // IMPORTANT: read error stream or connection won't be returned to pool
                String error = readStream(conn.getErrorStream());
                throw new IOException("HTTP " + responseCode + ": " + error);
            }
        } finally {
            conn.disconnect(); // releases connection (returns to keep-alive pool if applicable)
        }
    }
    
    public static String post(String urlString, String json) throws IOException {
        URL url = new URL(urlString);
        HttpURLConnection conn = (HttpURLConnection) url.openConnection();
        
        try {
            conn.setRequestMethod("POST");
            conn.setDoOutput(true);            // enables writing body
            conn.setDoInput(true);             // enables reading response (default true)
            conn.setConnectTimeout(5_000);
            conn.setReadTimeout(10_000);
            conn.setRequestProperty("Content-Type", "application/json; charset=UTF-8");
            conn.setRequestProperty("Accept", "application/json");
            
            byte[] body = json.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
            conn.setFixedLengthStreamingMode(body.length); // don't buffer entire body
            // OR: conn.setChunkedStreamingMode(4096); // for streaming/unknown length
            
            try (OutputStream os = conn.getOutputStream()) {
                os.write(body);
                os.flush();
            }
            
            int responseCode = conn.getResponseCode();
            if (responseCode >= 200 && responseCode < 300) {
                return readStream(conn.getInputStream());
            } else {
                throw new IOException("HTTP " + responseCode + ": " +
                                      readStream(conn.getErrorStream()));
            }
        } finally {
            conn.disconnect();
        }
    }
    
    private static String readStream(InputStream is) throws IOException {
        if (is == null) return "";
        try (BufferedReader reader = new BufferedReader(
                new InputStreamReader(is, StandardCharsets.UTF_8))) {
            StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
            String line;
            while ((line = reader.readLine()) != null) sb.append(line).append('\n');
            return sb.toString().trim();
        }
    }
}
```

**HTTPS and SSL/TLS:**
```java
// HTTPS works transparently via HttpURLConnection тАФ URL protocol determines it
URL url = new URL("https://api.example.com/data");
HttpsURLConnection conn = (HttpsURLConnection) url.openConnection();

// Custom TrustManager тАФ for self-signed certs (TEST ONLY тАФ never in production!)
TrustManager[] trustAll = { new X509TrustManager() {
    public X509Certificate[] getAcceptedIssuers() { return new X509Certificate[0]; }
    public void checkClientTrusted(X509Certificate[] c, String a) {}
    public void checkServerTrusted(X509Certificate[] c, String a) {}
}};

SSLContext sc = SSLContext.getInstance("TLS");
sc.init(null, trustAll, new SecureRandom());
conn.setSSLSocketFactory(sc.getSocketFactory());
conn.setHostnameVerifier((hostname, session) -> true); // INSECURE тАФ test only!

// Production: load custom truststore
KeyStore trustStore = KeyStore.getInstance("JKS");
trustStore.load(new FileInputStream("truststore.jks"), "password".toCharArray());
TrustManagerFactory tmf = TrustManagerFactory.getInstance(TrustManagerFactory.getDefaultAlgorithm());
tmf.init(trustStore);
SSLContext sslContext = SSLContext.getInstance("TLS");
sslContext.init(null, tmf.getTrustManagers(), null);
conn.setSSLSocketFactory(sslContext.getSocketFactory());
```

**Proxy Configuration:**
```java
// Explicit proxy
Proxy proxy = new Proxy(Proxy.Type.HTTP, new InetSocketAddress("proxy.corp.com", 8080));
HttpURLConnection conn = (HttpURLConnection) url.openConnection(proxy);

// SOCKS proxy
Proxy socks = new Proxy(Proxy.Type.SOCKS, new InetSocketAddress("socks.corp.com", 1080));

// System-wide proxy via JVM properties
System.setProperty("http.proxyHost",  "proxy.corp.com");
System.setProperty("http.proxyPort",  "8080");
System.setProperty("https.proxyHost", "proxy.corp.com");
System.setProperty("https.proxyPort", "8080");
System.setProperty("http.nonProxyHosts", "localhost|127.*|internal.*");
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Not reading `getErrorStream()` on non-2xx responses тАФ connection not returned to pool, leaks
- Using `setFixedLengthStreamingMode` without knowing content length тАФ use `setChunkedStreamingMode` for unknown sizes
- Calling `disconnect()` eagerly тАФ kills keep-alive connection pooling; call only when truly done with the host
- Thread safety тАФ `HttpURLConnection` is NOT thread-safe; one instance per request



### 5. REST Clients

#### Java 11+ HttpClient (Modern Standard)

```java
// Create client тАФ reuse across requests (manages connection pool, thread pool)
HttpClient client = HttpClient.newBuilder()
    .version(HttpClient.Version.HTTP_2)            // prefer HTTP/2, fallback to HTTP/1.1
    .followRedirects(HttpClient.Redirect.NORMAL)   // follow non-HTTPSтЖТHTTP redirects
    .connectTimeout(Duration.ofSeconds(5))
    .executor(Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10))    // custom thread pool for async ops
    .authenticator(new Authenticator() {
        @Override protected PasswordAuthentication getPasswordAuthentication() {
            return new PasswordAuthentication("user", "pass".toCharArray());
        }
    })
    .proxy(ProxySelector.of(new InetSocketAddress("proxy.corp.com", 8080)))
    .sslContext(customSslContext)
    .build();
```

**Synchronous Requests:**
```java
// GET
HttpRequest getRequest = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
    .uri(URI.create("https://api.example.com/users/1"))
    .timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(10))
    .header("Accept", "application/json")
    .header("Authorization", "Bearer " + token)
    .GET()
    .build();

HttpResponse<String> response = client.send(getRequest, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString());

System.out.println(response.statusCode()); // 200
System.out.println(response.body());       // {"id":1,"name":"Alice"}
System.out.println(response.headers().firstValue("Content-Type").orElse(""));

// POST with JSON body
String jsonBody = """
    {
        "name": "Alice",
        "email": "alice@example.com"
    }
    """;

HttpRequest postRequest = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
    .uri(URI.create("https://api.example.com/users"))
    .timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(10))
    .header("Content-Type", "application/json")
    .header("Accept", "application/json")
    .POST(HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString(jsonBody))
    .build();

HttpResponse<String> postResponse = client.send(postRequest, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString());
// 201 Created

// PUT
HttpRequest putRequest = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
    .uri(URI.create("https://api.example.com/users/1"))
    .header("Content-Type", "application/json")
    .PUT(HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString(jsonBody))
    .build();

// DELETE
HttpRequest deleteRequest = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
    .uri(URI.create("https://api.example.com/users/1"))
    .DELETE()
    .build();

// PATCH тАФ no built-in method, use method()
HttpRequest patchRequest = HttpRequest.newBuilder()
    .uri(URI.create("https://api.example.com/users/1"))
    .header("Content-Type", "application/json-patch+json")
    .method("PATCH", HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString("[{\"op\":\"replace\",\"path\":\"/name\",\"value\":\"Bob\"}]"))
    .build();
```

**Asynchronous Requests:**
```java
// sendAsync тАФ returns CompletableFuture, non-blocking
CompletableFuture<HttpResponse<String>> future = client.sendAsync(
    getRequest, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()
);

// Transform response asynchronously
CompletableFuture<User> userFuture = future
    .thenApply(response -> {
        if (response.statusCode() != 200) {
            throw new RuntimeException("HTTP " + response.statusCode());
        }
        return response.body();
    })
    .thenApply(json -> parseUser(json)) // deserialize
    .exceptionally(ex -> {
        log.error("Request failed", ex);
        return null;
    });

// Wait for result (don't call join() on reactive pipelines тАФ defeats the purpose)
User user = userFuture.join(); // blocks calling thread

// Fan-out: fire multiple requests concurrently
List<URI> uris = List.of(
    URI.create("https://api.example.com/users/1"),
    URI.create("https://api.example.com/users/2"),
    URI.create("https://api.example.com/users/3")
);

List<CompletableFuture<User>> futures = uris.stream()
    .map(uri -> HttpRequest.newBuilder().uri(uri).GET().build())
    .map(req -> client.sendAsync(req, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString())
                      .thenApply(r -> parseUser(r.body())))
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

// Wait for all
CompletableFuture.allOf(futures.toArray(new CompletableFuture[0])).join();
List<User> users = futures.stream()
    .map(CompletableFuture::join)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());
```

**Body Handlers and Publishers:**
```java
// BodyHandlers тАФ how to process the response body
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()               // String (UTF-8 default)
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString(StandardCharsets.UTF_8)
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofByteArray()            // byte[]
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofFile(Path.of("out.json")) // write directly to file
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofInputStream()          // streaming тАФ you handle the stream
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.discarding()             // ignore body (e.g., DELETE)
HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofLines()                // Stream<String> line by line

// Streaming a large response without loading into memory
HttpResponse<InputStream> streamResponse = client.send(
    getRequest, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofInputStream()
);
try (InputStream is = streamResponse.body()) {
    // process incrementally тАФ never loads full body into heap
    processLargeResponse(is);
}

// BodyPublishers тАФ how to provide the request body
HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString(json)
HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofByteArray(bytes)
HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofFile(Path.of("upload.json"))
HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofInputStream(() -> new FileInputStream("data.bin"))
HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.noBody()                 // GET, DELETE
```

**Retry Logic with Exponential Backoff:**
```java
public class RetryingHttpClient {
    
    private final HttpClient client;
    private final int maxRetries;
    
    public HttpResponse<String> sendWithRetry(HttpRequest request) throws Exception {
        int attempt = 0;
        long delayMs = 500;
        
        while (true) {
            try {
                HttpResponse<String> response = client.send(
                    request, HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()
                );
                
                if (response.statusCode() == 429) { // Too Many Requests
                    String retryAfter = response.headers()
                        .firstValue("Retry-After").orElse("1");
                    long wait = Long.parseLong(retryAfter) * 1000;
                    Thread.sleep(wait);
                    continue;
                }
                
                if (response.statusCode() >= 500 && attempt < maxRetries) {
                    attempt++;
                    Thread.sleep(delayMs + ThreadLocalRandom.current().nextLong(100));
                    delayMs = Math.min(delayMs * 2, 30_000); // cap at 30s
                    continue;
                }
                
                return response;
                
            } catch (HttpTimeoutException | ConnectException e) {
                if (attempt >= maxRetries) throw e;
                attempt++;
                Thread.sleep(delayMs);
                delayMs = Math.min(delayMs * 2, 30_000);
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Third-Party REST Clients:**

```java
// тФАтФА OkHttp тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
OkHttpClient okClient = new OkHttpClient.Builder()
    .connectTimeout(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
    .readTimeout(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
    .writeTimeout(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
    .connectionPool(new ConnectionPool(10, 5, TimeUnit.MINUTES)) // pool config
    .addInterceptor(chain -> {                // request interceptor
        Request req = chain.request().newBuilder()
            .header("Authorization", "Bearer " + getToken())
            .build();
        return chain.proceed(req);
    })
    .addInterceptor(new HttpLoggingInterceptor()) // logging
    .retryOnConnectionFailure(true)
    .build();

// GET
Request request = new Request.Builder()
    .url("https://api.example.com/users/1")
    .get()
    .build();

try (Response response = okClient.newCall(request).execute()) {
    if (!response.isSuccessful()) throw new IOException("HTTP " + response.code());
    String body = response.body().string(); // MUST close response тАФ use try-with-resources
}

// POST
RequestBody body = RequestBody.create(json, MediaType.get("application/json"));
Request postReq = new Request.Builder()
    .url("https://api.example.com/users")
    .post(body)
    .build();

// Async
okClient.newCall(request).enqueue(new Callback() {
    @Override public void onFailure(Call call, IOException e) { /* handle */ }
    @Override public void onResponse(Call call, Response response) throws IOException {
        try (response) { String body = response.body().string(); }
    }
});

// тФАтФА Apache HttpClient 5 тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
CloseableHttpClient apacheClient = HttpClients.custom()
    .setConnectionManager(PoolingHttpClientConnectionManagerBuilder.create()
        .setMaxConnTotal(100)
        .setMaxConnPerRoute(20)
        .build())
    .setDefaultRequestConfig(RequestConfig.custom()
        .setConnectionRequestTimeout(Timeout.ofSeconds(5))
        .setResponseTimeout(Timeout.ofSeconds(10))
        .build())
    .setRetryStrategy(new DefaultHttpRequestRetryStrategy(3, TimeValue.ofSeconds(1)))
    .build();

try (CloseableHttpResponse response = apacheClient.execute(
        new HttpGet("https://api.example.com/data"))) {
    int statusCode = response.getCode();
    String responseBody = EntityUtils.toString(response.getEntity(), StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
}

// тФАтФА Spring WebClient (Reactive) тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА
WebClient webClient = WebClient.builder()
    .baseUrl("https://api.example.com")
    .defaultHeader("Accept", "application/json")
    .defaultHeader("Authorization", "Bearer " + token)
    .filter(ExchangeFilterFunction.ofRequestProcessor(req -> {
        log.info("Request: {} {}", req.method(), req.url());
        return Mono.just(req);
    }))
    .codecs(c -> c.defaultCodecs().maxInMemorySize(10 * 1024 * 1024)) // 10MB
    .build();

// Reactive GET
Mono<User> userMono = webClient.get()
    .uri("/users/{id}", 1)
    .retrieve()
    .onStatus(HttpStatusCode::is4xxClientError, resp ->
        resp.bodyToMono(ErrorResponse.class)
            .flatMap(err -> Mono.error(new ClientException(err.message()))))
    .onStatus(HttpStatusCode::is5xxServerError, resp ->
        Mono.error(new ServerException("Server error: " + resp.statusCode())))
    .bodyToMono(User.class); // non-blocking тАФ nothing executed yet

// Execute
User user = userMono.block(); // sync (avoid in reactive pipelines)
userMono.subscribe(u -> System.out.println(u)); // async

// Reactive POST
Mono<User> created = webClient.post()
    .uri("/users")
    .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON)
    .bodyValue(new CreateUserRequest("Alice", "alice@example.com"))
    .retrieve()
    .bodyToMono(User.class);
```

**Connection Pool Sizing тАФ production tuning:**
```
Pool size formula: threads ├Ч (avg_response_time / avg_request_time)
Too small тЖТ queue buildup тЖТ timeouts
Too large тЖТ memory pressure, connection overhead at server

For microservices: size per route not just globally
Idle eviction: close connections idle > server's keep-alive timeout
   (nginx default: 75s, so evict client-side at ~60s)
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Creating a new `HttpClient` per request тАФ loses connection pooling
- Not closing `OkHttp` `Response` body тАФ file descriptor and memory leak
- Forgetting to configure BOTH connect timeout AND read/request timeout тАФ they are independent
- Blocking on `WebClient` in a reactive pipeline (`block()` inside flatMap) тАФ deadlock
- Not handling `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After` header тАФ hammering the server



## Interview Questions

**Q1: What is the difference between a Socket and a ServerSocket?**

`ServerSocket` is a passive endpoint тАФ it binds to a port and listens for incoming connection requests, blocking on `accept()` until a client connects. It does not exchange data. `ServerSocket.accept()` returns a `Socket` representing the specific accepted connection. `Socket` is an active endpoint representing one established TCP connection, used by both the client (who initiated) and the server (returned from `accept()`). For each client connection, the server has one `Socket` but the `ServerSocket` remains open accepting more.



**Q2: What happens if you don't set `SO_TIMEOUT` on a socket?**

The socket read operations block **indefinitely** тАФ they never return unless data arrives or the connection is closed. In a thread-per-connection server, if a client connects and then stops sending data (network partition, crash without FIN), the server thread hangs forever, leaking the thread and its socket file descriptor. This is the classic cause of thread exhaustion under load. Always set both `setSoTimeout()` for reads and specify a connect timeout via `socket.connect(addr, timeout)` тАФ these are independent settings.



**Q3: Explain the TCP three-way handshake and why it matters for performance.**

Client sends SYN тЖТ Server responds SYN-ACK тЖТ Client sends ACK; at this point the connection is established and the first data can be sent. This takes one full round-trip before any application data flows. For short-lived connections (HTTP/1.0 style), this adds at least one RTT of latency per request. HTTP keep-alive and HTTP/2 connection reuse amortize this cost. TLS adds 1-2 additional round trips (TLS 1.2 = 2 RTT, TLS 1.3 = 1 RTT, TLS 1.3 0-RTT resumption = zero). In a distributed system, creating new connections on every request under load causes latency spikes тАФ connection pooling eliminates this.



**Q4: When would you choose UDP over TCP?**

Choose UDP when: (1) low latency matters more than reliability тАФ video/audio streaming where a dropped frame is preferable to delay; (2) you have a simple request/response that fits in one datagram тАФ DNS (most queries), small telemetry messages; (3) you want broadcast or multicast тАФ service discovery, mDNS; (4) you're building your own reliability protocol tuned to your use case (QUIC does this). Don't use UDP when data integrity or ordering is required and you'd have to re-implement TCP's features тАФ you'll end up with a worse TCP.



**Q5: What is the difference between `HTTP/1.1` keep-alive, `HTTP/2` multiplexing, and `HTTP/3`?**

HTTP/1.1 keep-alive reuses a TCP connection for multiple sequential requests but still has head-of-line blocking тАФ request N must complete before N+1 starts on the same connection. HTTP/2 multiplexing sends multiple requests in parallel over a single TCP connection using independently framed streams, eliminating HTTP-level head-of-line blocking тАФ but TCP-level head-of-line blocking remains (a dropped TCP packet stalls all streams). HTTP/3 runs over QUIC (UDP-based) where streams are independent at the transport level too тАФ a lost packet only affects its stream, not others. HTTP/3 also achieves faster handshakes (0-RTT connection resumption).



**Q6: How does `HttpClient` (Java 11+) handle connection pooling?**

`HttpClient` maintains an internal connection pool per `HttpClient` instance. Connections are keyed by host, port, and protocol version. It automatically reuses keep-alive HTTP/1.1 connections and maintains persistent HTTP/2 connections (which multiplex all requests to the same host). This is why you must create one `HttpClient` instance per application (or per logical "client configuration") and share it тАФ a new `HttpClient` per request forfeits all pooling benefits and creates one connection per request, exactly like HTTP/1.0.



**Q7: What is the self-invocation problem equivalent in networking тАФ and what is it?**

The networking equivalent of AOP self-invocation is the **proxy bypass problem**: when a service calls itself via `localhost` or its internal IP rather than through its load balancer or service mesh proxy, it bypasses circuit breakers, rate limiters, retries, and observability instrumentation that live in the proxy layer. Always route internal calls through the same service mesh/proxy path (sidecar patterns like Envoy) or implement resilience in the client directly.



**Q8: How do you handle connection leaks in HTTP clients?**

Connection leaks occur when responses are not fully read or properly closed. For `HttpURLConnection`: always read `getErrorStream()` on non-2xx responses; always call `disconnect()` in `finally`. For `OkHttp`: always close `Response` in try-with-resources. For `HttpClient` (Java 11): if using `ofInputStream()` handler, always close the stream. Use connection pool metrics (active, idle, pending counts) to detect leaks in production. Set `connectionPool` max-idle-time to prevent stale connections: servers close idle connections (nginx at 75s), clients must evict before that or receive RST.



**Q9: What is the difference between `connectTimeout` and `readTimeout`?**

`connectTimeout` limits how long the TCP handshake may take тАФ how long to wait for the server to respond to SYN. If the server is unreachable, down, or the network is dropping packets, this timeout triggers. `readTimeout` (or request timeout in Java 11 `HttpClient`) limits how long to wait for data AFTER the connection is established тАФ covers the time from sending the request to receiving the first byte of response (plus time between bytes for slow bodies). Both must be set independently. A missing `readTimeout` means a slow server can hold your thread/coroutine indefinitely even after successfully connecting.



**Q10: How does `NIO` Selector-based networking differ from thread-per-connection?**

Thread-per-connection: each connected client gets a dedicated thread that blocks on I/O. Simple to code but doesn't scale past ~10,000 concurrent connections (JVM thread overhead ~1MB stack each). NIO selector: a single thread (or small pool) monitors many channels simultaneously via `Selector.select()` тАФ an OS-level `epoll`/`kqueue` call. The thread only activates when a channel has data ready. This is the C10K problem solution тАФ Netty, Undertow, Vert.x are all built on this model. Java 21 virtual threads close this gap: thread-per-connection becomes viable again because virtual threads are cheap (~few KB) and block on virtual-thread-aware I/O without blocking OS threads.



**Q11: Explain the `TIME_WAIT` state and `SO_REUSEADDR`.**

After the active closer sends the final ACK in TCP teardown, the connection enters `TIME_WAIT` for `2├ЧMSL` (Maximum Segment Lifetime, typically 60sтАУ4min). This ensures the remote end receives the final ACK and prevents old packets from a closed connection being mistaken for a new one. During `TIME_WAIT`, the local port cannot be reused. For servers that restart quickly (e.g., development, rolling deploy), the same port can't rebind immediately, causing `Address already in use`. `SO_REUSEADDR` tells the OS to allow binding to a port in `TIME_WAIT` state тАФ safe because the kernel still prevents actual packet confusion. Always set `setReuseAddress(true)` before `bind()` on server sockets.



**Q12: How would you implement circuit breaker pattern in a REST client?**

A circuit breaker wraps HTTP calls with three states: Closed (calls pass through), Open (calls fail fast without making network calls), Half-Open (probe calls to test recovery). Track failure rate over a sliding window (failures / total calls in last N seconds). When rate exceeds threshold (e.g., 50% failures in 10s), trip to Open. After a cool-down period, transition to Half-Open and allow one probe. Success тЖТ Closed, failure тЖТ Open again. In Java: use Resilience4j's `CircuitBreaker`, wrap `HttpClient.send()` calls with `Decorators.ofSupplier(circuitBreaker, callable)`. Record `CallNotPermittedException` as circuit-open metrics in your monitoring.



**Q13: What is the difference between `WebClient` and `RestTemplate`?**

`RestTemplate` is Spring's synchronous, blocking HTTP client тАФ each call occupies a thread for the duration. It's straightforward but doesn't scale well under high concurrency in non-blocking runtimes. `WebClient` is Spring's reactive, non-blocking HTTP client built on Project Reactor and Netty тАФ it returns `Mono<T>` or `Flux<T>` and doesn't hold threads while waiting for I/O. `RestTemplate` is in maintenance mode (no new features). For new code in Spring Boot 3+, prefer `WebClient` or the new `RestClient` (Spring 6.1+, a synchronous but modern API). Use `WebClient` when building reactive pipelines; use `RestClient` for simple synchronous service-to-service calls.



**Q14: How do you securely handle TLS/SSL in Java HTTP clients?**

Use the JVM's default `SSLContext` for standard CA-signed certificates тАФ it trusts the JDK's cacerts store (updated JDK keeps up with CA changes). For custom/internal CAs: load your CA certificate into a `KeyStore`, create a `TrustManagerFactory`, initialize an `SSLContext`, and pass it to your client builder. For mutual TLS (mTLS): also load the client certificate into a `KeyStore`, create a `KeyManagerFactory`, and init the `SSLContext` with both. NEVER disable hostname verification or trust-all in production тАФ it defeats TLS entirely, making you vulnerable to MITM attacks. Pin certificates or public keys only for high-security scenarios (mobile clients, not server-to-server where certs rotate).



**Q15: How would you diagnose a "connection reset by peer" error?**

`Connection reset` means the remote side sent a TCP RST packet instead of a graceful FIN. Causes: (1) Server closed the connection (keep-alive timeout expired) between requests тАФ client tried to reuse a dead connection; fix: reduce client-side idle eviction time below server's keep-alive timeout; (2) Server crashed or was killed; (3) Load balancer or firewall forcibly closed idle connection; (4) Server-side code threw an exception after writing partial response and closed abruptly; (5) Client sent data after server closed (e.g., pipelining violation). Diagnose with: Wireshark/tcpdump to see the RST at the TCP level, server logs for uncaught exceptions, connection pool metrics for idle vs. active connection ratio.



## Advanced / Senior-Level Considerations

**Virtual Threads (Java 21) and Networking:**
```java
// Virtual threads make blocking I/O cheap тАФ thread-per-connection is viable again
// Each virtual thread is ~few KB vs ~1MB for OS thread
// Socket.read() on a virtual thread unmounts from OS thread while blocked
ExecutorService vt = Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor();

// Simple high-concurrency server with virtual threads тАФ no NIO complexity needed
try (ServerSocket server = new ServerSocket(8080)) {
    while (true) {
        Socket client = server.accept();
        // Spawning 100k virtual threads is fine тАФ impossible with OS threads
        vt.submit(() -> handleClient(client));
    }
}
```

**gRPC over HTTP/2:**
```
gRPC uses HTTP/2 framing with Protocol Buffers serialization.
- Streaming RPCs: client-streaming, server-streaming, bidirectional
- Strongly typed contracts via .proto files
- Built-in deadlines, cancellation, flow control, load balancing hints
- Requires HTTP/2 end-to-end тАФ load balancers must be HTTP/2-aware
- For Java: grpc-java library; HTTP/2 via Netty transport
```

**Network Observability тАФ what senior engineers instrument:**
```
Per-client-pool metrics:
  - connections.active, connections.idle, connections.pending
  - request.duration (histogram тАФ p50/p95/p99)
  - request.errors (by status code, by exception type)
  - retries.count, circuit_breaker.state

JVM DNS cache:
  networkaddress.cache.ttl (default: 30s in security context, unlimited otherwise)
  Short TTL critical for service discovery with DNS-based load balancing
  System.setProperty("networkaddress.cache.ttl", "5"); // or JVM flag
```

**Wireshark / tcpdump for diagnosis:**
```bash
# Capture HTTP traffic to port 8080
tcpdump -i eth0 -w capture.pcap 'tcp port 8080'

# Filter in Wireshark: follow TCP stream to see full HTTP conversation
# Look for: RST packets (reset), retransmissions (packet loss), 
#           window size 0 (backpressure), SYN without SYN-ACK (unreachable)
```



## Summary / Cheat Sheet

```
SOCKET ESSENTIALS
  ServerSocket(port)               тЖТ listen for connections
  socket.connect(addr, timeout)    тЖТ connect with explicit timeout
  setSoTimeout(ms)                 тЖТ read timeout (NOT connect timeout!)
  setTcpNoDelay(true)             тЖТ low latency (disable Nagle)
  setKeepAlive(true)              тЖТ OS keep-alive probes
  setReuseAddress(true)           тЖТ reuse TIME_WAIT port (set before bind)
  Always: try-with-resources, set both timeouts, flush buffers

TCP vs UDP
  TCP:  reliable, ordered, flow-controlled, connection-oriented
  UDP:  fast, best-effort, datagram, supports multicast, 8-byte header
  Choose UDP for: DNS, real-time media, gaming, service discovery

HTTP VERSIONS
  HTTP/1.1 тЖТ keep-alive, sequential, text-based, head-of-line blocking
  HTTP/2   тЖТ multiplexed, binary, header compression, still TCP HOL blocking
  HTTP/3   тЖТ QUIC (UDP), no TCP HOL blocking, 0-RTT resumption

STATUS CODE TRAPS
  401 тЖТ NOT authenticated (needs credentials)
  403 тЖТ authenticated, but NOT authorized (no permission)
  404 тЖТ not found (or deliberately hidden тАФ same as 403 sometimes)
  429 тЖТ rate limited тЖТ check Retry-After header
  503 тЖТ unavailable тЖТ retry with backoff

JAVA HTTP CLIENT (Java 11+)
  Create ONE HttpClient, reuse everywhere тАФ connection pool lives in it
  .sendAsync() тЖТ CompletableFuture тАФ non-blocking
  .send()      тЖТ blocking тАФ fine for non-reactive code
  BodyHandlers.ofInputStream() for large responses тАФ don't load into heap
  Always set .timeout() on request AND .connectTimeout() on client

HTTPCLIENT vs OKHTTP vs APACHE vs WEBCLIENT
  Java HttpClient  тЖТ built-in, no dependencies, HTTP/2, good for most cases
  OkHttp           тЖТ interceptors, mature retry, HTTP/2, Android compatible
  Apache HC5       тЖТ most configurable, enterprise features, large ecosystem
  WebClient        тЖТ reactive (non-blocking), Spring ecosystem, use in Reactor chains
  RestClient       тЖТ Spring 6.1+, synchronous, modern API, replaces RestTemplate

CONNECTION POOL RULES
  One client instance per app (not per request!)
  Idle eviction < server keep-alive timeout (nginx: 75s тЖТ evict at ~60s)
  Size: threads ├Ч (response_time / request_time)
  Monitor: active, idle, pending queue depth

TIMEOUT HIERARCHY
  DNS resolution timeout    тЖТ networkaddress.cache.ttl JVM property
  Connect timeout           тЖТ socket.connect(addr, ms) or client connectTimeout
  Request/read timeout      тЖТ setSoTimeout(ms) or HttpRequest.timeout()
  Always set BOTH тАФ they are independent

PRODUCTION CHECKLIST
  тЬУ Set connect AND read timeouts
  тЬУ Retry with exponential backoff + jitter
  тЬУ Handle 429 with Retry-After header
  тЬУ Circuit breaker (Resilience4j) for downstream calls
  тЬУ Read and close error stream on non-2xx (HttpURLConnection)
  тЬУ Connection pool metrics in monitoring
  тЬУ Don't disable TLS hostname verification
  тЬУ SO_REUSEADDR on ServerSocket before bind
  тЬУ Virtual threads (Java 21) for high-concurrency blocking I/O

SENIOR GOTCHAS
  - new Socket(host, port) ignores connect timeout тАФ use socket.connect(addr, ms)
  - HttpURLConnection: not reading errorStream leaks connection
  - OkHttp Response body must be closed тАФ use try-with-resources
  - WebClient .block() in reactive chain тЖТ deadlock
  - Same host + different ClassLoader тЖТ ClassCastException (not networking, but ClassLoader)
  - UDP: no ordering guarantee, 65507 byte max payload, no backpressure
  - TIME_WAIT: normal, expected тАФ not a bug; SO_REUSEADDR handles server restarts
  - DNS caching: JVM caches indefinitely by default in some configs тАФ set TTL explicitly
```


# Advanced Java Language Features тАФ Senior Developer Study Notes



## Topic Overview

**Definition:** Advanced Java language features are syntactic and structural constructs that go beyond basic OOP, enabling more expressive, maintainable, and type-safe code. They span from classic inner/nested class mechanics to modern additions in Java 9тАУ21+ (modules, sealed classes, pattern matching, records).

**Why it matters:**
- Signals deep JVM and language understanding in interviews
- Many features directly impact encapsulation, API design, and compile-time safety
- Modern features (sealed classes, pattern matching) are reshaping how Java handles algebraic data types and control flow
- Modules are critical for large-scale enterprise and library design

**Real-world applications:**
- Inner/anonymous classes: GUI listeners, legacy callback patterns, `Comparator` usage
- `var`: reducing boilerplate in complex generic code
- Switch expressions: replacing verbose if-else chains in parsers, state machines
- Sealed classes + pattern matching: building type-safe ASTs, domain models, protocol parsers
- Modules: microservice JAR isolation, strong encapsulation in SDK design



## Core Concepts (Mental Model)

```
Java Type System
тФЬтФАтФА Top-level classes
тФФтФАтФА Nested types
    тФЬтФАтФА Static nested classes     тЖТ like a top-level class, scoped inside another
    тФЬтФАтФА Inner classes             тЖТ holds implicit reference to outer instance
    тФЬтФАтФА Local classes             тЖТ defined inside a method
    тФФтФАтФА Anonymous classes         тЖТ local class with no name, inline declaration

Modern Control Flow
тФЬтФАтФА Switch statement (classic)
тФЬтФАтФА Switch expression (Java 14+)  тЖТ yields a value
тФФтФАтФА Pattern matching
    тФЬтФАтФА instanceof (Java 16+)
    тФФтФАтФА switch patterns (Java 21+)

Type Hierarchy Control
тФФтФАтФА Sealed classes (Java 17+)     тЖТ restricted subtyping

Modularity
тФФтФАтФА Java Platform Module System (JPMS, Java 9+)
```



## Subtopics Breakdown



### 1. Static Nested Classes

**Definition:** A class declared `static` inside another class. Has no implicit reference to the enclosing class instance.

**Key Details:**
- Can access only `static` members of the outer class directly
- Can be instantiated without an outer class instance
- Used for logically grouping helper types that don't need outer state
- Common in builder pattern, entry types (`Map.Entry`)

```java
public class Graph {
    private List<Node> nodes;

    // Static nested тАФ doesn't need Graph instance
    public static class Node {
        private int value;
        public Node(int value) { this.value = value; }
    }

    public static class Edge {
        private Node from, to;
        public Edge(Node from, Node to) {
            this.from = from;
            this.to = to;
        }
    }
}

// Usage тАФ no Graph instance needed
Graph.Node n1 = new Graph.Node(1);
Graph.Node n2 = new Graph.Node(2);
Graph.Edge edge = new Graph.Edge(n1, n2);
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Forgetting `static` and creating an unintended memory leak (inner class holds outer reference)
- Misusing as inner class when outer state is never needed



### 2. Inner Classes (Non-static)

**Definition:** A non-static class declared inside another class. Holds an **implicit reference** to the enclosing outer class instance.

**Key Details:**
- Can access all members (including `private`) of the outer class
- Cannot have `static` members (except `static final` constants)
- Each inner class instance is tied to an outer class instance
- Memory implication: inner instance prevents GC of outer instance

```java
public class EventBus {
    private List<String> log = new ArrayList<>();

    public class Subscriber {
        private String name;

        public Subscriber(String name) { this.name = name; }

        public void receive(String event) {
            // Directly accesses outer class's private field
            log.add("[" + name + "] received: " + event);
            System.out.println(log);
        }
    }
}

// Usage тАФ must have outer instance first
EventBus bus = new EventBus();
EventBus.Subscriber sub = bus.new Subscriber("ServiceA");
sub.receive("ORDER_CREATED");
```

**Accessing outer `this` explicitly:**
```java
public class Outer {
    private String name = "Outer";

    class Inner {
        private String name = "Inner";

        void print() {
            System.out.println(name);          // Inner
            System.out.println(Outer.this.name); // Outer тАФ explicit outer reference
        }
    }
}
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Classic Android/Java memory leak: passing an inner class instance to a long-lived component (e.g., thread, cache) keeps the outer alive indefinitely
- Serialization issues тАФ inner classes are not easily serializable
- Fix: use `static` nested class + pass needed outer state explicitly



### 3. Local Classes

**Definition:** A class defined inside a method body. Scoped to that method.

**Key Details:**
- Can access `effectively final` local variables from the enclosing method
- Rarely used in modern code (lambdas/anonymous classes usually preferred)
- Can implement interfaces, extend classes

```java
public void processOrders(List<Order> orders) {
    final String prefix = "ORDER"; // effectively final

    class OrderFormatter {
        String format(Order o) {
            return prefix + "-" + o.getId(); // captures local var
        }
    }

    OrderFormatter fmt = new OrderFormatter();
    orders.forEach(o -> System.out.println(fmt.format(o)));
}
```



### 4. Anonymous Classes

**Definition:** A one-off, nameless class that simultaneously declares and instantiates. Typically used to implement an interface or extend a class inline.

**Key Details:**
- Cannot have explicit constructors
- Can capture `effectively final` variables
- Pre-Java 8 workhorse for callbacks; now largely replaced by lambdas
- Still relevant when you need to override multiple methods or maintain state

```java
// Pre-lambda callback pattern
Comparator<String> byLength = new Comparator<String>() {
    @Override
    public int compare(String a, String b) {
        return Integer.compare(a.length(), b.length());
    }
};

// With state тАФ lambdas can't do this as cleanly
Runnable countingTask = new Runnable() {
    private int count = 0; // anonymous class can have fields

    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Run #" + (++count));
    }
};

// Anonymous class extending abstract class
abstract class Template {
    abstract void step1();
    abstract void step2();
    void run() { step1(); step2(); }
}

Template t = new Template() {
    void step1() { System.out.println("Custom step 1"); }
    void step2() { System.out.println("Custom step 2"); }
};
t.run();
```

**Anonymous vs Lambda:**
| Aspect | Anonymous Class | Lambda |
||||
| Target type | Any interface/class | Functional interface only |
| `this` reference | Refers to anonymous instance | Refers to enclosing scope |
| State (fields) | Yes | No (only captured finals) |
| Verbosity | High | Low |

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Using anonymous classes when a lambda suffices (verbosity without benefit)
- Forgetting `this` semantics difference from lambdas



### 5. `var` Keyword (Java 10+)

**Definition:** Local variable type inference. The compiler infers the type from the right-hand side at compile time. It is **not** dynamic typing.

**Key Details:**
- Only for **local variables**, `for` loop variables, and try-with-resources
- Cannot be used for fields, method parameters, or return types
- Type is fixed at compile time тАФ fully type-safe
- Improves readability with complex generic types

```java
// Without var тАФ verbose
Map<String, List<Map<Integer, String>>> registry = new HashMap<>();

// With var тАФ cleaner
var registry = new HashMap<String, List<Map<Integer, String>>>();

// For-each
var items = List.of("a", "b", "c");
for (var item : items) {
    System.out.println(item.toUpperCase()); // compiler knows item is String
}

// Try-with-resources
try (var conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url);
     var stmt = conn.createStatement()) {
    var rs = stmt.executeQuery("SELECT 1");
}

// ILLEGAL uses:
// var x;                  // no initializer
// var x = null;           // type cannot be inferred
// var x = {1, 2, 3};     // array initializer not allowed
// public var field = 5;   // fields not allowed
```

**`var` in lambdas (Java 11+):**
```java
// Allows annotations on lambda params
var result = list.stream()
    .filter((@NonNull var s) -> !s.isEmpty())
    .collect(Collectors.toList());
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Overusing `var` where the type adds clarity (`var x = parse(data)` тАФ what type?)
- Confusing with `dynamic` typing тАФ the type is still static
- Using `var` with diamond operator can sometimes widen type unexpectedly:
```java
var list = new ArrayList<>();  // inferred as ArrayList<Object> тАФ not ArrayList<String>!
var list = new ArrayList<String>(); // correct
```



### 6. Switch Expressions (Java 14+ stable)

**Definition:** `switch` that produces a **value**, using arrow syntax or `yield`. Eliminates fall-through bugs and enables exhaustiveness.

**Key Details:**
- Arrow form (`->`) executes one branch, no fall-through
- `yield` used to return values from blocks in switch expressions
- Works with `int`, `String`, `enum`, and (Java 21+) patterns
- Compiler enforces exhaustiveness for enums/sealed types

```java
// Classic switch statement (error-prone)
String result;
switch (day) {
    case MONDAY:
    case TUESDAY:
        result = "Weekday";
        break;
    case SATURDAY:
    case SUNDAY:
        result = "Weekend";
        break;
    default:
        result = "Midweek";
}

// Switch expression тАФ arrow form
String result = switch (day) {
    case MONDAY, TUESDAY, WEDNESDAY, THURSDAY, FRIDAY -> "Weekday";
    case SATURDAY, SUNDAY -> "Weekend";
};

// Switch expression тАФ with blocks and yield
int score = switch (grade) {
    case "A" -> 100;
    case "B" -> 85;
    case "C" -> {
        System.out.println("Average grade");
        yield 70; // yield required in block form
    }
    default -> throw new IllegalArgumentException("Unknown: " + grade);
};

// With enum тАФ exhaustive (no default needed if all cases covered)
enum Status { ACTIVE, INACTIVE, PENDING }

String label = switch (status) {
    case ACTIVE  -> "Running";
    case INACTIVE -> "Stopped";
    case PENDING -> "Waiting";
    // No default needed тАФ compiler verifies exhaustiveness
};
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Mixing arrow and colon forms in the same switch (compile error)
- Forgetting `yield` inside a block-form switch expression
- Using statement-form `switch` where expression is cleaner



### 7. Pattern Matching

#### 7a. `instanceof` Pattern Matching (Java 16+)

**Definition:** Combines type check and cast into a single expression.

```java
// Old way тАФ redundant cast
if (obj instanceof String) {
    String s = (String) obj;  // redundant
    System.out.println(s.length());
}

// Pattern matching тАФ binding variable scoped to true branch
if (obj instanceof String s) {
    System.out.println(s.length()); // s is String here, no cast
}

// Negative branch тАФ s NOT available (compiler enforced)
if (!(obj instanceof String s)) {
    return; // s not in scope here
}
System.out.println(s.length()); // s IS available тАФ flow-sensitive typing

// Combined conditions
if (obj instanceof String s && s.length() > 5) {
    System.out.println(s.toUpperCase());
}
```

#### 7b. Pattern Matching in `switch` (Java 21+ stable)

**Definition:** Switch can now match on type patterns, allowing type-safe dispatch over object hierarchies.

```java
sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {}
record Circle(double radius) implements Shape {}
record Rectangle(double width, double height) implements Shape {}
record Triangle(double base, double height) implements Shape {}

double area(Shape shape) {
    return switch (shape) {
        case Circle c    -> Math.PI * c.radius() * c.radius();
        case Rectangle r -> r.width() * r.height();
        case Triangle t  -> 0.5 * t.base() * t.height();
        // No default needed тАФ sealed hierarchy is exhaustive
    };
}
```

**Guarded patterns:**
```java
String describe(Object obj) {
    return switch (obj) {
        case Integer i when i < 0  -> "Negative int";
        case Integer i when i == 0 -> "Zero";
        case Integer i             -> "Positive int: " + i;
        case String s when s.isEmpty() -> "Empty string";
        case String s              -> "String: " + s;
        case null                  -> "null value";
        default                    -> "Other: " + obj.getClass().getName();
    };
}
```

**Deconstruction patterns (Java 21+ preview тЖТ stabilizing):**
```java
record Point(int x, int y) {}

// Destructure directly in switch
if (obj instanceof Point(int x, int y)) {
    System.out.println("x=" + x + ", y=" + y);
}
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Pattern variable `s` shadowing outer variables тАФ compiler warns
- Null handling: classic `switch` throws NPE on null; switch patterns can handle `case null` explicitly



### 8. Sealed Classes (Java 17+)

**Definition:** Restricts which classes/interfaces can extend or implement a type. Creates **closed type hierarchies** that the compiler can reason about exhaustively.

**Key Details:**
- Declared with `sealed` modifier + `permits` clause
- Permitted subtypes must be: `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`
- All permitted subclasses must be in the same package (or module)
- Enables exhaustive switch expressions without `default`
- Perfect companion to records and pattern matching (the "algebraic data type" trio)

```java
// Sealed interface with permitted implementations
sealed interface Result<T> permits Success, Failure, Pending {}

record Success<T>(T value) implements Result<T> {}
record Failure<T>(String error, Throwable cause) implements Result<T> {}
final class Pending<T> implements Result<T> {}  // not a record, but final

// Compiler-verified exhaustiveness
<T> String describe(Result<T> result) {
    return switch (result) {
        case Success<T> s  -> "OK: " + s.value();
        case Failure<T> f  -> "ERR: " + f.error();
        case Pending<T> p  -> "PENDING";
        // No default needed тАФ compiler knows all subtypes
    };
}
```

**Sealed interface hierarchy (tree of types):**
```java
sealed interface Expr permits Num, Add, Mul, Neg {}

record Num(int value)         implements Expr {}
record Add(Expr left, Expr right) implements Expr {}
record Mul(Expr left, Expr right) implements Expr {}
record Neg(Expr expr)         implements Expr {}

// Recursive evaluator
int eval(Expr e) {
    return switch (e) {
        case Num(int v)          -> v;
        case Add(var l, var r)   -> eval(l) + eval(r);
        case Mul(var l, var r)   -> eval(l) * eval(r);
        case Neg(var inner)      -> -eval(inner);
    };
}
```

**`non-sealed` escape hatch:**
```java
sealed interface Vehicle permits Car, Truck, OpenVehicle {}
final class Car implements Vehicle {}
final class Truck implements Vehicle {}
non-sealed class OpenVehicle implements Vehicle {} // anyone can extend this
class Bicycle extends OpenVehicle {} // allowed
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Permitted subclasses must explicitly `extend`/`implement` the sealed type тАФ won't compile otherwise
- If one subtype is `non-sealed`, the switch is no longer exhaustive without `default`
- Cannot use sealed with classes in different packages (unless same module)



### 9. Java Platform Module System тАФ JPMS (Java 9+)

**Definition:** A module is a named, self-describing collection of packages with explicit dependencies and exports. Declared in `module-info.java`.

**Key Details:**
- Replaces the fragile classpath with strong encapsulation
- Internal packages are inaccessible by default (unlike classpath where everything is visible)
- Enables JVM to link only required modules (smaller runtime images via `jlink`)
- Critical for large codebases, SDKs, and security-sensitive applications

**Module descriptor syntax:**
```java
// module-info.java at root of module source
module com.company.payments {
    // What this module depends on
    requires java.net.http;          // hard dependency
    requires transitive java.logging; // transitive тАФ consumers also get this
    requires static org.junit;        // optional at runtime (compile-time only)

    // What this module exposes
    exports com.company.payments.api;          // to everyone
    exports com.company.payments.internal      // only to these modules
        to com.company.admin, com.company.audit;

    // Reflection access (for frameworks like Spring, Hibernate)
    opens com.company.payments.model;          // open for deep reflection
    opens com.company.payments.config to spring.core;

    // Service provider interface pattern
    uses com.company.payments.spi.Gateway;
    provides com.company.payments.spi.Gateway
        with com.company.payments.impl.StripeGateway;
}
```

**Module types:**
| Type | Description |
|||
| Named module | Has `module-info.java`, on module path |
| Automatic module | JAR on module path without `module-info.java` тАФ module name from JAR name |
| Unnamed module | JAR on classpath тАФ can access all, accessible to no named module |

**Directory structure:**
```
src/
тФФтФАтФА com.company.payments/
    тФЬтФАтФА module-info.java
    тФФтФАтФА com/company/payments/
        тФЬтФАтФА api/
        тФВ   тФФтФАтФА PaymentService.java
        тФФтФАтФА internal/
            тФФтФАтФА PaymentProcessor.java  тЖР inaccessible outside module

```

**Compiling and running with modules:**
```bash
# Compile
javac -d out --module-source-path src \
      $(find src -name "*.java")

# Run
java --module-path out \
     --module com.company.payments/com.company.payments.Main

# Create minimal runtime image
jlink --module-path $JAVA_HOME/jmods:out \
      --add-modules com.company.payments \
      --output custom-runtime
```

**Reflection and module access (common framework issue):**
```bash
# If Spring/Hibernate needs reflective access тАФ add at runtime:
java --module-path out \
     --add-opens com.company.payments/com.company.payments.model=spring.core \
     --module com.company.app/com.company.app.Main
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- `InaccessibleObjectException` тАФ forgetting to `open` packages used by reflection-heavy frameworks
- Split packages: same package in two modules тАФ forbidden, common in legacy migration
- Automatic modules are a migration bridge тАФ don't rely on them permanently
- `module-info.java` must be at the module root, not in a subpackage



## Interview Questions тАФ 15 Deep-Dive Q&As



**Q1. What is the difference between a static nested class and an inner class?**

> A **static nested class** has no implicit reference to its enclosing class instance and can be instantiated independently (`new Outer.Nested()`). An **inner class** holds an implicit `Outer.this` reference тАФ every inner instance is bound to an outer instance and is instantiated via `outerRef.new Inner()`. Key consequence: inner classes prevent GC of the outer instance, while static nested classes don't. Prefer static nested unless you genuinely need access to outer instance state.



**Q2. What is an effectively final variable and why does it matter for anonymous/inner classes?**

> A variable is "effectively final" if it is never reassigned after initialization (even without the `final` keyword). Anonymous classes, local classes, and lambdas can only capture effectively final local variables because they may outlive the stack frame where the variable was declared. The compiler captures the *value* at capture time, so mutating it afterward would create inconsistency. Violating this causes a compile error: `"Variable used in lambda/anonymous class must be final or effectively final"`.



**Q3. Is `var` dynamically typed? When should you avoid it?**

> No. `var` is purely a compile-time type inference feature тАФ the type is resolved statically and fixed. The bytecode is identical to explicit typing. Avoid `var` when: (1) the right-hand side doesn't make the type obvious (`var result = process(data)` тАФ what is `result`?), (2) at API boundaries (can't use for fields/params), (3) with raw types or unparameterized generics where inference widens to `Object`. Use it freely for complex generic types where the type name adds noise but the context is clear.



**Q4. What is the difference between `switch` statement and `switch` expression?**

> A `switch` **statement** controls flow but produces no value; it uses `break` to prevent fall-through and is prone to accidental fall-through bugs. A `switch` **expression** produces a value and uses arrow syntax (`->`) or `yield` in blocks. Arrow-form has no fall-through. The compiler enforces **exhaustiveness** for switch expressions (every possible input must be covered), which catches bugs at compile time. Switch expressions are preferred in modern Java for assigning values based on conditions.



**Q5. What happens if you don't handle `null` in a pattern matching switch?**

> A `switch` with pattern matching throws `NullPointerException` if the selector expression is `null` and there is no explicit `case null`. This is a deliberate design тАФ you must opt-in to null handling. Best practice: add `case null -> ...` or handle null before the switch. Classic `switch` on primitives/strings also throws NPE on null, so this behavior is consistent.



**Q6. What are sealed classes and how do they work with pattern matching?**

> Sealed classes restrict their inheritance hierarchy to a known, compile-time set of permitted subtypes (via `permits`). This creates a **closed algebraic data type**. Combined with pattern matching in `switch`, the compiler can verify exhaustiveness тАФ if all permitted subtypes are covered, no `default` is needed. This is powerful for domain modeling (e.g., `Result<T>` with `Success`/`Failure`/`Pending`) because adding a new subtype to the sealed hierarchy causes compile errors at all switch sites that don't handle it, acting like a compiler-enforced contract.



**Q7. What is the `non-sealed` modifier and when would you use it?**

> `non-sealed` reopens a branch of a sealed hierarchy to unrestricted extension. It's an escape hatch for when you want most of your hierarchy controlled but need one subtype to be freely extensible (e.g., for third-party extensibility). The trade-off: any `switch` covering the sealed hierarchy must now include a `default` case because the `non-sealed` branch can have unknown subtypes.



**Q8. Explain the difference between `exports`, `opens`, and `requires transitive` in JPMS.**

> - **`exports pkg`**: Makes types in `pkg` accessible at compile and runtime to other modules (but not via deep reflection).
> - **`opens pkg`**: Makes types in `pkg` accessible for **deep reflection** (e.g., setting private fields) at runtime. Required by frameworks like Spring, Hibernate, JPA.
> - **`requires transitive M`**: This module depends on M, and any module depending on *this* module also implicitly depends on M. Used when your public API returns types from M тАФ callers would need M too.



**Q9. What is a split package in JPMS and why is it a problem?**

> A split package occurs when two modules both contain classes in the same package. JPMS forbids this because the module system needs each package to belong to exactly one module for reliable encapsulation. This is a common pain point when migrating legacy codebases where packages were spread across JARs. Fix: refactor packages so each belongs to one module, or consolidate the JARs.



**Q10. How does anonymous class `this` differ from lambda `this`?**

> In an **anonymous class**, `this` refers to the anonymous class instance itself. In a **lambda**, `this` refers to the enclosing class instance (the lexical `this`). This means lambdas cannot reference themselves (`this.someMethod()` refers to the outer class), while anonymous classes can. This also means lambdas cannot be used when `this` self-reference is needed (e.g., recursive anonymous Runnable that re-schedules itself).



**Q11. Can a sealed class be abstract? Can it be a record?**

> Yes to both. A sealed class can be `abstract sealed` тАФ it can't be instantiated directly, and all permitted subtypes must still be `final`/`sealed`/`non-sealed`. Records can implement sealed interfaces (common pattern): `record Success<T>(T value) implements Result<T> {}`. Records are implicitly `final`, which satisfies the sealed hierarchy constraint. This combination (sealed interface + records as data carriers) is the idiomatic Java algebraic data type pattern.



**Q12. What is `yield` in a switch expression and when is it required?**

> `yield` is used inside a **block** (`{ ... }`) in a switch expression to return a value from that block. It is required whenever you use the colon/block form inside a switch expression and need to return a value. In the arrow form (`case X -> value`), the value after `->` is implicitly yielded тАФ no `yield` keyword needed. `yield` is a **context-sensitive keyword** (not reserved), so existing code with a variable named `yield` still compiles.



**Q13. What is the module path vs classpath and how do they interact?**

> The **classpath** is the legacy mechanism тАФ all JARs are visible to each other with no encapsulation. The **module path** is the JPMS mechanism тАФ JARs with `module-info.java` are named modules with declared dependencies and exports. A JAR on the module path without `module-info.java` becomes an **automatic module** (module name derived from JAR filename). A JAR on the classpath is part of the **unnamed module** тАФ it can access all named modules' exported packages but named modules cannot `requires` the unnamed module. This asymmetry is intentional for gradual migration.



**Q14. When would you use a local class over an anonymous class or lambda?**

> Use a **local class** when you need a named type you can instantiate multiple times within the method, or when you need multiple methods (anonymous classes are limited to one interface/class). Use an **anonymous class** for a one-off implementation with possible state or multiple method overrides. Use a **lambda** when the target is a functional interface and no state or `this` self-reference is needed. In practice, local classes are rare in modern code тАФ lambdas handle most cases.



**Q15. How would you design a type-safe error handling type using sealed classes, records, and pattern matching?**

```java
sealed interface ApiResult<T> permits ApiResult.Ok, ApiResult.Err {
    record Ok<T>(T data) implements ApiResult<T> {}
    record Err<T>(int code, String message) implements ApiResult<T> {}

    // Factory methods
    static <T> ApiResult<T> ok(T data) { return new Ok<>(data); }
    static <T> ApiResult<T> err(int code, String msg) { return new Err<>(code, msg); }
}

// Usage тАФ exhaustive, no casting
ApiResult<User> result = userService.findById(id);
String response = switch (result) {
    case ApiResult.Ok<User>(User u)        -> "Found: " + u.name();
    case ApiResult.Err<User>(int c, var m) -> "Error " + c + ": " + m;
};
```

> This pattern gives you ML-style `Option`/`Either` types in Java with full compiler enforcement. Adding a new case to `ApiResult` breaks all existing switches тАФ the compiler finds every site needing update.



## Advanced / Senior-Level Considerations

### Memory and Performance
- **Inner class memory leak**: Pass inner class instances to thread pools, caches, or static fields тЖТ outer instance is permanently retained. Use `WeakReference` or refactor to static nested
- **Anonymous class bloat**: Each anonymous class generates a synthetic `.class` file. In hot paths, lambdas may be more JIT-friendly (captured as `invokedynamic`)
- **`var` has zero runtime cost** тАФ purely compile-time; bytecode identical to explicit type

### Design Patterns Enabled
- **Visitor pattern** тЖТ replaced by sealed classes + pattern matching switch (no boilerplate)
- **Builder pattern** тЖТ static nested class is idiomatic (`Person.Builder`)
- **Strategy pattern** тЖТ functional interfaces + lambdas (anonymous class was pre-Java 8 approach)
- **Algebraic data types** тЖТ sealed + records + pattern matching

### Modularity at Scale
- JPMS enables **strong encapsulation**: internal implementation packages are truly hidden (unlike just "don't use this" convention)
- Use `jdeps` to analyze actual dependencies before modularizing: `jdeps --print-module-deps myapp.jar`
- **jlink** creates minimal JRE bundles тАФ critical for containerized microservices (reduces image from ~300MB to ~40MB)
- Service loader (`uses`/`provides`) enables plugin architectures without coupling

### Integration with Other Systems
- **Spring/Hibernate** use reflection heavily тЖТ must `opens` packages to framework modules, or use `--add-opens` JVM flags
- **Lombok** generates code that may conflict with records (prefer records over Lombok `@Value` in Java 16+)
- **Pattern matching + Jackson**: serialize/deserialize sealed hierarchies using `@JsonSubTypes` or configure ObjectMapper for polymorphic types

### Java Version Roadmap (Feature Timeline)
```
Java 9  тЖТ Modules (JPMS)
Java 10 тЖТ var (local variables)
Java 11 тЖТ var in lambda params
Java 14 тЖТ Switch expressions (stable)
Java 16 тЖТ instanceof pattern matching (stable)
Java 16 тЖТ Records (stable)
Java 17 тЖТ Sealed classes (stable)
Java 21 тЖТ Pattern matching for switch (stable), Record patterns (stable)
```



## Summary / Cheat Sheet

```
NESTED TYPES
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ static nested   тЖТ no outer ref, instantiate freely          тФВ
тФВ inner class     тЖТ holds Outer.this, needs outer instance    тФВ
тФВ local class     тЖТ in method, captures effectively final varsтФВ
тФВ anonymous class тЖТ inline, one-off, can have fields          тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ

VAR
тАв Compile-time inference only тАФ NOT dynamic typing
тАв Only local vars, for-each, try-with-resources
тАв Avoid when type is non-obvious from RHS

SWITCH EXPRESSION
тАв Arrow form (->): no fall-through, value is RHS
тАв Block form: requires yield to return value
тАв Compiler enforces exhaustiveness

PATTERN MATCHING
тАв instanceof: if (x instanceof Foo f) тАФ f scoped to true branch
тАв switch: case Foo f -> ..., case Bar b when b.x() > 0 -> ...
тАв Null: add case null -> or handle before switch

SEALED CLASSES
тАв sealed + permits тЖТ closed hierarchy
тАв Permitted types must be final | sealed | non-sealed
тАв Enables exhaustive switch without default
тАв Combine with records for algebraic data types

MODULES (JPMS)
тАв exports pkg       тЖТ public API (no reflection)
тАв opens pkg         тЖТ reflection access (for frameworks)
тАв requires M        тЖТ hard dependency
тАв requires transitive M тЖТ dependency exposed to consumers
тАв Split packages forbidden
тАв unnamed module (classpath) тЙа named module (module path)
```

**Senior engineer differentiators to mention:**
- Memory implications of inner class captures (GC roots, leaks)
- Sealed + records + pattern matching as Java's ADT answer
- JPMS `opens` vs `exports` distinction (critical for Spring/Hibernate)
- `var` inference widening gotcha with raw generics
- `yield` as context-sensitive keyword (not reserved)
- Exhaustiveness checking as compile-time correctness guarantee

# JDBC тАФ Senior Developer Study Notes



## Topic Overview

**Definition:** Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) is a standard Java API (`java.sql`, `javax.sql`) that provides a uniform interface for connecting to and interacting with relational databases, regardless of the underlying database vendor.

**Why it matters:**
- Foundation of every Java data access layer тАФ JPA/Hibernate, Spring Data, MyBatis all sit on top of JDBC
- Understanding JDBC deeply means you can diagnose ORM issues, tune performance, and reason about what SQL is actually being executed
- Direct JDBC is still used in high-throughput, latency-sensitive paths where ORM overhead is unacceptable
- Transactions, connection pooling, and batch processing are senior-level concerns in any production system

**Real-world applications:**
- Every Spring `JdbcTemplate` / `NamedParameterJdbcTemplate` call goes through JDBC
- Hibernate's JDBC connection management, dirty checking flush, and batch insert tuning
- Microservices with HikariCP pools hitting PostgreSQL/MySQL
- ETL pipelines using batch JDBC inserts for bulk data loading
- Financial systems where explicit transaction control is mandatory



## Core Concepts (Mental Model)

```
Application Code
      тФВ
      тЦ╝
 JDBC API (java.sql.*)          тЖР standard interfaces your code targets
      тФВ
      тЦ╝
 JDBC Driver Manager            тЖР selects and loads the correct driver
      тФВ
      тЦ╝
 JDBC Driver (vendor JAR)       тЖР translates JDBC calls to DB wire protocol
      тФВ
      тЦ╝
 Database Server                тЖР PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, etc.

Key objects and their lifecycle:
DriverManager / DataSource
    тФФтФАтФА Connection              тЖР session with the DB
          тФЬтФАтФА Statement         тЖР ad-hoc SQL, no parameters
          тФЬтФАтФА PreparedStatement тЖР pre-compiled SQL, parameters
          тФФтФАтФА CallableStatement тЖР stored procedures
                тФФтФАтФА ResultSet  тЖР cursor over query results
                      тФФтФАтФА ResultSetMetaData
```



## Subtopics Breakdown



### 1. JDBC Architecture

**Definition:** JDBC follows a two-layer model тАФ the API layer (standard Java interfaces) and the Driver layer (vendor implementations). Your application always talks to the API; the driver handles translation to the specific database protocol.

**Key architectural layers:**

```
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ             Application Layer                    тФВ
тФВ   (your Java code using java.sql.* interfaces)   тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
                       тФВ JDBC API calls
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ            JDBC Driver Manager                   тФВ
тФВ   (DriverManager or DataSource routing)          тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
                       тФВ delegates to registered driver
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ              JDBC Driver (vendor)                тФВ
тФВ  PostgreSQL Driver тФВ MySQL Connector тФВ OracleDB  тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
                       тФВ native DB protocol (TCP)
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ                Database Server                   тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
```

**Two-tier vs Three-tier:**
- **Two-tier**: Java app connects directly to DB (typical microservice)
- **Three-tier**: Java app тЖТ application server (connection pool) тЖТ DB (enterprise pattern)

**Key interfaces in `java.sql`:**

| Interface | Responsibility |
|||
| `Driver` | Entry point for a vendor driver |
| `DriverManager` | Manages registered drivers, creates connections |
| `Connection` | Represents an active DB session |
| `Statement` | Executes static SQL |
| `PreparedStatement` | Pre-compiled parameterized SQL |
| `CallableStatement` | Stored procedure execution |
| `ResultSet` | Cursor over query result rows |
| `DatabaseMetaData` | Schema, capability introspection |
| `ResultSetMetaData` | Column type/name introspection |

**`javax.sql` extensions (enterprise):**

| Interface | Responsibility |
|||
| `DataSource` | Factory for connections (replaces `DriverManager` in production) |
| `ConnectionPoolDataSource` | Driver-side pooling support |
| `XADataSource` | Distributed (XA) transaction support |



### 2. JDBC Drivers

**Definition:** A JDBC driver is a vendor-provided implementation of the `java.sql.Driver` interface that translates JDBC API calls into the database's native wire protocol.

**Four driver types (historical but commonly asked):**

| Type | Name | Mechanism | Notes |
|||||
| 1 | JDBC-ODBC Bridge | Java тЖТ ODBC тЖТ DB | Deprecated, removed Java 8+ |
| 2 | Native API | Java + native C libs | Platform-dependent |
| 3 | Network Protocol | Pure Java тЖТ middleware | Flexible, adds hop |
