```
тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР  тФВ
тФВ  тФВ       Business Services        тФВ  тФВ
тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ  тФВ
тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР  тФВ
тФВ  тФВ       Data Access Layer        тФВ  тФВ
тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ  тФВ
тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР  тФВ
тФВ  тФВ     Single Database Schema     тФВ  тФВ
тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ  тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
```

**Types:**
- **Modular Monolith** тАФ well-structured internal modules, single deployable (recommended starting point)
- **Big Ball of Mud** тАФ no structure, everything depends on everything (what monoliths become without discipline)
- **Single-Page App + API Monolith** тАФ UI separate, backend is monolith

```java
// Modular Monolith тАФ Java 9+ modules enforce boundaries
// module-info.java in order module
module com.example.orders {
    requires com.example.catalog;
    requires com.example.payments;
    exports com.example.orders.api;          // public API only
    // com.example.orders.internal is NOT exported тАФ enforced at compile time
}

// module-info.java in payments module
module com.example.payments {
    exports com.example.payments.api;
    // Does NOT require orders тАФ no circular dependency
}
```

**Key Details:**
- Simple to develop, test, debug, deploy initially
- Vertical scaling (bigger machine) vs horizontal scaling (more instances тАФ requires stateless design)
- Shared DB schema creates implicit coupling between modules
- One bad deployment takes down everything
- Technology lock-in тАФ entire app must use same language/framework

**When to choose:**
- Team < 10 engineers
- Domain not yet well-understood
- Startup/MVP тАФ premature decomposition is a real risk
- Low traffic тАФ no need to scale parts independently

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Jumping to microservices before understanding the domain
- Letting the monolith become a Big Ball of Mud through lack of module discipline
- Shared mutable static state prevents horizontal scaling



### 3.2 Layered (N-Tier) Architecture

**Definition:** Application organized into horizontal layers, each with a specific responsibility. Most common: Presentation тЖТ Business Logic тЖТ Data Access тЖТ Database.

```
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ         Presentation Layer          тФВ  тЖР REST controllers, DTOs
тФВ         (@RestController)           тФВ
тФЬтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФд
тФВ         Business Logic Layer        тФВ  тЖР Domain logic, rules
тФВ         (@Service)                  тФВ
тФЬтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФд
тФВ         Data Access Layer           тФВ  тЖР Repositories, queries
тФВ         (@Repository)               тФВ
тФЬтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФд
тФВ         Database                    тФВ  тЖР PostgreSQL, MySQL
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
         тЖУ Dependencies flow downward only
```

```java
// Presentation Layer
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/orders")
public class OrderController {
    private final OrderService orderService;    // depends on layer below

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<OrderResponse> createOrder(@RequestBody @Valid CreateOrderRequest request) {
        OrderDTO order = orderService.createOrder(request.toCommand());
        return ResponseEntity.status(201).body(OrderResponse.from(order));
    }
}

// Business Layer
@Service
@Transactional
public class OrderService {
    private final OrderRepository orderRepository;
    private final InventoryService inventoryService;

    public OrderDTO createOrder(CreateOrderCommand command) {
        inventoryService.reserve(command.items()); // cross-service within layer
        Order order = Order.create(command);
        return OrderDTO.from(orderRepository.save(order));
    }
}

// Data Access Layer
@Repository
public interface OrderRepository extends JpaRepository<Order, UUID> {
    List<Order> findByCustomerIdAndStatus(UUID customerId, OrderStatus status);
}
```

**Key Details:**
- Strict layering: layer N can only call layer N-1 (no skipping)
- Relaxed layering: layer N can call any layer below (common in practice, less pure)
- The "Lasagna problem": too many layers adds indirection without value
- Easy to understand, test (mock the layer below), and onboard new developers

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Anemic domain model: business logic leaks into service layer, entities become data bags
- Fat services: all logic ends up in @Service classes, poor cohesion
- Circular dependencies between services in the same layer



### 3.3 Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters)

**Definition:** Domain is at the center, isolated from all external concerns (HTTP, DB, messaging) via ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). Coined by Alistair Cockburn.

```
                    тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
   REST API тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║ тФВ  Input Adapter          тФВ
   GraphQL тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ  (driving/primary)      тФВ
   Kafka   тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА тФЬтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР             тФВ
                    тФВ  INPUT    тФВ             тФВ
                    тФВ  PORT     тФВ  DOMAIN     тФВ
                    тФВ(interface)тФВ  (pure Java тФВ
                    тФВ           тФВ  no imports)тФВ
                    тФВ  OUTPUT   тФВ             тФВ
                    тФВ  PORT     тФВ             тФВ
                    тФВ(interface)тФЬтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФд
                    тФВ           тФВ  Output     тФВтФАтФАтЦ║ PostgreSQL
                    тФВ           тФВ  Adapter    тФВтФАтФАтЦ║ Kafka
                    тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШтФАтФАтЦ║ Redis
```

```java
// === DOMAIN (center тАФ zero framework dependencies) ===
public class Order {
    private OrderId id;
    private CustomerId customerId;
    private List<OrderLine> lines;
    private OrderStatus status;

    // Rich domain model тАФ behavior lives here
    public void confirm() {
        if (this.status != OrderStatus.PENDING) {
            throw new InvalidOrderStateException("Can only confirm pending orders");
        }
        this.status = OrderStatus.CONFIRMED;
        DomainEvents.raise(new OrderConfirmed(this.id));
    }
}

// === INPUT PORT (driving side interface) ===
public interface OrderUseCase {
    OrderId createOrder(CreateOrderCommand command);
    void confirmOrder(OrderId orderId);
}

// === OUTPUT PORT (driven side interface тАФ defined by domain) ===
public interface OrderRepository {           // Domain defines this interface
    void save(Order order);
    Optional<Order> findById(OrderId id);
}

public interface PaymentGateway {
    PaymentResult charge(PaymentRequest request);
}

// === DOMAIN SERVICE (implements input port) ===
@UseCase                                     // custom annotation, not @Service
public class OrderApplicationService implements OrderUseCase {
    private final OrderRepository orderRepository;      // OUTPUT PORT
    private final PaymentGateway paymentGateway;        // OUTPUT PORT
    private final InventoryPort inventoryPort;           // OUTPUT PORT

    @Override
    public OrderId createOrder(CreateOrderCommand command) {
        // Pure business logic тАФ no framework, no infrastructure
        Order order = Order.create(command);
        inventoryPort.reserve(order.getLines());
        orderRepository.save(order);
        return order.getId();
    }
}

// === INPUT ADAPTER (driving side тАФ framework lives here) ===
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/orders")
public class OrderRestAdapter {
    private final OrderUseCase orderUseCase;    // DEPENDS ON PORT, not implementation

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<String> create(@RequestBody CreateOrderRequest request) {
        OrderId id = orderUseCase.createOrder(request.toCommand());
        return ResponseEntity.status(201).body(id.value());
    }
}

// === OUTPUT ADAPTER (driven side тАФ JPA implementation) ===
@Repository
public class JpaOrderRepository implements OrderRepository {  // IMPLEMENTS DOMAIN PORT
    private final JpaOrderEntityRepository jpaRepository;     // Spring Data JPA

    @Override
    public void save(Order order) {
        jpaRepository.save(OrderEntity.from(order));
    }

    @Override
    public Optional<Order> findById(OrderId id) {
        return jpaRepository.findById(id.value()).map(OrderEntity::toDomain);
    }
}
```

**Key Details:**
- Domain has ZERO dependencies on frameworks (no Spring annotations, no JPA, no Jackson)
- Easy to test domain in isolation тАФ mock the ports, no Spring context needed
- Swap adapters without touching domain тАФ replace PostgreSQL with MongoDB by implementing new adapter
- Testability: `OrderApplicationService` can be tested with in-memory `OrderRepository` stub
- The inside (domain) defines the output port interfaces тАФ dependency inversion at the architectural level

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Putting framework annotations inside domain classes
- Creating "ports" for everything тАФ adds overhead, use judgment (simple CRUD may not need it)
- Mapper explosion: domain тЖФ JPA entity тЖФ DTO mappings add boilerplate; use MapStruct



### 3.4 Clean Architecture

**Definition:** Robert Martin's refinement of Hexagonal. Four concentric layers with the Dependency Rule: source code dependencies can only point inward.

```
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ  FRAMEWORKS & DRIVERS (outermost)                           тФВ
тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  INTERFACE ADAPTERS                                  тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР   тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  тФВ  APPLICATION BUSINESS RULES (Use Cases)      тФВ   тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР  тФВ   тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  тФВ  тФВ  ENTERPRISE BUSINESS RULES (Entities)  тФВ  тФВ   тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  тФВ  тФВ  (most stable, most protected)         тФВ  тФВ   тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ  тФВ   тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ   тФВ   тФВ
тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ   тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
         Dependencies always point INWARD тЖТ
```

**Layer responsibilities:**
| Layer | Contents | Changes when |
|-|-|-|
| Entities | Domain objects, business rules | Business rules change (rare) |
| Use Cases | Application-specific logic | Application behavior changes |
| Interface Adapters | Controllers, Presenters, Gateways | UI/DB technology changes |
| Frameworks & Drivers | Spring, JPA, HTTP, DB | Framework versions change |

**Key difference from Hexagonal:** Clean Architecture explicitly separates use-case layer from entity layer, and mandates the Dependency Rule formally. In practice, they're very similar тАФ Hexagonal is the pattern, Clean Architecture is a formalization.

**Package structure:**
```
com.example.orders/
тФЬтФАтФА domain/                          # Entities layer
тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА model/Order.java
тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА model/OrderLine.java
тФВ   тФФтФАтФА event/OrderConfirmed.java
тФЬтФАтФА application/                     # Use Cases layer
тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА port/in/CreateOrderUseCase.java
тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА port/out/OrderRepository.java
тФВ   тФФтФАтФА service/OrderService.java
тФЬтФАтФА adapter/                         # Interface Adapters layer
тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА in/web/OrderController.java
тФВ   тФЬтФАтФА in/messaging/OrderEventConsumer.java
тФВ   тФФтФАтФА out/persistence/JpaOrderRepository.java
тФФтФАтФА infrastructure/                  # Frameworks & Drivers layer
    тФЬтФАтФА config/PersistenceConfig.java
    тФФтФАтФА config/SecurityConfig.java
```



### 3.5 Microservices Architecture

**Definition:** Application decomposed into small, independently deployable services, each owning its data and communicating over the network.

```
                        тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
Clients тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║ тФВ API Gateway  тФВ
                        тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
              тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
              тЦ╝                 тЦ╝                  тЦ╝
    тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
    тФВ  Order Service  тФВ тФВ  Inventory  тФВ  тФВ Payment Service  тФВ
    тФВ  (Spring Boot)  тФВ тФВ  Service    тФВ  тФВ  (Spring Boot)   тФВ
    тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР  тФВ тФВ             тФВ  тФВ                  тФВ
    тФВ  тФВ Orders DB тФВ  тФВ тФВ Inventory DBтФВ  тФВ  Payments DB     тФВ
    тФВ  тФВ (Postgres)тФВ  тФВ тФВ  (MySQL)    тФВ  тФВ  (Postgres)      тФВ
    тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ  тФВ тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
    тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
```

**Decomposition strategies:**
- **By business capability** (recommended): Order Service, Inventory Service, Payment Service
- **By subdomain (DDD)**: aligns with bounded contexts
- **By volatility**: separate services that change at different rates
- **Strangler Fig**: incrementally extract services from a monolith

```java
// Service-to-service communication тАФ resilient REST client
@Service
public class InventoryClient {

    private final WebClient webClient;
    private final CircuitBreakerRegistry circuitBreakerRegistry;

    public boolean reserve(UUID productId, int quantity) {
        CircuitBreaker cb = circuitBreakerRegistry.circuitBreaker("inventory-service");

        return cb.executeSupplier(() ->
            webClient.post()
                .uri("/api/inventory/reserve")
                .bodyValue(new ReservationRequest(productId, quantity))
                .retrieve()
                .onStatus(HttpStatusCode::is5xxServerError, response ->
                    Mono.error(new InventoryServiceException("Inventory service error")))
                .bodyToMono(ReservationResponse.class)
                .timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(3))
                .map(ReservationResponse::success)
                .block()
        );
    }
}
```

**Data isolation patterns:**
| Pattern | Description | Trade-off |
||-|--|
| Database per service | Each service owns its DB | No joins, eventual consistency |
| Schema per service | Separate schemas in shared DB | Easier ops, less isolation |
| Shared database | Anti-pattern (tight coupling) | Avoid тАФ defeats the purpose |

**Service communication:**
| Type | Technology | Use case |
||--|-|
| Sync request/response | REST, gRPC | Real-time queries |
| Async messaging | Kafka, RabbitMQ | Commands, events |
| Service mesh | Istio, Linkerd | Cross-cutting concerns |

**Common Pitfalls:**
- **Distributed monolith**: services that must be deployed together or are tightly coupled via sync calls тАФ worst of both worlds
- **Chatty services**: 10 sync calls for one business operation = distributed performance nightmare
- **Premature decomposition**: splitting before understanding domain boundaries = wrong boundaries = massive refactoring
- **No contract testing**: Pact or Spring Cloud Contract essential тАФ consumer-driven contract testing prevents breaking changes



### 3.6 Event-Driven Architecture (EDA)

**Definition:** Components communicate by emitting and reacting to events. Producers emit events without knowing consumers. Consumers react independently.

```
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР      event       тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ Order       тФВтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║ тФВ  Event Broker   тФВ
тФВ Service     тФВ  OrderPlaced     тФВ  (Kafka)        тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ                  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФмтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
                                          тФВ
                    тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
                    тЦ╝                     тЦ╝                    тЦ╝
           тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР      тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР   тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
           тФВ Inventory   тФВ      тФВ Payment Service  тФВ   тФВNotification  тФВ
           тФВ Service     тФВ      тФВ                 тФВ   тФВService       тФВ
           тФВ (reserves)  тФВ      тФВ (charges card)  тФВ   тФВ(sends email) тФВ
           тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ      тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ   тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
```

**Event types:**
- **Domain Event**: something that happened in the domain (`OrderPlaced`, `PaymentProcessed`)
- **Event Notification**: thin event, consumers query for details
- **Event-Carried State Transfer**: fat event, contains all necessary data (preferred тАФ avoids query-back)
- **Event Sourcing**: events ARE the source of truth (not just notifications)

```java
// Domain event definition
public record OrderPlaced(
    UUID orderId,
    UUID customerId,
    List<OrderLineDTO> lines,
    Money totalAmount,
    Instant occurredAt
) implements DomainEvent {
    public String topic() { return "orders.placed"; }
}

// Event producer (Kafka)
@Service
public class OrderEventPublisher {

    private final KafkaTemplate<String, Object> kafkaTemplate;

    public void publish(OrderPlaced event) {
        kafkaTemplate.send(
            event.topic(),
            event.orderId().toString(),    // partition key тАФ all events for same order тЖТ same partition
            event
        ).whenComplete((result, ex) -> {
            if (ex != null) {
                log.error("Failed to publish event orderId={}", event.orderId(), ex);
                // Outbox pattern handles this тАФ see below
            }
        });
    }
}

// Event consumer
@Component
public class InventoryEventConsumer {

    @KafkaListener(
        topics = "orders.placed",
        groupId = "inventory-service",
        containerFactory = "kafkaListenerContainerFactory"
    )
    public void handle(OrderPlaced event, Acknowledgment ack) {
        try {
            inventoryService.reserve(event.orderId(), event.lines());
            ack.acknowledge();   // manual ack only after successful processing
        } catch (InsufficientStockException e) {
            // publish OrderReservationFailed тАФ compensating event
            eventPublisher.publish(new OrderReservationFailed(event.orderId(), e.getMessage()));
            ack.acknowledge();   // still ack тАФ don't retry inventory failures
        } catch (TransientException e) {
            // don't ack тАФ let Kafka retry
            log.warn("Transient error, will retry: {}", e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Transactional Outbox Pattern тАФ guaranteed event delivery:**
```java
// Problem: save order + publish event must be atomic
// Solution: write event to outbox table in same DB transaction

@Transactional
public void createOrder(CreateOrderCommand command) {
    Order order = Order.create(command);
    orderRepository.save(order);

    // Write to outbox IN SAME TRANSACTION тАФ never lost
    outboxRepository.save(new OutboxEvent(
        UUID.randomUUID(),
        "orders.placed",
        order.getId().toString(),
        serialize(new OrderPlaced(order))
    ));
    // Separate process (CDC/polling) reads outbox тЖТ publishes to Kafka
}
```

**CDC (Change Data Capture) with Debezium:**
```
PostgreSQL WAL тФАтФАтЦ║ Debezium тФАтФАтЦ║ Kafka (outbox.events topic) тФАтФАтЦ║ consumers
                  (reads changes                               (receive events)
                   from DB log)
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- **Dual write problem**: writing to DB and Kafka without outbox тЖТ events can be lost on crash
- **Event schema evolution**: consumers break when producers add/remove fields тАФ use Avro + Schema Registry or design for backward/forward compatibility
- **Consumer idempotency**: Kafka delivers at-least-once тАФ consumers must handle duplicates (idempotency key)



### 3.7 Reactive Architecture

**Definition:** Architecture built around reactive streams тАФ asynchronous, non-blocking, back-pressure-aware data flows.

**Reactive Manifesto pillars:** Responsive, Resilient, Elastic, Message-Driven

```
Traditional (thread-per-request):
Request тФАтФАтЦ║ Thread (blocked on DB) тФАтФАтЦ║ Response
            тФФтФАтФА Thread is idle, wasting memory

Reactive (event-loop):
Request тФАтФАтЦ║ Event Loop тФАтФАтЦ║ (async DB call) тФАтФАтЦ║ callback тФАтФАтЦ║ Response
            тФФтФАтФА Event loop handles other requests while waiting
```

```java
// Spring WebFlux тАФ reactive REST endpoint
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/orders")
public class ReactiveOrderController {

    private final ReactiveOrderService orderService;

    @GetMapping("/{orderId}")
    public Mono<OrderResponse> getOrder(@PathVariable UUID orderId) {
        return orderService.findById(orderId)
            .map(OrderResponse::from)
            .switchIfEmpty(Mono.error(new OrderNotFoundException(orderId)));
    }

    @GetMapping("/customer/{customerId}")
    public Flux<OrderResponse> getCustomerOrders(@PathVariable UUID customerId) {
        return orderService.findByCustomer(customerId)
            .map(OrderResponse::from);
    }

    // Reactive composition тАФ parallel calls
    @GetMapping("/{orderId}/enriched")
    public Mono<EnrichedOrderResponse> getEnrichedOrder(@PathVariable UUID orderId) {
        return Mono.zip(
            orderService.findById(orderId),
            customerService.findById(orderId).defaultIfEmpty(Customer.unknown()),
            inventoryService.getStatus(orderId)
        ).map(tuple -> EnrichedOrderResponse.from(
            tuple.getT1(), tuple.getT2(), tuple.getT3()
        ));
    }
}

// Back-pressure example
@Service
public class OrderExportService {

    public Flux<Order> exportOrders(LocalDate from, LocalDate to) {
        return orderRepository.findByDateRange(from, to)  // returns Flux
            .buffer(100)                                   // batch 100 orders
            .flatMap(batch -> exportService.write(batch), 4)  // 4 concurrent exports
            .onBackpressureBuffer(1000,                   // buffer up to 1000 batches
                dropped -> log.warn("Backpressure: dropped batch"))
            .doOnError(e -> log.error("Export failed", e))
            .retryWhen(Retry.backoff(3, Duration.ofSeconds(1)));
    }
}
```

**R2DBC тАФ reactive database access:**
```java
// Reactive JPA doesn't exist тАФ use R2DBC for truly reactive DB access
public interface ReactiveOrderRepository extends ReactiveCrudRepository<OrderEntity, UUID> {
    Flux<OrderEntity> findByCustomerId(UUID customerId);

    @Query("SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = :status AND created_at > :since")
    Flux<OrderEntity> findRecentByStatus(String status, LocalDateTime since);
}
```

**Key Details:**
- Reactive is NOT always faster тАФ it's about resource efficiency at scale
- Best for: I/O-bound operations, high concurrency, streaming
- Not good for: CPU-intensive work (blocking the event loop is catastrophic)
- Learning curve is steep тАФ debugging reactive pipelines requires discipline
- `block()` in a reactive context is a code smell and can cause deadlocks

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Blocking inside reactive pipeline: `webClient.get()...block()` inside a `Flux.flatMap()`
- Not handling back-pressure тАФ unbounded queues lead to OOM
- Over-using reactive for simple CRUD тАФ adds complexity without benefit



### 3.8 Domain-Driven Design (DDD)

**Definition:** Software design approach focused on modeling the domain (business problem) as the primary driver of technical design.

**Strategic DDD:**
```
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ                    DOMAIN                                       тФВ
тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР    тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР                  тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  Order Context   тФВ    тФВ  Catalog Context  тФВ                  тФВ
тФВ  тФВ                  тФВтЧДтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ                  тФВ  (via ACL)       тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  Order           тФВ    тФВ  Product         тФВ                  тФВ
тФВ  тФВ  Customer (thin) тФВ    тФВ  Customer (rich) тФВ тЖР same word,     тФВ
тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ    тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ   different meaning
тФВ                                                                 тФВ
тФВ  тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР    тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР                  тФВ
тФВ  тФВ Payment Context  тФВ    тФВ Shipping Context  тФВ                  тФВ
тФВ  тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ    тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ                  тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
```

**Bounded Context:** a linguistic boundary where a term has a single, unambiguous meaning. "Customer" in Order Context = buyer; in Loyalty Context = points-holder. Don't share the model across contexts.

**Context Map relationships:**
| Relationship | Description |
|-|-|
| Partnership | Two contexts evolve together |
| Shared Kernel | Shared subset of domain model |
| Customer-Supplier | Downstream context is customer of upstream |
| Conformist | Downstream conforms to upstream model |
| ACL (Anti-Corruption Layer) | Translates between models |
| Open Host Service | Upstream publishes stable protocol |
| Published Language | Shared language (Avro schema, Protobuf) |

**Tactical DDD building blocks:**
```java
// VALUE OBJECT тАФ equality by value, immutable
public record Money(BigDecimal amount, Currency currency) {
    public Money {
        Objects.requireNonNull(amount);
        Objects.requireNonNull(currency);
        if (amount.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) < 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Money cannot be negative");
        }
    }

    public Money add(Money other) {
        if (!this.currency.equals(other.currency)) {
            throw new CurrencyMismatchException();
        }
        return new Money(this.amount.add(other.amount), this.currency);
    }
}

// ENTITY тАФ equality by identity, mutable
public class Order {
    private final OrderId id;          // strongly-typed ID
    private OrderStatus status;
    private List<OrderLine> lines;

    // Business behavior on the entity
    public void addLine(Product product, int quantity) {
        if (status != OrderStatus.DRAFT) {
            throw new OrderNotEditableException(id);
        }
        lines.add(new OrderLine(product.getId(), product.getPrice(), quantity));
    }
}

// AGGREGATE тАФ consistency boundary, accessed via root
public class Order {  // AGGREGATE ROOT
    private OrderId id;
    private List<OrderLine> lines;   // OrderLine only accessed through Order

    // Invariant: total lines <= 50
    public void addLine(OrderLine line) {
        if (lines.size() >= 50) {
            throw new OrderLineLimitExceededException();
        }
        lines.add(line);
    }
}

// REPOSITORY тАФ collection-like interface for aggregates
public interface OrderRepository {
    void save(Order order);
    Optional<Order> findById(OrderId id);
    // Never: List<OrderLine> findLinesByProductId(ProductId id) тАФ bypass aggregate
}

// DOMAIN SERVICE тАФ operations spanning multiple aggregates
@DomainService
public class OrderTransferService {
    public void transfer(Order from, Order to, List<OrderLineId> lineIds) {
        List<OrderLine> lines = from.removeLines(lineIds);
        to.addLines(lines);
        // Both aggregates modified тАФ cannot belong to either; hence domain service
    }
}
```

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Anemic domain model: all logic in services, entities are just getters/setters
- Large aggregates: loading 1000 order lines for every operation
- Exposing aggregate internals: `order.getLines().add(line)` тАФ bypasses invariants



### 3.9 CQRS Architecture

**Definition:** Command Query Responsibility Segregation тАФ split the write model (commands) from the read model (queries). Different models optimized for different purposes.

```
                    WRITE SIDE                    READ SIDE
                  (Commands)                    (Queries)
                      тФВ                              тФВ
 CreateOrder тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ                              тФВтЧДтФАтФАтФАтФА GetOrdersByCustomer
 UpdateOrder тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ  Domain Model                тФВтЧДтФАтФАтФАтФА GetOrderDashboard
 CancelOrder тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ  (normalized, consistent)    тФВ
                      тФВ                              тФВ
                      тФВтФАтФАтФАтФА events тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ  Read Models
                      тФВ   (OrderCreated,             тФВ  (denormalized,
                      тФВ    OrderUpdated)             тФВ   optimized views)
                      тФВ                              тФВ
                  Write DB                       Read DB
                 (PostgreSQL)                   (Elasticsearch /
                                                Redis / MongoDB)
```

```java
// COMMAND side
public record CreateOrderCommand(UUID customerId, List<OrderLineRequest> lines) {}

@CommandHandler
@Service
public class OrderCommandService {

    private final OrderRepository orderRepository;
    private final ApplicationEventPublisher eventPublisher;

    @Transactional
    public UUID handle(CreateOrderCommand command) {
        Order order = Order.create(command.customerId(), command.lines());
        orderRepository.save(order);
        eventPublisher.publishEvent(new OrderCreatedEvent(order));
        return order.getId().value();
    }
}

// QUERY side тАФ separate, optimized read model
@Document(indexName = "orders")   // Elasticsearch
public class OrderSummaryDocument {
    private String orderId;
    private String customerName;   // denormalized from Customer
    private String customerEmail;  // no join needed
    private int lineCount;
    private BigDecimal totalAmount;
    private String status;
    private Instant createdAt;
}

@QueryHandler
@Service
public class OrderQueryService {

    private final OrderSearchRepository searchRepository; // Elasticsearch repo

    public Page<OrderSummaryDocument> findByCustomer(UUID customerId, Pageable pageable) {
        return searchRepository.findByCustomerId(customerId.toString(), pageable);
    }

    public OrderDashboard getDashboard(LocalDate from, LocalDate to) {
        return searchRepository.aggregateDashboard(from, to);  // Elasticsearch aggregation
    }
}

// EVENT HANDLER тАФ synchronizes write model тЖТ read model
@Component
public class OrderProjection {

    private final OrderSummaryRepository readRepository;
    private final CustomerQueryClient customerClient;

    @EventListener
    @Async
    public void on(OrderCreatedEvent event) {
        Order order = event.getOrder();
        Customer customer = customerClient.findById(order.getCustomerId());

        OrderSummaryDocument doc = new OrderSummaryDocument();
        doc.setOrderId(order.getId().toString());
        doc.setCustomerName(customer.getFullName());    // denormalized
        doc.setCustomerEmail(customer.getEmail());      // denormalized
        doc.setLineCount(order.getLines().size());
        doc.setTotalAmount(order.getTotalAmount().getAmount());
        doc.setStatus(order.getStatus().name());
        doc.setCreatedAt(order.getCreatedAt());

        readRepository.save(doc);
    }
}
```

**Key Details:**
- Read and write models can use different databases
- Read model is eventually consistent (acceptable for most query scenarios)
- Commands return IDs or void тАФ never return data (that's a query's job)
- Significant complexity overhead тАФ use only when read/write performance profiles differ dramatically
- CQRS + Event Sourcing is a natural pairing but CQRS does NOT require Event Sourcing

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Using CQRS for simple CRUD тАФ massive over-engineering
- Forgetting that read models are eventually consistent тАФ UI must handle this (optimistic updates)
- Commands that return rich data тАФ blurs the boundary



### 3.10 Saga Pattern

**Definition:** Manage distributed transactions across multiple services through a sequence of local transactions and compensating transactions for rollback.

**Two implementations:**

#### Choreography-based Saga
```
Order Service        Inventory Service      Payment Service
     тФВ                      тФВ                    тФВ
     тФВтФАтФАтФА OrderCreated тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ                   тФВ
     тФВ                      тФВтФАтФАтФА StockReservedтФАтФАтЦ║тФВ
     тФВ                      тФВ                    тФВтФАтФАтФАтФА PaymentProcessed
     тФВтЧДтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФВ
(OrderConfirmed)
     тФВ
     тФВ   IF PAYMENT FAILS:
     тФВтЧДтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА PaymentFailed тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФВ
     тФВтФАтФАтФА OrderCancelled тФАтЦ║тФВ
                           тФВ (StockReleased тАФ compensating transaction)
```

```java
// Choreography: each service listens to events and reacts
@Component
public class InventoryService {

    @KafkaListener(topics = "orders.created")
    public void onOrderCreated(OrderCreatedEvent event) {
        try {
            reserve(event.orderId(), event.lines());
            eventPublisher.publish(new StockReservedEvent(event.orderId()));
        } catch (InsufficientStockException e) {
            eventPublisher.publish(new StockReservationFailedEvent(event.orderId(), e.getReason()));
        }
    }

    @KafkaListener(topics = "payments.failed")
    public void onPaymentFailed(PaymentFailedEvent event) {
        // COMPENSATING TRANSACTION
        release(event.orderId());
        eventPublisher.publish(new StockReleasedEvent(event.orderId()));
    }
}
```

#### Orchestration-based Saga
```
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ                   SAGA ORCHESTRATOR                     тФВ
тФВ                   (Order Saga)                          тФВ
тФВ                                                         тФВ
тФВ  step 1: ReserveStock тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║   тФВ
тФВ          тЧДтФАтФА StockReserved or StockReservationFailed     тФВ
тФВ  step 2: ProcessPayment тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║    тФВ
тФВ          тЧДтФАтФА PaymentProcessed or PaymentFailed          тФВ
тФВ  step 3: ConfirmOrder тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║    тФВ
тФВ                                                         тФВ
тФВ  On failure:                                            тФВ
тФВ  compensate: ReleaseStock тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║    тФВ
тФВ  compensate: RefundPayment тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║    тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
```

```java
// Orchestration with Axon Framework (or custom state machine)
@Saga
public class OrderFulfillmentSaga {

    @Autowired
    private transient CommandGateway commandGateway;

    private UUID orderId;
    private UUID inventoryReservationId;

    @StartSaga
    @SagaEventHandler(associationProperty = "orderId")
    public void on(OrderCreatedEvent event) {
        this.orderId = event.orderId();
        commandGateway.send(new ReserveStockCommand(event.orderId(), event.lines()));
    }

    @SagaEventHandler(associationProperty = "orderId")
    public void on(StockReservedEvent event) {
        this.inventoryReservationId = event.reservationId();
        commandGateway.send(new ProcessPaymentCommand(orderId, event.totalAmount()));
    }

    @SagaEventHandler(associationProperty = "orderId")
    public void on(PaymentFailedEvent event) {
        // COMPENSATE
        commandGateway.send(new ReleaseStockCommand(inventoryReservationId));
        commandGateway.send(new CancelOrderCommand(orderId, event.reason()));
        SagaLifecycle.end();
    }

    @EndSaga
    @SagaEventHandler(associationProperty = "orderId")
    public void on(OrderConfirmedEvent event) {
        // Happy path complete тАФ saga ends
    }
}
```

**Choreography vs Orchestration:**
| Aspect | Choreography | Orchestration |
|--|-||
| Coupling | Low (event-based) | Medium (orchestrator knows steps) |
| Visibility | Hard тАФ flow is implicit | Easy тАФ flow is explicit |
| Debugging | Complex | Simpler |
| Single point of failure | No | Orchestrator (mitigated by persistence) |
| Best for | Simple, stable flows | Complex, many-step flows |

**Common Pitfalls:**
- No idempotency in saga steps тАФ Kafka delivers at-least-once, compensating transactions may run twice
- Saga state not persisted тАФ orchestrator crash loses saga state (use event-sourced saga state)
- No timeout handling тАФ saga can hang indefinitely if a service never responds



### 3.11 Serverless Java Architecture

**Definition:** Deploy functions (not services) that are executed on-demand. Infrastructure managed by cloud provider.

```
HTTP Request тФАтФАтЦ║ API Gateway тФАтФАтЦ║ Lambda Function (Java)
                                        тФВ
                                        тФЬтФАтФАтЦ║ DynamoDB
                                        тФЬтФАтФАтЦ║ SQS
                                        тФФтФАтФАтЦ║ S3
```

**Java Cold Start Problem:**
```
Cold start breakdown:
JVM startup:          ~300ms
Spring Boot init:     ~2000ms  тЖР the killer
Handler execution:    ~50ms
Total cold start:     ~2350ms  тЖР unacceptable for many use cases
```

**Solutions:**
```java
// 1. GraalVM Native Image (Spring Native / Quarkus / Micronaut)
// Compile to native binary тАФ cold start <100ms, smaller memory
// quarkus-maven-plugin for native compilation:
// mvn package -Pnative

// 2. Minimize Spring context with Spring Cloud Function
@SpringBootApplication
public class OrderFunctionApp {
    // Minimal context тАФ no web server, no full auto-configuration
    @Bean
    public Function<OrderRequest, OrderResponse> processOrder(OrderService service) {
        return request -> service.process(request);
    }
}

// 3. AWS Lambda SnapStart (Java 21)
// Snapshot JVM state after initialization, restore on invocation
// Add to SAM/CloudFormation:
// SnapStart:
//   ApplyOn: PublishedVersions
```

**Quarkus тАФ cloud-native Java:**
```java
@Path("/orders")
@ApplicationScoped
public class OrderResource {

    @Inject
    OrderService orderService;

    @POST
    @Transactional
    public Response createOrder(CreateOrderRequest request) {
        Order order = orderService.create(request);
        return Response.status(201).entity(order).build();
    }
}
// quarkus dev тАФ live reload in <100ms
// quarkus build --native тАФ native binary, cold start ~10ms
```

**Key Details:**
- Function should be stateless тАФ no in-memory state between invocations
- Concurrency model: each Lambda invocation is isolated (no shared state between concurrent invocations)
- Pricing: pay per invocation + duration (100ms increments)
- Not suited for: long-running operations, CPU-intensive tasks, WebSocket



### 3.12 Cloud-Native Java Design

**12-Factor App principles for Java:**
| Factor | Java Implementation |
|--|-|
| Codebase | One repo, multiple deploys via env vars |
| Dependencies | Maven/Gradle тАФ no system-level dependencies |
| Config | Environment variables / Kubernetes ConfigMaps |
| Backing services | Injected via env vars тАФ DB URL, Redis URL |
| Build/Release/Run | Docker multi-stage build, separate stages |
| Processes | Stateless тАФ session in Redis, not in-memory |
| Port binding | Embedded Tomcat тАФ app IS the server |
| Concurrency | Scale via more pods, not bigger pods |
| Disposability | Fast startup, graceful shutdown |
| Dev/Prod parity | Docker тАФ same image in all environments |
| Logs | Write to stdout тАФ log aggregation handles the rest |
| Admin processes | One-off jobs via Kubernetes Jobs |

```yaml
# Dockerfile тАФ multi-stage build
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY pom.xml .
RUN mvn dependency:go-offline    # cache deps layer
COPY src ./src
RUN mvn package -DskipTests

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine
WORKDIR /app
# Non-root user for security
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
USER appuser

COPY --from=builder /app/target/*.jar app.jar

# Tune JVM for containers
ENV JAVA_OPTS="-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 \
               -XX:+UseContainerSupport \
               -XX:+ExitOnOutOfMemoryError \
               -Djava.security.egd=file:/dev/./urandom"

EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["sh", "-c", "java $JAVA_OPTS -jar app.jar"]
```

```yaml
# application.yml тАФ externalized config
spring:
  datasource:
    url: ${DATABASE_URL}
    username: ${DATABASE_USERNAME}
    password: ${DATABASE_PASSWORD}
  kafka:
    bootstrap-servers: ${KAFKA_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS}
  redis:
    host: ${REDIS_HOST}

# Graceful shutdown
server:
  shutdown: graceful
spring:
  lifecycle:
    timeout-per-shutdown-phase: 30s
```

**JVM container tuning:**
```bash
# Modern JVM (11+) is container-aware тАФ use these flags:
-XX:+UseContainerSupport           # Use cgroup limits (default in JDK 11+)
-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0          # Use 75% of container memory for heap
-XX:InitialRAMPercentage=50.0      # Start heap at 50%
-XX:+ExitOnOutOfMemoryError        # Let K8s restart on OOM (cleaner than zombie)
-XX:+UseG1GC                       # G1 for most workloads
-XX:+UseZGC                        # ZGC for low-latency (JDK 15+, <1ms pauses)
```



### 3.13 Resilience Architecture

**Definition:** Ability of a system to maintain acceptable behavior in the presence of failures.

**Resilience4j тАФ the standard Java resilience library:**

```java
// application.yml тАФ Resilience4j config
resilience4j:
  circuitbreaker:
    instances:
      payment-service:
        registerHealthIndicator: true
        slidingWindowType: COUNT_BASED
        slidingWindowSize: 20
        failureRateThreshold: 50       # Open circuit at 50% failures
        waitDurationInOpenState: 30s   # Wait 30s before trying again
        permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState: 5
        minimumNumberOfCalls: 10
  retry:
    instances:
      payment-service:
        maxAttempts: 3
        waitDuration: 500ms
        exponentialBackoffMultiplier: 2
        retryExceptions:
          - java.net.ConnectException
          - java.util.concurrent.TimeoutException
        ignoreExceptions:
          - com.example.PaymentDeclinedException  # Don't retry business errors
  bulkhead:
    instances:
      payment-service:
        maxConcurrentCalls: 20   # Max concurrent calls to payment service
        maxWaitDuration: 100ms
  timelimiter:
    instances:
      payment-service:
        timeoutDuration: 3s
```

```java
@Service
public class PaymentClient {

    private final WebClient webClient;

    @CircuitBreaker(name = "payment-service", fallbackMethod = "paymentFallback")
    @Retry(name = "payment-service")
    @Bulkhead(name = "payment-service")
    @TimeLimiter(name = "payment-service")
    public CompletableFuture<PaymentResult> charge(PaymentRequest request) {
        return webClient.post()
            .uri("/charge")
            .bodyValue(request)
            .retrieve()
            .bodyToMono(PaymentResult.class)
            .toFuture();
    }

    // Fallback тАФ called when circuit is open or all retries exhausted
    private CompletableFuture<PaymentResult> paymentFallback(
            PaymentRequest request, Throwable ex) {
        log.warn("Payment service unavailable, queuing for retry: {}", ex.getMessage());
        // Put in persistent retry queue for async processing
        retryQueue.enqueue(request);
        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(PaymentResult.pending());
    }
}
```

**Resilience patterns summary:**
| Pattern | Problem solved | Trade-off |
|||--|
| Circuit Breaker | Cascading failures | Fail-fast; need fallback |
| Retry | Transient failures | Risk retry storms |
| Bulkhead | Resource exhaustion from one consumer | Wasted capacity |
| Timeout | Slow responses holding threads | False timeouts under load |
| Rate Limiter | Overloading downstream | Legitimate requests rejected |
| Fallback | Service unavailable | Data may be stale/incomplete |

**Retry storm prevention:**
```java
// Exponential backoff + jitter prevents thundering herd
RetryConfig config = RetryConfig.custom()
    .maxAttempts(5)
    .intervalFunction(IntervalFunction.ofExponentialRandomBackoff(
        Duration.ofMillis(100),  // initial interval
        2.0,                      // multiplier
        Duration.ofSeconds(10)   // max interval
    ))
    .build();
```



### 3.14 Scalability Patterns

**Horizontal vs Vertical scaling:**
```
Vertical (scale up):   1 server ├Ч 64 cores, 256GB RAM
Horizontal (scale out): 32 servers ├Ч 2 cores, 8GB RAM  тЖР preferred
```

**Stateless design for horizontal scaling:**
```java
// BAD тАФ in-memory session (breaks with multiple instances)
HttpSession session = request.getSession();
session.setAttribute("cart", cart);

// GOOD тАФ session in Redis (shared across all instances)
@Configuration
@EnableRedisHttpSession(maxInactiveIntervalInSeconds = 1800)
public class SessionConfig {}
// Spring Session automatically stores session in Redis
```

**Database scaling patterns:**
```
Read Replicas:
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ All WRITES тФАтФАтЦ║ Primary DB                          тФВ
тФВ All READS  тФАтФАтЦ║ Read Replica 1                      тФВ
тФВ             тФАтФАтЦ║ Read Replica 2   (round-robin)     тФВ
тФВ             тФАтФАтЦ║ Read Replica 3                     тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ

Sharding (horizontal partitioning):
тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
тФВ userId 0-33%   тФАтФАтЦ║ Shard 1                         тФВ
тФВ userId 34-66%  тФАтФАтЦ║ Shard 2                         тФВ
тФВ userId 67-100% тФАтФАтЦ║ Shard 3                         тФВ
тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
Shard key choice is critical тАФ avoid hot shards
```

```java
// Read/Write routing with Spring
@Configuration
public class DataSourceConfig {

    @Bean
    @Primary
    public DataSource routingDataSource(
            @Qualifier("primary") DataSource primary,
            @Qualifier("replica") DataSource replica) {
        return new AbstractRoutingDataSource() {
            @Override
            protected Object determineCurrentLookupKey() {
                return TransactionSynchronizationManager.isCurrentTransactionReadOnly()
                    ? "replica" : "primary";
            }
        };
    }
}

// Usage тАФ @Transactional(readOnly=true) routes to replica
@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public List<Order> findOrdersByCustomer(UUID customerId) {
    return orderRepository.findByCustomerId(customerId);
}
```

**Async processing for scalability:**
```java
// Instead of synchronous processing that ties up threads:
// Write тФАтФАтЦ║ DB тФАтФАтЦ║ synchronous downstream calls тФАтФАтЦ║ response

// Async: write fast, process later
@PostMapping("/orders")
public ResponseEntity<String> createOrder(@RequestBody CreateOrderRequest request) {
    String orderId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
    // Instant acknowledgement
    commandQueue.send(new CreateOrderCommand(orderId, request));
    return ResponseEntity.accepted()
        .header("Location", "/api/orders/" + orderId)
        .body(orderId);
}
```



### 3.15 Data Consistency Models

**The CAP Theorem:**
```
           Consistency
                тФВ
                тФВ   CA        CP
                тФВ
AvailabilityтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФА Partition Tolerance
                тФВ
                тФВ   AP
```
In a distributed system, network partitions WILL happen. So the real choice is CP vs AP.

**Consistency spectrum:**
```
Strong тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтЦ║ Eventual
(ACID)    Read-your-writes   Monotonic   Session   (BASE)
          consistency        reads       consistency

Postgres   Master reads      Replica     Sticky     Cassandra
           only              ordering    sessions   DynamoDB
```

**Concurrency control:**
```java
// Optimistic Locking (JPA) тАФ no DB lock, detect conflicts on commit
@Entity
public class Order {
    @Version
    private Long version;    // JPA increments on every update
    // ...
}

// If two threads read version=5 and both try to update:
// First commit: version 5 тЖТ 6, succeeds
// Second commit: expects version=5, finds version=6 тЖТ OptimisticLockException
// Retry logic required

// Pessimistic Locking тАФ DB-level row lock
@Repository
public interface OrderRepository extends JpaRepository<Order, UUID> {

    @Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
    @Query("SELECT o FROM Order o WHERE o.id = :id")
    Optional<Order> findByIdWithLock(@Param("id") UUID id);
}

// Idempotency тАФ safe to retry
@Transactional
public PaymentResult processPayment(UUID idempotencyKey, PaymentRequest request) {
    // Check if already processed
    return paymentResultRepository.findByIdempotencyKey(idempotencyKey)
        .orElseGet(() -> {
            PaymentResult result = doProcess(request);
            result.setIdempotencyKey(idempotencyKey);
            return paymentResultRepository.save(result);  // unique constraint on idempotencyKey
        });
}
```

**Two-phase commit (2PC) vs Saga:**
| | 2PC | Saga |
|-|--||
| Consistency | Strong (ACID) | Eventual |
| Availability | Lower (locks held) | Higher |
| Failure handling | Coordinator SPOF | Compensating transactions |
| Performance | Higher latency | Lower latency |
| Use case | Same DB, single service | Cross-service |



### 3.16 Caching Architecture

**Cache topology patterns:**
```
Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading) тАФ most common:
App тФАтФАтЦ║ Cache HIT? тФАтФАтЦ║ return cached value
           тФФтФАтФА MISS тФАтФАтЦ║ load from DB тФАтФАтЦ║ write to cache тФАтФАтЦ║ return

Write-Through:
App тФАтФАтЦ║ write to Cache AND DB synchronously

Write-Behind (Write-Back):
App тФАтФАтЦ║ write to Cache тФАтФАтЦ║ async write to DB (risk: cache loss = data loss)

Read-Through:
Cache loads from DB automatically on miss (cache is the primary interface)
```

```java
// Spring Cache abstraction тАФ vendor-neutral
@Service
public class ProductService {

    @Cacheable(
        value = "products",
        key = "#productId",
        condition = "#productId != null",
        unless = "#result == null"  // don't cache null (negative caching is separate concern)
    )
    public Product findById(UUID productId) {
        return productRepository.findById(productId)
            .orElseThrow(() -> new ProductNotFoundException(productId));
    }

    @CachePut(value = "products", key = "#product.id")
    public Product updateProduct(Product product) {
        return productRepository.save(product);
    }

    @CacheEvict(value = "products", key = "#productId")
    public void deleteProduct(UUID productId) {
        productRepository.deleteById(productId);
    }

    // Evict all entries in a cache
    @CacheEvict(value = "products", allEntries = true)
    @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 600_000)  // every 10 minutes
    public void evictProductCache() {}
}

// Caffeine тАФ in-process cache config
@Configuration
@EnableCaching
public class CacheConfig {

    @Bean
    public CacheManager cacheManager() {
        CaffeineCacheManager cacheManager = new CaffeineCacheManager();
        cacheManager.setCaffeine(Caffeine.newBuilder()
            .maximumSize(10_000)
            .expireAfterWrite(Duration.ofMinutes(10))
            .expireAfterAccess(Duration.ofMinutes(5))
            .recordStats()               // expose cache stats to Micrometer
        );
        return cacheManager;
    }
}
```

**Redis distributed cache:**
```java
// Redis with custom serialization
@Configuration
public class RedisConfig {

    @Bean
    public RedisCacheManager cacheManager(RedisConnectionFactory factory) {
        Jackson2JsonRedisSerializer<Object> serializer =
            new Jackson2JsonRedisSerializer<>(Object.class);

        RedisCacheConfiguration config = RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig()
            .entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(10))
            .serializeValuesWith(
                RedisSerializationContext.SerializationPair.fromSerializer(serializer))
            .disableCachingNullValues();

        return RedisCacheManager.builder(factory)
            .cacheDefaults(config)
            .withCacheConfiguration("products", config.entryTtl(Duration.ofHours(1)))
            .withCacheConfiguration("sessions", config.entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(30)))
            .build();
    }
}
```

**Cache stampede / thundering herd prevention:**
```java
// Problem: cache expires, 1000 concurrent requests all go to DB
// Solution 1: Probabilistic early expiration (refresh before expiry)
// Solution 2: Lock-based cache loading

private final Cache<UUID, Product> localCache = Caffeine.newBuilder()
    .maximumSize(1000)
    .build();

private final ConcurrentHashMap<UUID, CompletableFuture<Product>> loadingFutures
    = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

public Product findById(UUID id) {
    Product cached = localCache.getIfPresent(id);
    if (cached != null) return cached;

    // Only ONE thread loads from DB; others wait for the same future
    CompletableFuture<Product> future = loadingFutures.computeIfAbsent(id, key ->
        CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            Product product = productRepository.findById(key).orElseThrow();
            localCache.put(key, product);
            loadingFutures.remove(key);
            return product;
        })
    );

    return future.join();
}
```

**Cache consistency strategies:**
| Strategy | Consistency | Complexity |
|-|-|--|
| TTL-based expiry | Eventual | Low |
| Event-driven eviction | Near real-time | Medium |
| Write-through | Strong | Medium |
| Read-through | Eventual | Low |
| Cache-aside + events | Near real-time | Medium |

**Common Pitfalls:**
- Cache key collisions: `@Cacheable(key = "#id")` тАФ if two methods use same cache name and same ID type, they conflict. Use specific names.
- Serialization issues: complex objects with JPA lazy proxies can't be serialized to Redis
- Infinite TTL: stale data lives forever тАФ always set a TTL
- Caching mutable shared state: cache should store immutable snapshots



## 4. Interview Questions & Answers

**Q1: When would you choose a monolith over microservices?**

> Always start with a monolith unless you have proven reasons not to. Microservices require: a well-understood domain (wrong boundaries are expensive to fix), operational maturity (CI/CD, container orchestration, distributed tracing, service mesh), and team structure aligned with service ownership (Conway's Law). For startups, small teams (<10 engineers), or poorly-understood domains, a well-structured modular monolith with Java modules enforcing boundaries delivers faster development speed, simpler debugging, no distributed systems complexity, and easy refactoring. Extract services when you have concrete pain: an independent scaling need, separate deployment lifecycle, or team ownership boundary.



**Q2: What's the difference between Hexagonal and Clean Architecture?**

> They solve the same problem тАФ isolating the domain from infrastructure тАФ but at different levels of formality. Hexagonal defines the structural pattern: domain at center, ports as interfaces, adapters as implementations. Clean Architecture adds explicit layering (Entities, Use Cases, Interface Adapters, Frameworks) and formalizes the Dependency Rule: source code dependencies can only point inward. Hexagonal doesn't mandate separate layers for entities vs use cases. In practice, Clean Architecture is a refinement of Hexagonal тАФ you can implement Clean Architecture using Hexagonal structure. Both result in the same key benefit: domain is testable in isolation, infrastructure is swappable.



**Q3: Explain the Transactional Outbox pattern and why it's necessary.**

> The dual-write problem: you need to save an order to PostgreSQL AND publish an `OrderPlaced` event to Kafka. If you do them separately, you can save to DB but then crash before publishing тАФ or publish to Kafka but then DB rollback. Neither two-phase commit across DB + Kafka nor compensating transactions are practical. The Outbox pattern solves it: write the event to an `outbox` table in the same database transaction as the business entity. A separate process (CDC via Debezium, or a polling publisher) reads from the outbox table and publishes to Kafka, deleting processed rows. Now the DB transaction is the source of truth тАФ if the DB transaction commits, the event will eventually be published. If it rolls back, no event is written.



**Q4: How does CQRS improve system performance and what are its costs?**

> CQRS improves performance by allowing the read model to be optimized independently of the write model. Write side: normalized, ACID-consistent, optimized for transactional integrity. Read side: denormalized, pre-computed, stored in a technology optimized for querying (Elasticsearch for full-text search, Redis for low-latency lookups, MongoDB for flexible document queries). A complex dashboard query that would require 8 JOIN operations on the write model becomes a single document fetch on the read model. The costs are real: eventual consistency between write and read models (UI must handle this), significantly more code (two models, projection handlers, two databases), operational complexity of keeping read models synchronized, and debugging complexity when read models drift from write models.



**Q5: Explain the Saga pattern and when to choose choreography vs orchestration.**

> Sagas manage long-running distributed transactions without distributed locking, using sequences of local transactions with compensating transactions for rollback. Choreography: each service publishes events and reacts to others' events. No central coordinator. Works well for simple, stable flows with few steps тАФ but complex flows become impossible to reason about (implicit choreography = implicit bugs). Orchestration: a central saga orchestrator explicitly commands each step and handles failures. The flow is visible in code, easier to monitor and debug. Use orchestration for complex flows (>3 steps), flows requiring explicit timeout handling, or when you need clear visibility into saga state. Use choreography for simple flows where loose coupling and no single point of failure matter more than visibility.



**Q6: What is the difference between Reactive programming and asynchronous programming?**

> Async programming (CompletableFuture, @Async) means operations don't block the calling thread. Reactive programming is a superset: it's async + back-pressure. Back-pressure is the key differentiator тАФ it means the consumer can signal to the producer "slow down, I can't keep up," preventing unbounded queue growth and OOM. In Project Reactor, `Flux` and `Mono` are back-pressure-aware publishers. A `Flux` of database results won't overwhelm the consumer тАФ the consumer controls the rate. Additionally, reactive frameworks (WebFlux) use an event loop model (few threads, many concurrent requests) vs thread-per-request (many threads, each blocked). The tradeoff: reactive is significantly more complex to write, debug, and reason about тАФ only justified for I/O-bound high-concurrency scenarios.



**Q7: How do you handle eventual consistency in a CQRS system from a UX perspective?**

> Eventual consistency is the price of CQRS scalability. Strategies: (1) Optimistic UI updates: immediately update the local UI state after a command succeeds, without waiting for the read model to catch up тАФ most users never notice the lag. (2) Polling/WebSocket: after a command, poll the read endpoint until the expected change appears (works for short lags). (3) Return the created/updated resource from the command endpoint (pragmatic violation of pure CQRS тАФ acceptable in many systems). (4) Version tracking: include an expected version in queries; if the read model version is older than the command result version, wait or return a "processing" status. (5) Design flows where consistency matters to use synchronous reads from the write model immediately after writes.



**Q8: What is the CAP Theorem and how does it apply to database selection?**

> CAP states that a distributed system can provide at most two of: Consistency (every read sees the most recent write), Availability (every request gets a non-error response), and Partition Tolerance (the system continues operating despite network partitions). Since partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, the real choice is CP vs AP. PostgreSQL (CP): on partition, refuses writes to maintain consistency. Cassandra (AP): on partition, accepts writes on both sides (eventual consistency). DynamoDB: configurable per-operation (eventually consistent reads by default, strongly consistent optional). MongoDB (configurable). The practical implication: use CP databases for financial data (money must be consistent), AP databases for catalog/profile data where eventual consistency is acceptable and availability matters more.



**Q9: Explain Circuit Breaker states and what triggers each transition.**

> Three states: (1) Closed (normal): calls pass through, failures tracked in a sliding window. (2) Open: all calls fail immediately (fail-fast), no calls to downstream. Transition: failure rate exceeds threshold (e.g., 50% of last 20 calls failed). (3) Half-Open: allows a configured number of test calls through. Transition from Open: after the wait duration expires. Transition from Half-Open to Closed: if test calls succeed. Transition from Half-Open to Open: if test calls fail. The key insight: circuit breaker protects the system from cascading failures. When payment service is down, every order request waiting 30s before timing out ties up a thread. With a circuit breaker, order service fails-fast in milliseconds, frees threads, and gives payment service time to recover.



**Q10: How do you design for zero-downtime deployments in a Java microservice?**

> Several techniques: (1) Rolling deployment: deploy new version to one pod at a time, health checks ensure old version still serves traffic during rollout. (2) Blue-Green: two identical environments; switch traffic atomically; instant rollback. (3) Canary: route 5% of traffic to new version, monitor error rates, gradually increase. Technical requirements: backward-compatible API changes (never remove/rename fields in one deployment), database migrations must be backward-compatible (add columns with defaults, don't rename/drop until old version is gone тАФ Expand-Contract pattern), externalize configuration, graceful shutdown (drain in-flight requests before stopping), readiness probes (don't route traffic until app is ready). The critical mistake is a database migration that breaks the old version тАФ always decouple DB migration from code deployment.



**Q11: What patterns prevent cache stampede in high-traffic systems?**

> Cache stampede occurs when a popular cache entry expires and many concurrent requests all miss the cache simultaneously, flooding the database. Solutions: (1) Lock-based loading: only one thread/process loads the value; others wait тАФ use `ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent()` with a `CompletableFuture` value. (2) Probabilistic early expiration (XFetch algorithm): before expiry, randomly refresh based on how expensive the load is and how close to expiry. (3) Background refresh: a background thread pre-emptively refreshes before expiry, so the cache is never cold. (4) Request coalescing at the load balancer level (e.g., Varnish Grace mode). (5) Different TTLs across instances: add random jitter to TTL so entries don't expire simultaneously across 50 pods.



**Q12: How do you design a high-availability architecture for a Java service?**

> HA requires eliminating single points of failure at every layer: (1) Application: multiple instances behind a load balancer, stateless design (sessions in Redis, no local state). (2) Load balancer: AWS ALB is managed HA; if self-managed, use VRRP/floating IPs. (3) Database: primary + replicas with automatic failover (RDS Multi-AZ, PostgreSQL Patroni). (4) Cache: Redis Sentinel (automatic failover) or Redis Cluster (horizontal HA). (5) Messaging: Kafka with replication factor тЙе 3, min ISR тЙе 2. (6) Deployment: across multiple availability zones тАФ at least 2, ideally 3. (7) Circuit breakers and fallbacks for all downstream dependencies. (8) Health probes correctly configured (liveness тЙа readiness). (9) Graceful shutdown for in-flight request draining. Target: 99.9% (8.7 hours downtime/year) requires multiple nines at each layer since availability compounds multiplicatively.



**Q13: What is DDD's Anti-Corruption Layer and when do you need it?**

> The ACL is a translation layer that sits between your bounded context and an external context (legacy system, third-party API, another team's service) with a different domain model. Without ACL, you'd let the external model "pollute" your domain тАФ your `Order` would have fields like `legacyOrderCode` and `sapCustomerNumber`. With ACL, you translate at the boundary: external `SAPCustomer` тЖТ your `Customer` (only the fields you care about, in your ubiquitous language). Need ACL when: consuming a legacy system with a fundamentally different model; integrating with a third-party API that doesn't align with your domain; consuming an upstream bounded context that changes frequently (ACL shields your domain from their changes); or when the external model would pollute your clean domain language.



**Q14: Explain Event Sourcing and when it's worth the complexity.**

> Event Sourcing stores the sequence of events that caused the current state, not the current state itself. Instead of `UPDATE orders SET status='CONFIRMED'`, you append `OrderConfirmed{orderId, timestamp, confirmedBy}` to an event log. Current state is derived by replaying events. Benefits: complete audit trail (required for financial/healthcare systems); temporal queries ("what was the order state last Tuesday?"); replay events against new projections (add a new read model by replaying history); natural fit with CQRS and event-driven architecture. Costs: significant complexity тАФ event schema evolution is hard (immutable history), eventual consistency is mandatory, querying current state requires projection, tooling (Axon, EventStoreDB) has learning curve. Worth it for: financial transactions, healthcare records, anywhere audit history is legally required, or when the event history itself has business value.



**Q15: How do you handle schema evolution in an event-driven architecture?**

> Events are immutable once published тАФ you can't change published events. Strategies: (1) Additive changes only in v1: add optional fields with defaults. Consumers using schema registry (Avro + Confluent Schema Registry) with forward/backward compatibility checks will accept new fields. (2) Upcasting: when replaying old events, transform them to the new schema in-code тАФ v1 event тЖТ v2 event format. Axon Framework has built-in upcasting support. (3) Multiple event versions: publish `OrderPlacedV2` alongside `OrderPlacedV1` during migration period; consumers migrate at their own pace; deprecate v1 after all consumers upgraded. (4) Consumer-first migration: upgrade consumers to handle both old and new format BEFORE upgrading producers. Never remove/rename fields in a published event schema тАФ this is a breaking change that can silently corrupt consumers.



## 5. Advanced / Senior-Level Considerations

### Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
Senior engineers document architecture decisions. Every significant choice (why Kafka over RabbitMQ, why CQRS for this module) deserves an ADR:
```markdown
# ADR-003: Use Outbox Pattern for Event Publishing

## Status: Accepted

## Context
Order Service must publish events to Kafka reliably. Direct dual-write 
risks message loss on crash between DB commit and Kafka publish.

## Decision
Use Transactional Outbox pattern with Debezium CDC.

## Consequences
+ Guaranteed at-least-once delivery
+ No performance overhead on write path
- Requires Debezium operational overhead
- Events delayed by CDC lag (~100ms typical)
- Outbox table requires cleanup job
```

### Strangler Fig Pattern тАФ Monolith to Microservices Migration
```
Phase 1: Route traffic through Facade (no code change yet)
Client тФАтФАтЦ║ Facade тФАтФАтЦ║ Monolith (100%)

Phase 2: Implement Order Service, route orders there
Client тФАтФАтЦ║ Facade тФАтФАтЦ║ Order Service (orders)
                  тФФтФАтФАтЦ║ Monolith (everything else)

Phase 3: Gradually move more capabilities
Client тФАтФАтЦ║ API Gateway тФАтФАтЦ║ Order Service
                       тФАтФАтЦ║ Payment Service
                       тФАтФАтЦ║ Monolith (remaining)

Phase 4: Monolith is empty тАФ decommission
```

### Saga Failure Modes Senior Engineers Must Handle
- **Lost update**: two sagas modify the same aggregate тАФ use semantic locking (aggregate state machine that rejects conflicting sagas)
- **Dirty reads**: saga A reads aggregate state modified by saga B that later rolls back тАФ use countermeasures (pessimistic view, reread value before committing)
- **Non-repeatable reads**: saga reads aggregate twice, gets different values тАФ design sagas to read once and store locally

### Service Mesh vs Application-Level Resilience
- Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd): handles retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, observability at the infrastructure level тАФ language agnostic
- Application-level (Resilience4j): business-aware fallbacks, retry with business logic, custom circuit breaker conditions
- Senior recommendation: use both тАФ service mesh for infrastructure concerns, application-level for business-aware resilience

```
тФВ Bulkhead             тФВ Isolate resource pools per dependency            тФВ

тФЬтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФ╝тФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФд

KEY DECISION HEURISTICS:
тФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБ
  Monolith first тЖТ split when you have PROVEN pain
  Domain model first тЖТ let architecture emerge from domain
  Async by default тЖТ sync only when real-time response required
  Idempotent by default тЖТ the network WILL deliver duplicates
  Eventual consistency by default тЖТ strong consistency costs availability
  Test domain in isolation тЖТ if you can't, your architecture is wrong
тФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБтФБ
```


# Java Design Patterns тАФ Senior Developer Study Notes



## Topic Overview

Design patterns are **reusable solutions to commonly occurring problems** in software design. They are not finished code but templates/blueprints. Introduced formally by the "Gang of Four" (GoF) in *Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software* (1994).

**Why they matter:**
- Provide a shared vocabulary among developers
- Reduce coupling, increase cohesion
- Enable maintainability, testability, and scalability
- Fundamental to frameworks like Spring, Hibernate, and Java EE

**Real-world applications:**
- `java.util.Calendar.getInstance()` тЖТ Factory
- `java.lang.Runtime.getRuntime()` тЖТ Singleton
- `java.io.InputStream` wrappers тЖТ Decorator
- Spring's `ApplicationContext` тЖТ Factory + Singleton
- Spring AOP тЖТ Proxy
- `javax.swing` event listeners тЖТ Observer



## Core Concepts

- **Encapsulate what varies** тАФ isolate the parts that change
- **Program to interfaces, not implementations**
- **Favor composition over inheritance**
- **Depend on abstractions, not concretions** (DIP)
- Patterns fall into 3 categories: **Creational**, **Structural**, **Behavioral**



## 1. CREATIONAL PATTERNS

> *Concerned with object creation mechanisms.*



### 1.1 Singleton

**Definition:** Ensures a class has only **one instance** and provides a global access point.

**Use cases:** Configuration manager, thread pools, logging, cache, connection pools.

#### Key Details & Trade-offs:
- Must handle **thread safety**
- Lazy vs. Eager initialization
- Breaks with reflection, serialization, cloning тАФ must guard against all three
- Hard to unit test (global state) тАФ use dependency injection to inject the singleton instead of accessing it statically
- Anti-pattern in many modern architectures (prefer DI containers)

#### Implementations:

**тЭМ Naive (not thread-safe):**
```java
public class Singleton {
    private static Singleton instance;
    private Singleton() {}
    public static Singleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton(); // race condition here
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
```

**тЬЕ Double-Checked Locking (thread-safe, lazy):**
```java
public class Singleton {
    private static volatile Singleton instance; // volatile is critical
    private Singleton() {}

    public static Singleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {                    // first check (no lock)
            synchronized (Singleton.class) {
                if (instance == null) {            // second check (with lock)
                    instance = new Singleton();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }
}
// volatile prevents instruction reordering during object construction
```

**тЬЕ Bill Pugh (best practice тАФ lazy, thread-safe, no sync overhead):**
```java
public class Singleton {
    private Singleton() {}

    private static class SingletonHolder {
        private static final Singleton INSTANCE = new Singleton();
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {
        return SingletonHolder.INSTANCE;
        // JVM class loading guarantees thread safety
    }
}
```

**тЬЕ Enum Singleton (serialization-safe):**
```java
public enum Singleton {
    INSTANCE;
    public void doSomething() { ... }
}
// Enum prevents multiple instantiation even with reflection/serialization
```

**Guard against serialization break:**
```java
protected Object readResolve() {
    return getInstance(); // return the existing instance
}
```

#### Common Pitfalls:
- Forgetting `volatile` in DCL тЖТ broken on multi-core due to instruction reordering
- Not handling reflection attacks (`throw exception in constructor if instance exists`)
- Overusing Singleton тАФ creates hidden global state



### 1.2 Factory Pattern

**Two variants:** Factory Method and Abstract Factory.

#### Factory Method
**Definition:** Defines an interface for creating an object, but lets **subclasses decide** which class to instantiate.

```java
// Product
public interface Notification {
    void send(String message);
}

// Concrete Products
public class EmailNotification implements Notification {
    public void send(String message) { System.out.println("Email: " + message); }
}
public class SMSNotification implements Notification {
    public void send(String message) { System.out.println("SMS: " + message); }
}

// Creator (Factory Method)
public abstract class NotificationFactory {
    public abstract Notification createNotification(); // factory method

    public void notify(String message) {
        Notification n = createNotification();
        n.send(message);
    }
}

// Concrete Creators
public class EmailFactory extends NotificationFactory {
    public Notification createNotification() { return new EmailNotification(); }
}
public class SMSFactory extends NotificationFactory {
    public Notification createNotification() { return new SMSNotification(); }
}
```

#### Abstract Factory
